The students lived on campus, and many of them ate in dining halls that used trays. So we wanted to see if we could trigger healthy eating behavior by using the dining room tray to remind students of the slogan.

我们的学生并不喜欢托盘上的标语。他们觉得它“老套”，而且认为它的吸引力不到更通用的“健康生活”标语的一半。此外，当被问及该标语是否会影响他们自身的水果和蔬菜摄入量时，看过托盘标语的学生更有可能回答“不会”。

Our students didn’t care for the tray slogan. They called it “corny” and rated it as less than half as attractive as the more generic “live healthy” slogan. Further, when asked whether the slogan would influence their own fruit and vegetable consumption, the students who had been shown the “tray” slogan were significantly more likely to say no.

但就实际行为而言，效果却十分显著。那些看到更笼统的“健康生活”标语的学生并没有改变饮食习惯。但那些看到“托盘”标语并在食堂使用托盘的学生，他们的行为发生了显著变化。托盘让他们想起标语，结果他们多吃了25%的水果和蔬菜。这个触发点奏效了。

But when it came to actual behavior, the effects were striking. Students who had been shown the more generic “live healthy” slogan didn’t change their eating habits. But students who had seen the “tray” slogan and used trays in their cafeterias markedly changed their behavior. The trays reminded them of the slogan and they ate 25 percent more fruits and vegetables as a result. The trigger worked.

我们对结果感到非常兴奋。让大学生做任何事——更别提多吃水果蔬菜了——都是一项了不起的成就。

We were pretty excited by the results. Getting college students to do anything—let alone eat more fruits and vegetables—is an impressive feat.

但当我们的一位同事听说这项研究后，他想知道触发因素是否会影响更重要的行为：投票。

But when a colleague of ours heard about the study he wondered whether triggers would impact an even more consequential behavior: voting.

—————

—————

上次选举你在哪里投票的？

Where did you cast your ballot in the last election?

大多数人会用所在城市或州的名字来回答这个问题。埃文斯顿。伯明翰。佛罗里达。内华达。如果被要求进一步说明，他们可能会补充说“在我办公室附近”或“超市对面”。很少有人会说得更具体。他们为什么要说得更具体呢？虽然地理位置显然会影响投票——东海岸倾向于民主党，而南部则倾向于共和党——但很少有人会认为自己投票的具体地点很重要。

Most people will answer this question with the name of their city or state. Evanston. Birmingham. Florida. Nevada. If asked to clarify, they might add “near my office” or “across from the supermarket.” Few will be more specific. And why should they be? Although geography clearly matters in voting—the East Coast leans Democratic while the South skews Republican—few people would think that the exact venue in which they vote matters.

但事实的确如此。

But it does.

政治学家通常假设投票是基于理性且稳定的偏好：人们拥有核心信念，并在决定如何投票时权衡利弊。如果我们关心环境，我们会投票给承诺保护自然资源的候选人。如果我们关注医疗保健，我们会支持旨在降低医疗成本、让更多人受益的举措。在这个经过深思熟虑的认知模型中……投票行为，人们碰巧在哪种类型的建筑物投票，不应该影响他们的行为。

Political scientists usually assume that voting is based on rational and stable preferences: people possess core beliefs and weigh costs and benefits when deciding how to vote. If we care about the environment, we vote for candidates who promise to protect natural resources. If we’re concerned about health care, we support initiatives to make it more affordable and available to greater numbers of people. In this calculating, cognitive model of voting behavior, the particular kind of building people happen to cast their ballot in shouldn’t affect behavior.

但根据我们对触发因素的了解，我们对此并不确定。在美国，大多数选民都被分配到特定的投票站投票。这些投票站通常是公共建筑，例如消防站、法院或学校，但也可能是教堂、私人办公楼或其他场所。

But in light of what we were learning about triggers, we weren’t so sure. Most people in the United States are assigned to vote at a particular polling location. They are typically public buildings—firehouses, courthouses, or schools—but can also be churches, private office buildings, or other venues.

不同的地点包含不同的触发因素。教堂里到处都是宗教图像，这可能会让人想起教会教义。学校里到处都是储物柜、课桌和黑板，这可能会让人想起孩子或早期的学习经历。一旦这些想法被触发，他们的行为就可能发生改变。

Different locations contain different triggers. Churches are filled with religious imagery, which might remind people of church doctrine. Schools are filled with lockers, desks, and chalkboards, which might remind people of children or early educational experiences. And once these thoughts are triggered, they might change behavior.

在教堂投票是否会导致人们对堕胎或同性婚姻产生更负面的看法？在学校投票是否会导致人们支持教育经费？

Could voting in a church lead people to think more negatively about abortion or gay marriage? Could voting in a school lead people to support education funding?

为了验证这个想法，我和马克·梅雷迪思、克里斯蒂安·惠勒从每个投票站获取了数据。这是亚利桑那州2000年大选的情况。我们根据每个投票站的名称和地址来确定它是教堂、学校还是其他类型的建筑物。40%的选民被安排在教堂投票，26%在学校，10%在社区中心，其余的则被安排在公寓楼、高尔夫球场甚至房车营地等各种场所投票。

To test this idea, Marc Meredith, Christian Wheeler, and I acquired data from each polling place in Arizona’s 2000 general election. We used the name and address of each polling location to determine whether it was a church, a school, or some other type of building. Forty percent of people were assigned to vote in churches, 26 percent in schools, 10 percent in community centers, and the rest in a mix of apartment buildings, golf courses, and even RV parks.

然后，我们研究了人们在不同类型的投票站的投票情况是否存在差异。我们重点关注了一项将销售税从5.0%提高到5.6%以支持公立学校的提案。这项提案引发了激烈的辩论，双方都有充分的理由。大多数人支持教育，但很少有人乐意缴纳更多税款。这是一个艰难的决定。

Then we examined whether people voted differently at different types of polling places. In particular, we focused on a ballot initiative that proposed raising the sales tax from 5.0 percent to 5.6 percent to support public schools. This initiative had been hotly debated, with good arguments on both sides. Most people support education but few people enjoy paying more taxes. It was a tough decision.

如果投票地点无关紧要，那么在学校和其他投票站，支持该倡议的百分比应该相同。

If where people voted didn’t matter, then the percent supporting the initiative should be the same at schools and other polling locations.

但事实并非如此。当投票地点设在学校时，支持学校拨款提案的人数比预期多出一万多人。投票地点对投票行为产生了显著影响。

But it wasn’t. More than ten thousand more people voted in favor of the school funding initiative when the polling place was a school. Polling location had a dramatic impact on voting behavior.

该提案获得通过。

And the initiative passed.

即使我们控制了政治倾向和人口统计特征等区域差异等因素，这种差异依然存在。为了进一步验证我们的发现，我们甚至比较了两组相似的选民：一组是住在学校附近并被安排在学校投票的选民，另一组是住在学校附近但被安排在其他类型投票站（例如消防站）投票的选民。结果显示，在学校投票的选民中，支持增加学校经费的比例显著更高。他们在学校投票这一事实激发了他们更倾向于支持学校的行为。

This difference persisted even after we controlled for things like regional differences in political preferences and demographics. We even compared two similar groups of voters to double-check our findings. People who lived near schools and were assigned to vote at one versus people who lived near schools but were assigned to vote at a different type of polling place (such as a firehouse). A significantly higher percentage of the people who voted in schools were in favor of increasing funding for schools. The fact that they were in a school when they voted triggered more school-friendly behavior.

在全州选举中，一万张选票的差距看似微不足道，但却足以左右一场势均力敌的选举结果。2000年总统大选中，乔治·布什和阿尔·戈尔之间的票数差距不到一千张。如果一千张选票足以改变选举结果，那么一万张选票当然也足以做到。关键在于触发因素。

A ten-thousand-vote difference in a statewide election might not seem like much. But it was more than enough to shift a close election. In the 2000 presidential election the difference between George Bush and Al Gore came down to less than 1,000 votes. If 1,000 votes is enough to shift an election, 10,000 certainly could. Triggers matter.

那么，触发因素是如何帮助决定产品和理念能否流行起来的呢？

So how do triggers help determine whether products and ideas catch on?

在……星期五搜索“星期五”

SEARCHING FOR “FRIDAY” ON . . . FRIDAY

2011年，丽贝卡·布莱克完成了一项意义非凡的成就。这位13岁的女孩发行了一首被许多音乐评论家称为史上最烂的歌曲。

In 2011, Rebecca Black accomplished a momentous achievement. The thirteen-year-old released what many music critics dubbed the worst song ever.

丽贝卡出生于1997年，发行第一首完整歌曲时还只是个孩子。但这远非她首次涉足音乐领域。她曾参加过各种演出试镜，上过音乐夏令营，并且公开演唱多年。在听一位同学讲述自己如何寻求外界帮助发展音乐事业后，丽贝卡的父母支付了四千美元给洛杉矶的唱片公司 ARK Music Factory，请他们为女儿创作一首歌。

Born in 1997, Rebecca was just a kid when she released her first full-length song. But this was far from her first foray into music. She had auditioned for shows, had attended music summer camp, and had sung publicly for a number of years. After hearing from a classmate who had turned to outside help for her music career, Rebecca’s parents paid four thousand dollars to ARK Music Factory, a Los Angeles label, to write a song for their daughter to sing.

结果嘛，简直糟透了。这首名为《星期五》的歌，旋律矫揉造作，歌词充斥着青少年生活和周末的快乐。歌曲开头是她早上起床准备去上学的情景：

The result was decidedly, well, awful. Entitled “Friday,” the tune was a whiny, overproduced number about teenage life and the joys of the weekend. The song starts with her getting up in the morning and getting ready to go to school:

早上七点，醒来

Seven a.m., waking up in the morning

得精神抖擞，得下楼去。

Gotta be fresh, gotta go downstairs

我得吃我的碗，我得吃麦片

Gotta have my bowl, gotta have cereal

然后她匆匆赶到公交车站，看到朋友们开车经过，犹豫着是坐前排还是后排。最后，在经历了所有这些艰难的抉择之后，她唱起了副歌，那是一首表达她对即将到来的两天自由时光的兴奋之歌：

Then she hustles down to the bus stop, sees her friends drive by, and ponders whether to sit in the front seat or the back. Finally, after all those tough decisions, she hits the chorus, an ode to her excitement about the impending two days of freedom:

今天是星期五，星期五

It’s Friday, Friday

周五得好好放松一下。

Gotta get down on Friday

大家都盼着周末快点到来。

Everybody’s lookin’ forward to the weekend, weekend.

总而言之，这首曲子听起来更像是某个特别空洞的青少年脑海中随机闪过的念头的独白，而不是一首真正的歌曲。

All in all, the piece sounds more like a monologue of the random thoughts going through an especially vacant teenager’s head than a real song.

然而，这首歌却是 2011 年最热门的病毒式传播视频之一。它在 YouTube 上的观看次数超过 3 亿次，还有数百万人通过其他渠道收听了这首歌。

Yet this song was one of the most viral videos of 2011. It was viewed more than 300 million times on YouTube, and many millions more listened to it over other channels.

为什么？这首歌很糟糕，但很多歌都很糟糕。那么，是什么让这首歌成功了呢？

Why? The song was terrible, but lots of songs are terrible. So what made this one a success?

看看2011年3月，也就是这首歌刚发行不久后，YouTube上“Rebecca Black”的每日搜索量。看看你是否发现了什么规律。

Take a look at the number of daily searches for “Rebecca Black” on YouTube in March 2011, soon after the song was first released. See if you notice a pattern.

2011年3月在YouTube上搜索“Rebecca Black”

Searches for “Rebecca Black” on YouTube March 2011

注意到每周都会出现一次高峰了吗？仔细观察你会发现，高峰每周都出现在同一天。3月18日出现了一次，七天后的3月25日出现了一次，又过了七天，4月1日出现了一次。

Notice the spike once every week? Look closer and you’ll see that the spike happens on the same day every week. There was one on March 18, seven days later on March 25, and seven days later, on April 1.

具体是星期几？你猜对了，星期五——就像丽贝卡·布莱克的那首歌的名字一样。

The particular day of the week? You guessed it. Friday—just like the name of Rebecca Black’s song.

所以，虽然这首歌每天都一样糟糕，但每个星期五它都会受到强烈的推动，从而走向成功。

So while the song was equally bad every day of the week, each Friday it received a strong trigger that contributed to its success.

被触发说话

TRIGGERED TO TALK

正如“社交货币”章节中所讨论的，口碑传播的部分动机源于人们想要在他人眼中留下好印象。提及一些巧妙或有趣的事情会让人显得自己既聪明又风趣。但这并非促使我们分享的唯一因素。

As discussed in the Social Currency chapter, some word of mouth is motivated by peoples’ desire to look good to others. Mentioning clever or entertaining things makes people seem clever and entertaining. But that isn’t the only factor that drives us to share.

大多数对话都可以被称作闲聊。我们在孩子踢足球时和家长们聊天，或者在休息室里和同事们寒暄几句。这些对话与其说是为了展现自己的魅力而寻找有趣的话题，不如说是为了填补谈话的空白。我们不想沉默不语，所以就随便说点什么。什么都行。我们的目标未必是证明自己有趣、幽默或聪明，只是想说点什么让对话继续下去。任何能证明自己不至于太不善言辞的事情都行。

Most conversations can be described as small talk. We chat with parents at our kids’ soccer games or schmooze with coworkers in the break room. These conversations are less about finding interesting things to say to make us look good than they are about filling conversational space. We don’t want to sit there silently, so we talk about something. Anything. Our goal isn’t necessarily to prove that we are interesting, funny, or intelligent. We just want to say something to keep the conversation going. Anything to prove that we’re not terrible conversationalists.

那我们聊些什么呢？从你最先想到的话题开始总是没错的。如果某件事很容易找到相关信息，那它通常都与当前的情况相关。你读过关于新桥建设的新闻吗？你觉得昨晚的比赛怎么样？

So what do we talk about? Whatever is top of mind is a good place to start. If something is accessible, it’s usually relevant to the situation at hand. Did you read about the new bridge construction? What did you think about the game last night?

我们谈论这些话题是因为它们就发生在我们周围的环境中。我们开车进来的时候看到了推土机，所以建筑工地成了我们关注的焦点。我们碰巧遇到一个喜欢运动的朋友，所以我们开始讨论那场重要的比赛。触发因素能促进口碑传播。

We talk about these topics because they are going on in the surrounding environment. We saw the bulldozers on our drive in, so construction is on our mind. We bump into a friend who likes sports, so we think about the big game. Triggers boost word of mouth.

回到 BzzAgent 数据，触发器帮助我们解答了为什么有些产品更容易被提及。那些被频繁触发的产品获得了15%的口碑传播。即使是像保鲜袋和润肤霜这样看似普通的产品，也因为人们经常想起它们而引发了大量讨论。使用润肤霜的人通常每天至少涂抹一次。人们也经常在饭后使用保鲜袋来打包剩菜剩饭。这些日常行为让这些产品更容易被人们记住，从而也让它们更容易被人们谈论。

Returning to the BzzAgent data, triggers helped us answer why some products get talked about more. More frequently triggered products got 15 percent more word of mouth. Even mundane products like Ziploc bags and moisturizer received lots of buzz because people were triggered to think about them so frequently. People who use moisturizer often apply it at least once a day. People often use Ziploc bags after meals to wrap up leftovers. These everyday activities make those products more top of mind and, as a result, lead them to be talked about more.

此外，触发式产品不仅能更快地获得口碑传播，还能持续获得口碑传播。

Furthermore, not only did triggered products get more immediate word of mouth, they also got more word of mouth on an ongoing basis.

这样看来，Ziploc 保鲜袋与我想要去的地方完全相反。老师打扮成海盗的样子。海盗的故事很有趣，但转瞬即逝。保鲜袋或许很无聊，但它们却每周都被提及，因为它们经常被触发。触发因素起到提醒的作用，不仅能让人们谈论它们，还能让它们持续讨论。“最先想到”意味着“脱口而出”。

In this way, Ziploc bags are the antithesis of me going to teach dressed like a pirate. The pirate story is interesting, but it’s here today, gone tomorrow. Ziploc bags may be boring, but they get mentioned week in and week out because they are frequently triggered. By acting as reminders, triggers not only get people talking, they keep them talking. Top of mind means tip of tongue.

—————

—————

所以，与其仅仅追求朗朗上口的宣传语，不如考虑其语境。想想目标受众的日常环境是否会影响到这条信息。我们往往倾向于追求趣味性。无论是竞选班长还是推销汽水，我们都认为朗朗上口或巧妙的口号能够帮助我们达成目标。

So rather than just going for a catchy message, consider the context. Think about whether the message will be triggered by the everyday environments of the target audience. Going for interesting is our default tendency. Whether running for class president or selling soda, we think that catchy or clever slogans will get us where we need to go.

但正如我们在水果蔬菜研究中看到的那样，强有力的触发因素远比朗朗上口的口号更有效。即使大学生们讨厌那句口号，但当食堂餐盘上的提示语提醒他们水果蔬菜的健康益处时，他们吃的水果蔬菜也更多了。仅仅接触一句巧妙的口号并不会改变他们的行为。

But as we saw in our fruits and vegetables study, a strong trigger can be much more effective than a catchy slogan. Even though they hated the slogan, college students ate more fruits and vegetables when cafeteria trays triggered reminders of the health benefits. Just being exposed to a clever slogan didn’t change behavior at all.

几年前，汽车保险公司GEICO投放广告，声称转投GEICO非常简单，连原始人都能做到。从创意角度来看，这些广告非常成功。它们既幽默风趣，又准确地传达了转投GEICO非常便捷这一信息。

A few years ago, auto insurance company GEICO ran ads that said switching to GEICO was so simple that even a caveman could do it. On the cleverness dimension the ads were great. They were funny and made the point that switching to GEICO was easy.

但从触发点来看，这些广告失败了。我们在日常生活中很少见到穴居人，所以不太可能经常想起这则广告，也就更难引起人们的讨论。

But judged on triggers, the ads fail. We don’t see many cavemen in our daily lives, so the ad is unlikely to come to mind often, making it less likely to be talked about.

这与百威啤酒的“Wassup?”广告活动形成鲜明对比。两个男人一边喝着百威啤酒，一边打电话看电视上的篮球比赛。这时，第三个朋友来了，他喊道：“Wassup？” 前面两个人中的一个喊道：“怎么了？”对方回应道。这开启了越来越多爱喝百威啤酒的朋友之间无休止的“怎么了”聊天循环。

Contrast that with the Budweiser beer “Wassup?” campaign. Two guys are talking on the phone while drinking Budweiser and watching a basketball game on television. A third friend arrives. He yells, “Wassup?” One of the first two guys yells “Wassup?” back. This kicks off an endless cycle of wassups between a growing number of Budweiser-drinking buddies.

不，这则广告算不上最精妙。但它却风靡全球。而它的成功至少部分归功于一些触发因素。百威啤酒考虑到了当时的社会背景。“Wassup”在当时的年轻男性中很流行。仅仅是和朋友打招呼，就能让百威啤酒的目标受众联想到百威啤酒。

No, it wasn’t the cleverest of commercials. But it became a global phenomenon. And at least part of its success was due to triggers. Budweiser considered the context. “Wassup” was a popular greeting among young men at the time. Just greeting friends triggered thoughts of Budweiser in Budweiser’s prime demographic.

预期行为发生延迟的时间越长，触发因素就越重要。市场调研通常关注消费者对广告信息或活动的即时反应。这在消费者有机会立即购买产品的情况下可能很有价值。但在大多数情况下，人们某天听到广告，几天或几周后才去商店。如果他们没有被触发去思考，那么当他们在商店时，又如何能想起这条广告呢？

The more the desired behavior happens after a delay, the more important being triggered becomes. Market research often focuses on consumers’ immediate reaction to an advertising message or campaign. That might be valuable in situations where the consumer is immediately offered a chance to buy the product. But in most cases, people hear an ad one day and then go to the store days or weeks later. If they’re not triggered to think about it, how will they remember that ad when they’re at the store?

公共卫生宣传活动也应考虑其具体情境。以鼓励大学生理性饮酒的宣传信息为例。虽然这些信息可能非常巧妙且令人信服，但它们张贴在校园健康中心，远离兄弟会会所或其他学生实际饮酒的场所。因此，即使学生在阅读时认同这些信息，但除非他们在实际饮酒时被提醒思考这些信息，否则这些信息不太可能改变他们的行为。

Public health campaigns would also benefit from considering the context. Take messages that encourage college students to drink responsibly. While the messages might be really clever and convincing, they’re posted at the campus health center, far away from the frat houses or other places where students actually drink. So while students may agree with the message when they read it, unless they are triggered to think about it when they are actually drinking, the message is unlikely to change behavior.

触发因素甚至揭示了负面口碑何时会产生积极影响。经济学家艾伦·索伦森、斯科特·拉斯穆森和我分析了数百篇《纽约时报》书评，以了解正面和负面评论如何影响图书销量。

Triggers even shed light on when negative word of mouth has positive effects. Economist Alan Sorensen, Scott Rasmussen, and I analyzed hundreds of New York Times book reviews to see how positive and negative reviews affected book sales.

与“任何宣传都是好宣传”的观点相反，负面评论确实会损害某些书籍的销量。但对于新作者的书籍来说，情况则有所不同。对于一些名不见经传的作者来说，负面评论反而使销量增长了45%。例如，一本名为《凶猛之人》（Fierce People）的书就收到了一篇糟糕的评论。《泰晤士报》指出，作者“眼光不够敏锐”，并抱怨说“文风转变过于突兀，这种不协调感几乎令人反感”。然而，评论发表后，这本书的销量却翻了四倍多。

In contrast to the notion that any publicity is good publicity, negative reviews hurt sales for some books. But for books by new or relatively unknown authors, negative reviews increased sales by 45 percent. A book called Fierce People, for example, got a terrible review. The Times noted that the author “does not have a particularly sharp eye” and complained that “the change in tone is so abrupt that the dissonance it creates is almost distasteful.” Yet sales more than quadrupled after the review.

触发因素解释了其中的原因。即使是差评或负面口碑，如果能够告知或提醒人们某个产品或理念的存在，也能促进销售。例如，一款售价 60 美元的托斯卡纳红葡萄酒，在被一家知名葡萄酒网站形容为“散发着臭袜子味”后，销量反而增长了 5%。这也是 Shake Weight（一款曾被媒体和消费者广泛嘲讽的振动哑铃）最终实现 5000 万美元销售额的原因之一。即使是负面关注，如果能够让产品和理念深入人心，也能发挥积极作用。

Triggers explain why. Even a bad review or negative word of mouth can increase sales if it informs or reminds people that the product or idea exists. That’s why a sixty-dollar Tuscan red wine saw sales rise by 5 percent after a prominent wine website described it as “redolent of stinky socks.” It’s also one reason why the Shake Weight, a vibrating dumbbell that was widely ridiculed by the media and consumers, went on to do $50 million in sales. Even negative attention can be useful if it makes products and ideas top of mind.

Kit Kat 和咖啡：营造良好的生活环境

KIT KAT AND COFFEE: GROWING THE HABITAT

Kit Kat 就是一款巧妙运用触发效应的产品。

One product that used triggers brilliantly is Kit Kat.

“饶了我吧，饶了我吧，给我一块奇巧巧克力吧！” 1986年在美国推出。Kit Kat 的广告歌是有史以来最具标志性的广告歌曲之一。你只要唱给几乎所有25岁以上的人听，他们就能接上剩下的歌词。研究人员甚至认为……这首歌是史上十大“洗脑神曲”之一——旋律一旦响起就会一直萦绕在你的脑海里。甚至比“YMCA”更令人难忘（乡村乐队，你们输了）。

“Give me a break, give me a break, break me off a piece of that Kit Kat bar!” Introduced in the United States in 1986, the Kit Kat tune is one of the most iconic jingles ever made. Sing the first couple of words to almost anyone over twenty-five and the person can finish the line. Researchers even deemed it one of the top ten “earworms”—a melody that gets stuck in your head—of all time. Even more memorable than “YMCA” (take that, Village People).

但在2007年，科琳·乔拉克（Colleen Chorak）受命重振奇巧（Kit Kat）品牌。自广告歌首次推出以来的二十多年里，该品牌已风光不再。好时公司生产的产品种类繁多，从里斯花生酱巧克力豆（Reese's Pieces）、好时之吻（Hershey's Kisses）到杏仁巧克力，应有尽有。Joy、Twizzlers 和 Jolly Ranchers。如此众多的产品，一个品牌很容易被埋没。Kit Kat 就遭遇了这样的困境。好时公司在“给我个机会”广告宣传活动之后，一直未能找到新的突破口。销售额每年下降约 5%，品牌规模也大幅缩水。人们仍然喜爱这款产品，但消费者的兴趣却大不如前。

But in 2007, Colleen Chorak was tasked with reviving the Kit Kat brand. In the twenty-plus years since the jingle was first introduced, the brand had run out of gas. Hershey produces everything from Reese’s Pieces and Hershey’s Kisses to Almond Joy, Twizzlers, and Jolly Ranchers. With this huge slate of different items, it’s not surprising that a brand can get lost. And that is exactly what had happened with Kit Kat. Hershey had floundered with replacing the “give me a break” campaign. Sales were declining around 5 percent a year, and the brand had contracted considerably. People still loved the product, but consumer interest was way down.

科琳需要找到一种方法，让消费者重新关注这个品牌，让奇巧巧克力再次成为消费者心中的首选。鉴于多年来尝试的新方向均告失败，高层管理人员不愿花钱让奇巧重返电视荧屏。任何财政支持充其量也只能是微薄的。

Colleen needed a way to get consumers to start thinking about the brand again. To make Kit Kat more top of mind. And given the years of failed new directions, upper management was unwilling to spend the money to put the brand back on TV. Any financial support would be modest at best.

于是她做了一些调查。科琳研究了人们实际食用奇巧巧克力的时间。她发现了两点：消费者经常在休息时吃奇巧巧克力，而且许多人会搭配热饮一起食用。

So she did some research. Colleen looked at when people actually consumed Kit Kats. She found two things: consumers often ate Kit Kats to take a break, and many consumed it in coordination with a hot beverage.

她想到了一个主意。

She had an idea.

KitKat 巧克力和咖啡。

Kit Kat and coffee.

科琳在短短几个月内就完成了整个营销活动。广告中，Kit Kat 巧克力棒被誉为“休息时的最佳伴侣”，广告画面中，巧克力棒摆放在吧台上，旁边放着一杯咖啡；或者有人拿起咖啡，要了一块 Kit Kat。Kit Kat 和咖啡，咖啡和 Kit Kat。广告反复将两者搭配在一起。

Colleen pulled the campaign together in a matter of months. Described as “a break’s best friend,” the radio spots featured the candy bar sitting on a counter next to a cup of coffee, or someone grabbing coffee and asking for a Kit Kat. Kit Kat and coffee. Coffee and Kit Kat. The spots repeatedly paired the two together.

这次活动非常成功。

The campaign was a hit.

到年底，销售额增长了8%。12个月后，销售额增长了三分之一。Kit Kat和咖啡的结合让Kit Kat重回大众视野。这个当时价值3亿美元的品牌，如今已增长至5亿美元。

By the end of the year it had lifted sales by 8 percent. After twelve months, sales were up by a third. Kit Kat and coffee put Kit Kat back on the map. The then-$300 million brand has since grown to $500 million.

这次活动的成功有很多因素。“Kit Kat 和咖啡”的押韵很好听，而且也契合了休息一下的理念。吃一块奇巧巧克力很符合咖啡休息的理念。但我还想再补充一个理由。

Many things contributed to the campaign’s success. “Kit Kat and coffee” has a nice alliteration, and the idea of taking a break to have a Kit Kat fits well with the existing notion of a coffee break. But I’d like to add one more reason to the list.

触发点。“Kit Kat 和哈密瓜”同样押韵，霹雳舞也符合“打破常规”的概念。但咖啡尤其适合与品牌联系起来，因为它是环境中常见的刺激物。很多人都喝咖啡，很多人一天喝好几次。因此，通过将 Kit Kat 与咖啡联系起来，Colleen 创造了一个频繁触发点，让人们更容易想起这个品牌。

Triggers. “Kit Kat and cantaloupe” is equally alliterative, and break dancing would also have fitted with the break concept. But coffee is a particularly good thing to link the brand to because it is a frequent stimulus in the environment. A huge number of people drink coffee. Many drink it a number of times throughout the day. And so by linking Kit Kat to coffee, Colleen created a frequent trigger to remind people of the brand.

—————

—————

生物学家经常谈到动植物拥有栖息地，即包含维持生物体生命所需所有要素的自然环境。鸭子需要水和草来觅食。鹿在有开阔草地可供放牧的地区繁衍生息。

Biologists often talk about plants and animals as having habitats, natural environments that contain all necessary elements for sustaining an organism’s life. Ducks need water and grasses to eat. Deer thrive in areas that contain open spaces for grazing.

产品和思想也有其栖息地，或者说，有一系列触发因素会促使人们思考这些思想。

Products and ideas also have habitats, or sets of triggers that cause people to think about them.

以热狗为例。烧烤、夏季、棒球比赛，甚至腊肠犬（腊肠犬）都只是构成热狗生存环境的几个诱因。

Take hot dogs. Barbecues, summertime, baseball games, and even wiener dogs (dachshunds) are just a few of the triggers that make up the habitat for hot dogs.

相比之下，埃塞俄比亚美食的分布情况如何呢？大多数人想到埃塞俄比亚美食时，首先想到的是什么？埃塞俄比亚美食固然美味，但它的分布范围却远不如其他食物广泛。

Compare that with the habitat for Ethiopian food. What triggers most people to think of Ethiopian food? Ethiopian food is certainly delicious, but its habitat is not as prevalent.

大多数产品或理念都存在一些天然的触发因素。例如，玛氏巧克力棒和火星星球本身就有着天然的联系。玛氏公司无需刻意建立这种联系。同样，法国音乐是法国葡萄酒的天然触发因素，而一周的最后一天则是瑞贝卡·布莱克歌曲《星期五》的天然触发因素。

Most products or ideas have a number of natural triggers. Mars bars and Mars the planet are already naturally connected. The Mars company didn’t need to do anything to create that link. Likewise, French music is a natural trigger for French wine, and the last day of the workweek is a natural trigger for Rebecca Black’s song “Friday.”

但也可以通过与环境中的刺激物建立新的联系来培育一个想法的生存环境。Kit Kat 就做不到这一点。通常人们会将水果和蔬菜与咖啡联系起来，但通过反复配对，科琳·乔拉克成功地将两者联系起来。同样，我们的托盘实验也通过反复将餐桌托盘和“多吃水果蔬菜”的信息配对，建立了二者之间的联系。通过增加传递信息的环境，这些新建立的联系有助于推广这种期望的行为。

But it’s also possible to grow an idea’s habitat by creating new links to stimuli in the environment. Kit Kat wouldn’t normally be associated with coffee, but through repeated pairing, Colleen Chorak was able to link the two. Similarly, our trays experiment created a link between dining-room trays and a message to eat fruits and vegetables by repeatedly pairing the two ideas together. And by increasing the habitat for the message, these newly formed links helped the desired behavior catch on.

考虑我们与 BzzAgent 和 Boston Market 合作开展了一项实验。这家快餐休闲餐厅以家常风味美食（烤鸡和土豆泥）而闻名，主要被视为午餐场所。管理层希望提升餐厅的知名度。我们认为可以通过改善 Boston Market 的品牌形象来提供帮助。

Consider an experiment we conducted with BzzAgent and Boston Market. This fast-casual restaurant is best known for home-style comfort food (rotisserie chicken and mashed potatoes) and was primarily viewed as a lunch place. Management wanted to generate more buzz. We thought we could help by growing Boston Market’s habitat.

在为期六周的推广活动中，一部分人接触到的信息反复将餐厅与晚餐联系起来，例如：“想吃什么晚餐？那就去波士顿市场吧！”另一部分人则接触到类似的广告宣传，但信息更为笼统：“想找个地方吃饭？那就去波士顿市场吧！”之后，我们统计了两组人谈论这家餐厅的频率。

During a six-week campaign, some people were exposed to messages that repeatedly paired the restaurant with dinner. “Thinking about dinner? Think about Boston Market!”. Other people received a similar advertising campaign that contained a more generic message: “Thinking about a place to eat? Think about Boston Market!” We then measured how often the respective groups talked about the restaurant.

结果令人瞩目。与普通信息相比，将波士顿市场与晚餐联系起来的信息，使口碑传播率提高了20%，尤其是在那些之前只将该品牌与午餐联系起来的人群中。扩大品牌认知度显著提升了品牌热度。

The results were dramatic. Compared to the generic message, the message that grew the habitat (by associating Boston Market with dinner) increased word of mouth by 20 percent among people who previously had associated the brand only with lunch. Growing the habitat boosted buzz.

—————

—————

竞争对手甚至可以成为一种触发因素。

Competitors can even be used as a trigger.

公共卫生组织如何才能与资金更雄厚的竞争对手（例如烟草公司）的营销实力抗衡？解决这种不平等的一种方法是转变……化弱点为优势：利用竞争对手的信息作为触发你自身信息的契机。

How can public health organizations compete against the marketing strength of better-funded rivals like cigarette companies? One way to combat this inequality is to transform a weakness into a strength: by making a rival’s message act as a trigger for your own.

例如，一项著名的反吸烟运动就曾戏仿万宝路标志性的广告，在一张万宝路牛仔与另一位牛仔交谈的图片上配文：“鲍勃，我得了肺气肿。”所以现在每当人们看到万宝路广告时，都会想到反吸烟的信息。

A famous antismoking campaign, for example, spoofed Marlboro’s iconic ads by captioning a picture of one Marlboro cowboy talking to another with the words: “Bob, I’ve got emphysema.” So now whenever people see a Marlboro ad, it triggers them to think about the antismoking message.

研究人员称这种策略为这种毒害寄生虫会偷偷地将“毒药”（你的信息）注入竞争对手的信息中，使其成为你信息的触发点。

Researchers call this strategy the poison parasite because it slyly injects “poison” (your message) into a rival’s message by making it a trigger for your own.

什么因素构成有效的触发因素？

WHAT MAKES FOR AN EFFECTIVE TRIGGER?

触发因素可以帮助产品和理念流行起来，但有些刺激因素比其他刺激因素更能起到触发作用。

Triggers can help products and ideas catch on, but some stimuli are better triggers than others.

正如我们讨论过的，一个关键因素是刺激出现的频率。热巧克力其实也很适合搭配奇巧巧克力，而且这种甜饮料甚至可能比咖啡更能衬托出巧克力的味道。但咖啡之所以更能有效地触发这种联想，是因为人们经常会想到它，也经常会看到它。更频繁地。大多数人只在冬天喝热巧克力，而咖啡则一年四季都喝。

As we discussed, one key factor is how frequently the stimulus occurs. Hot chocolate would also have fitted really well with Kit Kat, and the sweet beverage might have even complemented the chocolate bar’s flavor better than coffee. But coffee is a more effective trigger because people think about and see it much more frequently. Most people drink hot chocolate only in the winter, while coffee is consumed year-round.

同样，米凯罗啤酒在20世纪70年代开展了一项成功的营销活动，将周末与啤酒品牌联系起来（“周末就该喝米凯罗”）。然而，这并非该活动最初的口号。最初的口号是“假期就该喝米凯罗”。但事实证明，这个口号效果不佳，因为所选的刺激因素——假期——并不经常发生。安海斯-布希公司将广告语修改为“周末就该喝米凯罗啤酒”，结果大获成功。

Similarly, Michelob ran a successful campaign in the 1970s that linked weekends with the beer brand (“Weekends are made for Michelob”). However, that wasn’t the slogan when the campaign started out. Originally the slogan was “Holidays are made for Michelob.” But this proved ineffective because the chosen stimuli—holidays—don’t happen that often. So Anheuser-Busch revised the slogan to “Weekends are made for Michelob,” which was much more successful.

然而，频率也必须与关联的强度相平衡。某个线索关联的事物越多，其关联强度就越弱。这就像在装满水的纸杯底部戳个洞。如果只戳一个洞，就会有强劲的水流喷涌而出。但如果戳的洞越来越多，每个洞喷出的水流压力就会逐渐减弱。戳太多孔，每个孔都只能流出涓涓细流。

Frequency, however, must also be balanced with the strength of the link. The more things a given cue is associated with, the weaker any given association. It’s like poking a hole in the bottom of a paper cup filled with water. If you poke just one hole, a strong stream of water will gush out. But poke more holes, and the pressure of the stream from each opening lessens. Poke too many holes and you’ll get barely a trickle from each.

触发因素的运作方式也类似。例如，红色可以与很多事物联系起来：玫瑰、爱情、可口可乐、跑车等等。正因为它无处不在，所以它本身并不是这些联想的强触发因素。你可以问问不同的人，当他们想到红色时，脑海中第一个浮现的词是什么，你就会明白我的意思了。

Triggers work the same way. The color red, for example, is associated with many things: roses, love, Coca-Cola, and fast cars, to name just a few. As a result of being ubiquitous, it’s not a particularly strong trigger for any of these ideas. Ask different people to say the word that first comes to their mind when they think of red and you’ll see what I mean.

想想看，很多人一听到“花生酱”就会想到“果冻”，这就足以说明为什么更强有力、更独特的联系效果更好。将产品或理念与一个已经与许多事物联系起来的刺激物联系起来，远不如建立一种更新颖、更独特的联系来得有效。

Compare that with how many people think “jelly” when you say “peanut butter” and it will be clear why stronger, more unusual links are better. Linking a product or idea with a stimulus that is already associated with many things isn’t as effective as forging a fresher, more original link.

选择触发点时，务必选择在预期行为发生地点附近出现的触发点。不妨想想新西兰一则构思巧妙但最终效果不佳的公益广告。广告画面精美，一个肌肉发达的男人正在淋浴。背景音乐是​​一段朗朗上口的广告歌，介绍的是HeatFlow——一种新型温控系统，它能确保你始终拥有充足的热水，享受长时间的舒适淋浴。男人关掉水龙头。当他打开淋浴门时，一位迷人的女士扔给他一条毛巾。他笑了。她也笑了。他开始走出淋浴间。

It is also important to pick triggers that happen near where the desired behavior is taking place. Consider a clever but ultimately ineffective public service ad from New Zealand. A handsome, muscular man is taking a shower. In the background you hear a catchy jingle about HeatFlow, a new temperature-control system that ensures you’ll always have sufficient hot water for long, luxurious showers. The man turns off the water. When he opens the shower door, an attractive woman tosses him a towel. He smiles. She smiles. He begins to step out of the shower stall.

他突然脚下一滑，摔倒在地，头部重重地撞在瓷砖地板上。他躺在那里一动不动，手臂微微抽搐了一下。画外音低沉地响起：“防止在家中滑倒其实很简单，只需使用防滑垫即可。”

Suddenly, he slips. Falling, he cracks his head on the tile floor. As he lies there, motionless, his arm twitches slightly. A voice-over somberly intones: “Preventing slips around your home can be as easy as using a bath mat.”

哇。真是出乎意料。令人难忘。难忘到什么程度呢？每次在没有地垫的浴室洗澡时，我都会想起这件事。

Wow. Definitely surprising. Extremely memorable. So memorable, I think about it every time I take a shower in a bathroom that doesn’t have a mat on the floor.

但只有一个问题。

But there’s only one problem.

我不能在浴室里买地垫。信息本身与我想要的行为完全脱节了。除非我离开浴室，打开笔记本电脑，在网上购买地垫，否则我必须一直记住这个信息，直到我走到商店为止。

I can’t buy a bath mat in a bathroom. The message is physically removed from the desired behavior. Unless I leave the bathroom, turn on my laptop, and buy a mat online, I have to remember the message until I get to a store.

与此形成鲜明对比的是纽约市卫生局发起的反汽水运动。虽然汽水与我们一天摄入的所有食物相比，热量似乎相对较低，但饮用含糖饮料实际上会对体重增加产生显著影响。纽约市卫生局的目标不仅仅是告诉人们汽水中含有多少糖，更重要的是确保人们记住要改变自己的行为，并将这一信息传播给他人。

Contrast that with a New York City Department of Health (DOH) antisoda campaign. While soda might seem like a relatively low-calorie item compared to all the food we eat during the course of a day, drinking sugary beverages actually has a big impact on weight gain. But the DOH didn’t just want to tell people how much sugar was in soda, it wanted to make sure people would remember to change their behavior and spread the message to others.

于是，卫生部门制作了一段视频，视频中有人打开了一个看似普通的汽水罐。但当他开始把汽水倒进杯子里时，一团团白色的、块状的脂肪溢出来。那家伙拿起杯子，像喝普通汽水一样，一口气把脂肪都喝了下去——连块状的脂肪都一并喝了下去。

So the DOH made a video showing someone opening what seems like a normal soda can. But when he starts to pour it into a glass, out spills fat. Blob after blob of white, chunky fat. The guy picks the glass up and knocks the fat back just as one would a regular soda—chunks and all.

“男人喝脂肪”短片的结尾是一大块凝固的脂肪掉在餐盘上。脂肪顺着桌面流淌下来，屏幕上闪现出一条信息：“每天喝一罐汽水，一年会让你胖10磅。所以别让自己喝胖。”

The “Man Drinks Fat” clip closes with a huge congealed chunk of fat being dropped on a dinner plate. It oozes over the table as a message flashes up on the screen: “Drinking one can of soda a day can make you 10 pounds fatter a year. So don’t drink yourself fat.”

这段视频很巧妙。通过展现脂肪从罐子里倾泻而出的画面，卫生部也巧妙地利用了人们的心理触发点。与浴室地垫广告不同，这段视频在人们正想喝汽水的时候，精准地传递了信息（不要喝含糖饮料）。

The video is clever. But by showing fat pouring out of a can, the DOH also nicely leveraged triggers. Unlike the bath mat ad, its video triggered the message (don’t consume sugary drinks) at precisely the right time: when people are thinking of drinking a soda.

考虑上下文

CONSIDER THE CONTEXT

这些活动凸显了考虑背景的重要性：要思考信息或理念试图触动的人群所处的环境。不同的环境包含不同的刺激因素。亚利桑那州被沙漠环绕，而佛罗里达州则遍布棕榈树。因此，不同的触发因素在不同地区的效果也会有所不同。

These campaigns underscore how important it is to consider the context: to think about the environments of the people a message or idea is trying to trigger. Different environments contain different stimuli. Arizona is surrounded by desert. Floridians see lots of palm trees. Consequently, different triggers will be more or less effective depending on where people live.

同样，我们在引言中提到的价值一百美元的芝士牛排的效果，也取决于它推出的城市。

Similarly, the effectiveness of the hundred-dollar cheesesteak that we talked about in the introduction depends on the city where it is introduced.

无论在哪里，一百美元的三明治都相当惊人。但人们会多久想到它一次，则取决于地理位置。在费城这样盛产芝士牛排的地方，人们会经常想到它，但在其他地方（例如芝加哥），则不会。

A hundred-dollar sandwich is pretty remarkable, wherever you are. But how frequently people will be triggered to think about it depends on geography. In places where people eat lots of cheesesteaks (Philadelphia), people would be triggered often, but in other places (such as Chicago) not so much.

即使在同一城市或地理区域内，人们也会因一天中的时间或一年中的不同季节而受到不同的触发因素影响。例如，我们进行的一项万圣节前后的研究发现，人们更容易想到……万圣节前一天，人们对橙色相关产品（例如橙味汽水或瑞氏花生酱巧克力豆）的关注度比一周后更高。万圣节前，环境中所有橙色的刺激物（南瓜和橙色装饰品）都会引发人们对橙色产品的联想。但节日一过，这些刺激物就消失了，人们对橙色产品的想法也随之消失。人们转而开始考虑圣诞节或其他下一个节日。

Even within a given city or geographic region, people experience different triggers based on the time of day or year. One study we conducted around Halloween, for example, found that people were much more likely to think about products associated with the color orange (such as orange soda or Reese’s Pieces) the day before Halloween than a week later. Before Halloween, all the orange stimuli in the environment (pumpkins and orange displays) triggered thoughts of orange products. But as soon as the holiday was over, those triggers disappeared, and so did thoughts of orange products. People moved on to thinking about Christmas or whatever holiday came next.

所以，比如说，当你思考如何才能记得带上可重复使用的购物袋去超市时，想想什么才能在恰当的时机提醒你。使用可重复使用的购物袋就像多吃蔬菜一样。我们知道应该这样做，甚至也想这样做（我们大多数人都买了购物袋）。但到了真正需要行动的时候，我们却往往忘记了。

So when thinking about, say, how to remember to take your reusable grocery bags to the grocery store, think about what will trigger you at exactly the right time. Using reusable grocery bags is like eating more vegetables. We know we should do it. We even want to do it (most of us have bought the bags). But when it comes time to take action, we forget.

就在我们把车开进超市停车场的时候，我们突然想起来：哎呀，我忘了带可重复使用的购物袋！但那时已经太晚了。我们已经到了超市，而购物袋还在家里的壁橱里。

Then, right as we pull into the grocery store parking lot, we remember. Argh, I forgot the reusable grocery bags! But by then it’s too late. We’re at the store and the grocery bags are at home in the closet.

我们一到超市就想到可重复使用的购物袋，这绝非偶然。超市购物是促使我们想到购物袋的重要因素。但不幸的是，这个时机并不合适。就像之前的浴室地垫公益广告一样，我们想到了购物袋，但时机不对。为了解决这个问题，我们需要……出门时要提醒自己带上购物袋。

It’s no accident that we think about reusable bags right when we get to the store. The grocery is a strong trigger for the bags. But unfortunately it is a badly timed one. Just as with the bath mat public service announcement, the idea is coming to mind, but at the wrong time. To solve this problem, we need to be reminded to bring the bags right when we are leaving the house.

在这种情况下，什么才是好的触发因素呢？任何你去超市购物必须带的东西。比如，你的购物清单就是一个很好的例子。想象一下，如果你每次看到购物清单，就想起你的可重复使用购物袋，那么你就很难把购物袋忘在家里了。

What’s a good trigger in this instance? Anything you have to take with you to buy groceries. Your shopping list, for example, is a great one. Imagine if every time you saw your shopping list, it made you think of your reusable bags. It would be much harder to leave the bags at home.

为什么 Cheerios 比迪士尼乐园更受口碑传播？

WHY CHEERIOS GETS MORE WORD OF MOUTH THAN DISNEY WORLD

回到本章开头的例子，触发因素有助于解释为什么麦片圈比迪士尼乐园更能获得口碑传播。诚然，迪士尼乐园有趣又刺激。用本书其他章节的话来说，它具有很高的社交价值，并且能唤起人们的强烈情感（下一章）。但问题在于，人们并不经常想起它。大多数人除非有孩子，否则不会去迪士尼乐园。即使是那些去过的人，去的频率也不高，一年一次就够了。而且，在最初的兴奋感消退之后，很少有触发因素能让他们想起这段经历。

To return to the example that started the chapter, triggers help explain why Cheerios get more word of mouth than Disney World. True, Disney World is interesting and exciting. To use the language of other chapters in the book, it has high Social Currency and evokes lots of Emotion (next chapter). But the problem is that people don’t think about it very frequently. Most people don’t go to Disney World unless they have kids. Even those who do go don’t go that often. Once a year if that. And there are few triggers to remind them about the experience after the initial excitement evaporates.

但每天有数十万人早餐吃 Cheerios 麦片。还有更多人每次推着购物车经过超市的谷物货架时，都会看到亮橙色的包装盒。这些因素让 Cheerios 更容易被消费者接触到，也增加了人们谈论这款产品的可能性。

But hundreds of thousands of people eat Cheerios for breakfast every day. Still more see the bright orange boxes every time they push their shopping carts down the supermarket cereal aisle. And these triggers make Cheerios more accessible, increasing the chance that people will talk about the product.

推特上提及 Cheerios 和迪士尼的次数很好地说明了这一点。Cheerios 的提及频率比迪士尼乐园高得多。但仔细分析数据后会发现……你会注意到一个清晰的图案。

The number of times Cheerios and Disney are mentioned on Twitter illustrates this nicely. Cheerios are mentioned more frequently than Disney World. But examine the data closely and you’ll notice a neat pattern.

在推特上提到 Cheerios

Mention of Cheerios on Twitter

每天几乎在同一时间，人们都会提到 Cheerios 麦片。最早的提及出现在早上 5 点，高峰期在早上 7:30 到 8:00 之间，并在上午 11 点左右开始下降。这种急剧的增加和相应的下降与传统的早餐时间完全吻合。即使在周末，由于人们早餐吃得晚，这种模式也会略有变化。触发因素驱动着人们的讨论。

Mentions of Cheerios spike every day at approximately the same time. The first references occur at 5:00 a.m. They peak between 7:30 a.m. and 8:00 a.m. And they diminish around 11:00 a.m. This sharp increase and corresponding decline align precisely with the traditional time for breakfast. The pattern even shifts slightly on weekends when people eat breakfast later. Triggers drive talking.

—————

—————

触发因素是口碑传播和传染效应的基础。打个比方，想想大多数摇滚乐队。社交货币就像乐队的主唱，它令人兴奋、充满乐趣，并且能吸引大量关注。触发因素就像乐队的鼓手或贝斯手。它不像社交货币那样引人注目，但却是推动成功的重要力量。人们可能不会特别关注它，但它为成功奠定了基础。触发的次数越多，它就越容易被人们记住，也就越容易取得成功。

Triggers are the foundation of word of mouth and contagiousness. To use an analogy, think of most rock bands. Social Currency is the front man or woman. It’s exciting, fun, and gets lots of attention. Triggers could be the drummer or bassist. It’s not as sexy a concept as Social Currency, but it’s an important workhorse that gets the job done. People may not pay as much attention to it, but it lays the groundwork that drives success. The more something is triggered, the more it will be top of mind, and the more successful it will become.

所以，我们需要考虑语境。就像百威啤酒的“wassup”或丽贝卡·布莱克的“星期五”一样，我们的产品和理念需要利用现有的触发因素。我们还需要拓展受众群体。就像科琳·乔拉克的奇巧巧克力和咖啡一样，我们需要与现有的触发因素建立新的联系。

So we need to consider the context. Like Budweiser’s “wassup” or Rebecca Black’s “Friday,” our products and ideas need to take advantage of existing triggers. We also need to grow the habitat. Like Colleen Chorak’s Kit Kat and coffee, we need to create new links to prevalent triggers.

触发因素和线索引导人们谈论、选择和使用。社交货币能让人们开始谈论，但触发因素能让他们持续谈论。“首选”意味着“脱口而出”。

Triggers and cues lead people to talk, choose, and use. Social currency gets people talking, but Triggers keep them talking. Top of mind means tip of tongue.

3.情绪

3. Emotion

截至2008年10月27日，丹尼斯·格雷迪为《纽约时报》撰写科学文章已超过十年。她善于捕捉奇特的话题，叙事风格娴熟，将深奥的科学知识以通俗易懂的方式呈现给普通读者，并因此赢得了众多新闻奖项。

By October 27, 2008, Denise Grady had been writing about science for The New York Times for more than a decade. With an eye for quirky topics and a deft narrative style, Grady won numerous journalism prizes by making esoteric topics accessible to lay readers.

那天，格雷迪的一篇文章迅速蹿升至报纸“最受欢迎邮件转发”榜单前列。文章发表后几个小时内，就有成千上万的人决定将这篇文章转发给他们的朋友、亲戚和同事。格雷迪的文章意外走红网络。

That day, one of Grady’s articles rocketed up the newspaper’s Most E-Mailed list. Within hours of its publication thousands of people had decided to pass on the article to their friends, relatives, and coworkers. Grady had scored a viral hit.

主题是？流体和气体动力学理论在医学研究中的应用。

The topic? How fluid and gas dynamic theories were being used in medical research.

格雷迪的文章详细描述了某种东西，叫做纹影摄影中，“一个小型明亮的光源、精确放置的透镜、一面弯曲的镜子、一片阻挡部分光束的剃刀以及其他工具，使得观察和拍摄空气中的扰动成为可能。”

Grady’s article detailed something called schlieren photography, in which “a small, bright light source, precisely placed lenses, a curved mirror, a razor blade that blocks part of the light beam and other tools make it possible to see and photograph disturbances in the air.”

听起来不够吸引人，对吧？别担心，很多人都这么觉得。当我们从多个角度询问人们对这篇文章的看法时，得分都很低。它有很多……社交货币？他们说不是。它包含很多实用信息吗（我们将在“实用价值”章节中讨论这一点）？答案仍然是否定的。

Sounds less than riveting, right? Join the club. When we asked people what they thought of this article on a number of different dimensions, the scores were pretty low. Did it have lots of Social Currency? No, they said. Did it contain a lot of practically useful information (something we’ll discuss in the Practical Value chapter)? No again.

事实上，如果你仔细核查那些传统上被认为是病毒式传播内容必备的特征，格雷迪那篇题为《神秘咳嗽，被镜头捕捉》的文章，几乎都不符合其中任何一项。然而，格雷迪的文章显然有其独特之处，否则不会有那么多人点击邮件按钮。那么，究竟是什么呢？

In fact, if you’d gone down the checklist of characteristics traditionally believed to be prerequisites for viral content, Grady’s article, entitled “The Mysterious Cough, Caught on Film,” would have lacked most of them. Yet Grady’s piece clearly had something special or so many people wouldn’t have hit the e-mail button. What was it?

—————

—————

格雷迪对科学的兴趣始于高中。当时她正在上化学课，读到了罗伯特·密立根著名的测定单个电子电荷的实验。这既是一个复杂的概念，也是一个复杂的实验。该实验需要将微小的油滴悬浮在两个金属电极之间，然后测量阻止油滴下落所需的电场强度。

Grady’s interest in science started in high school. She was sitting in chemistry class when she read about Robert Millikan’s famous experiment to determine the charge on a single electron. It was a complicated idea and a complicated experiment. The study involved suspending tiny droplets of oil between two metal electrodes, then measuring how strong the electric field had to be in order to stop the droplets from falling.

格雷迪读了好几遍。一遍又一遍，直到她最终理解为止。但当她明白的那一刻，就像一道闪电划过。她明白了。这感觉太棒了。实验背后的思路如此巧妙，能够理解它让她着迷。她彻底被吸引住了。

Grady read it several times. Again and again until she finally understood. But when she did, it was like a flash going off. She got it. It was thrilling. The thinking behind the experiment was so clever, and being able to grasp it was enthralling. She was hooked.

格雷迪放学后先是在《今日物理》杂志社工作，之后又先后在《发现》和《时代》杂志任职，最终晋升为《纽约时报》的健康版编辑。她的文章目标始终如一：让人们哪怕只感受到一丝她几十年前在化学课上感受到的那种兴奋——对科学发现的奇妙之处的欣赏。

After school Grady went to work at Physics Today magazine. Eventually she worked at Discover and Time magazine and finally worked her way up to health editor at The New York Times. The goal of her articles was always the same: to give people even just a little bit of that excitement that she had felt back in chemistry class decades before. An appreciation for the magic of scientific discovery.

在她那年十月的文章中，格雷迪描述了一位工程学教授如何运用摄影技术捕捉到可见光这张图像展现了一种看似不可见的现象——人类咳嗽。纹影技术多年来一直被航空和军事专家用于研究高速飞机周围冲击波的形成。但这位工程学教授以一种新的方式运用了这项技术：研究肺结核、SARS 和流感等空气传播疾病的传播方式。

In her piece that October, Grady described how an engineering professor used a photographic technique to capture a visible image of a seemingly invisible phenomenon—a human cough. The schlieren technique had been used for years by aeronautics and military specialists to study how shock waves form around high-speed aircraft. But the engineering professor had harnessed the technique in a new way: to study how airborne infections like tuberculosis, SARS, and influenza spread.

大多数人觉得这篇文章没什么用，这也不难理解。毕竟，他们既不是研究流体动力学的科学家，也不是试图将复杂现象可视化的工程师。

It made sense that most people thought the article wasn’t particularly useful. After all, they weren’t scientists studying fluid dynamics. Nor were they engineers trying to visualize complex phenomena.

虽然格雷迪是顶尖的科学作家之一，但普通大众更倾向于阅读体育或时尚类文章也是情理之中的事。最后，虽然咳嗽确实能很好地提醒人们想起这篇文章，但感冒和流感季的高峰期通常在二月份左右，也就是文章发表四个月之后。

And while Grady is one of the best science writers out there, it made sense that the general population would tend to be more interested in articles about sports or fashion. Finally, while coughs would certainly be a nice trigger to remind people of the article, cold and flu season tends to peak around February, four months after the article was released.

就连格雷迪也感到困惑。作为一名记者，她最乐于见到自己写的文章爆红网络。和大多数记者，甚至普通博主一样，她也很想知道为什么有些文章能被广泛传播，而有些却无人问津。

Even Grady was bemused. As a journalist, she’s delighted when something she writes goes viral. And like most journalists, or even casual bloggers, she’d love to understand why some of her pieces get widely shared while others don’t.

虽然她可以做出一些合理的猜测，但她和其他人一样，都不知道为什么有些内容比其他内容更容易被分享。究竟是什么让这篇文章爆红网络？

But while she could make some educated guesses, neither she nor anyone else really knew why one piece of content gets shared more than another. What made this particular article go viral?

—————

—————

经过多年的分析，我很高兴地报告，我和我的同事们已经找到了一些答案。格雷迪2008年的文章是我们一项多年研究的一部分，在该研究中，我们分析了数千篇《纽约时报》的文章，以更好地了解为什么某些网络内容会被广泛传播。

After years of analysis, I’m happy to report that my colleagues and I have some answers. Grady’s 2008 article was part of a multi-year study in which we analyzed thousands of New York Times articles to better understand why certain pieces of online content are widely shared.

格雷迪的照片里藏着一条线索。那年十月初，她在浏览《新英格兰医学杂志》时，偶然看到一篇题为《咳嗽与气溶胶》的文章。她一眼就看出，这项研究非常适合写一篇发表在《纽约时报》上的文章。文章的部分内容相当专业，讨论了传染性气溶胶和速度图。但抛开所有专业术语，最吸引她的是一张简单的图片，正是这张图片促使格雷迪决定撰写这篇文章。

A clue comes from the picture that accompanied Grady’s piece. Earlier that October, she had been scanning an issue of The New England Journal of Medicine when she came across a piece entitled “Coughing and Aerosols.” As soon as she saw it she knew the research would be the perfect basis for an article in the Times. Some of the piece was pretty technical, with discussions of infectious aerosols and velocity maps. But above all the jargon was a simple image, an image that made Grady decide to write her article.

简而言之，这太棒了。人们分享格雷迪的文章是因为情感。当我们关心某件事时，我们就会分享。

Simply put, it was amazing. The reason people shared Grady’s article was emotion. When we care, we share.

大多数电子邮件列表及其共享的重要性

MOST E-MAILED LISTS AND THE IMPORTANCE OF SHARING

人类是社会性动物。正如“社交货币”一章所述，人们喜欢与他人分享观点和信息。而我们爱八卦的倾向——无论好坏——都会影响我们与朋友和同事的关系。

Humans are social animals. As discussed in the chapter on Social Currency, people love to share opinions and information with others. And our tendency to gossip—for good or ill—shapes our relationships with friends and colleagues alike.

互联网的设计越来越倾向于迎合这些自然倾向。如果人们偶然看到一篇关于新的自行车共享计划的博客文章，或者找到一个帮助孩子解决复杂代数题的视频，他们可以轻松地点击“分享”按钮，或者将链接复制粘贴到电子邮件中。

The Internet has become increasingly engineered to support these natural inclinations. If people come across a blog post about a new bike sharing program or find a video that helps kids solve tough algebra problems, they can easily hit the Share button or copy and paste the link into an e-mail.

大多数主流新闻或娱乐网站都会额外记录哪些内容传播最广，列出过去一天、一周或一个月内浏览量或分享量最高的文章、视频和其他内容。

Most major news or entertainment websites take the extra step of documenting what has been passed along most frequently. Listing which articles, videos, and other content have been most viewed or shared over the past day, week, or month.

人们常常把这些列表当作捷径。可获取的内容实在太多，根本无法一一筛选——数亿个网站和博客，数十亿个视频。仅新闻一项，就有数十家信誉卓著的媒体持续发布新文章。

People often use these lists as shortcuts. There is way too much content available to sift through it all—hundreds of millions of websites and blogs, billions of videos. For news alone, dozens of highly reputable outlets continuously produce new articles.

很少有人有时间在浩瀚的信息海洋中寻找优质内容。所以他们通常先看看别人分享了什么。

Few people have time to seek out the best content in this ocean of information. So they start by checking out what others have shared.

因此，最受欢迎榜单对塑造公共舆论具有强大的影响力。如果一篇关于金融改革的文章恰好上榜，而一篇关于环境改革的文章勉强落榜，那么最初这微小的关注度差异会迅速放大。随着越来越多的人看到并分享关于金融改革的文章，民众可能会逐渐相信，金融改革比环境改革更值得政府关注，即便金融问题并不严重，而环境问题却十分严峻。

As a result, most-shared lists have a powerful ability to shape public discourse. If an article about financial reform happens to make the list, while one about environmental reform barely falls short, that initially small difference in interest can quickly become magnified. As more people see and share the article about financial reform, citizens may become convinced that financial reform deserves more governmental attention than environmental reform, even if the financial issue is mild and the environmental issue severe.

那么，为什么有些内容会登上“电子邮件发送量最高”榜单，而有些内容却不会呢？

So why does some content make the Most E-Mailed list while other content does not?

要想让内容爆红，很多人必须在同一时间转发同一篇文章。你可能很喜欢丹尼斯·格雷迪关于咳嗽的文章，或许你也分享过。我和几个朋友一起读过。但要想让这篇文章登上“邮件转发最多”榜单，就必须有大量的人做出和你一样的决定。

For something to go viral, lots of people have to pass along the same piece of content at around the same time. You might have enjoyed Denise Grady’s cough article, and maybe you shared it with a couple of friends. But for the piece to make the Most E-Mailed list, a large number of people had to make the same decision you did.

这仅仅是随机现象吗？还是说病毒式传播的成功背后存在一些一致的模式？

Is this just random? Or might there be some consistent patterns underlying viral success?

系统分析电子邮件发送次数最多的列表

SYSTEMATICALLY ANALYZING THE MOST E-MAILED LIST

斯坦福研究生的生活远称不上光鲜亮丽。我的办公室，如果可以称之为办公室的话，就是一个高墙隔间。它位于一栋建于20世纪60年代的建筑的阁楼里，这栋建筑没有窗户，其建筑风格常被描述为“粗野主义”。这是一栋矮胖的建筑，混凝土墙厚得仿佛能抵挡小型榴弹发射器的直接攻击。我们六十个人挤在这个狭小的空间里，而我那十乘十英尺、灯光昏暗的小隔间还要和另一个学生共用。

The life of a Stanford graduate student is far from grand. My office, if you could call it that, was a high-walled cubicle. It was tucked up in a windowless attic of a 1960s-era building whose architectural style has often been described as “brutalist.” A short, squat structure with concrete walls so thick they could probably withstand a direct hit from a small grenade launcher. Sixty of us were clustered together in a cramped space, and my own ten-by-ten fluorescent-lit box was shared with another student.

唯一的好处就是电梯。研究生们需要全天候工作，所以学校给我们发了一张门禁卡，可以24小时使用专用电梯。它不仅能把我们送到没有窗户的工作间，还能让我们进入图书馆，即使图书馆关门了也行。这算不上什么奢华的福利，但确实很实用。

The one upside was the elevator. Graduate students were expected to be working at all times of day and night, so the school gave us a keycard that allowed twenty-four-hour access to a special lift. Not only did it take us up to our windowless workstations, it also gave us access to the library, even after it closed. Not the most lavish perk, but a useful one.

那时候，网络内容的传播远不如今天这般成熟。如今，内容网站会将邮件转发量最高的文章列表发布在网上，但一些报纸也会在纸质版上刊登这些列表。《华尔街日报》每天都会发布前一天新闻中阅读量最高的五篇文章和邮件转发量最高的五篇文章。浏览了其中几个列表后，我被深深吸引了。这似乎是研究为什么有些内容比其他内容更容易被分享的完美数据来源。

Back then the distribution of online content was not as sophisticated as it is today. Content websites now post their most e-mailed lists online, but some newspapers published these lists in their print editions as well. Every day The Wall Street Journal published a list of the five most read articles and the five most e-mailed articles from the previous day’s news. After scanning a couple of these lists, I was enthralled. It seemed like the perfect data source to study why some things get shared more than others.

就像集邮者收集邮票一样，我开始收集《华尔街日报》收到的电子邮件最多的名单。

So just as a stamp collector collects stamps, I began to collect the Journal’s Most Emailed list.

每隔几天，我就会搭乘那部特殊的电梯去“寻宝”。我会带着我那把可靠的剪刀，在深夜下到图书馆，找到一摞最新一期的《期刊》印刷版，然后小心翼翼地剪下那些“邮件发送量最高”的列表。

Once every couple of days I would use the special elevator to go hunting. I would take my trusty scissors down to the library late at night, find a stack of the most recent print editions of the Journal, and carefully clip out the Most Emailed lists.

几周后，我的剪报收藏越来越多。我攒了一大摞新闻剪报，一切准备就绪。我把这些剪报整理到电子表格里，开始寻找规律。有一天，《如何应对“冷场”：夫妻疲惫到无法沟通》和《迪士尼礼服是给大女孩穿的》这两篇文章成了转发量最高的。几天后，《经济学家有资格解开自闭症之谜吗？》和《为什么观鸟者现在都带着iPod和激光笔？》也上了榜单。

After a few weeks, my collection had grown. I had a big stack of news clippings and was ready to go. I entered the lists in a spreadsheet and began looking for patterns. One day “Dealing with the Dead Zone: Spouses Too Tired to Talk” and “Disney Gowns Are for Big Girls” were two of the most e-mailed articles. A few days later “Is an Economist Qualified to Solve Puzzle of Autism?” and “Why Birdwatchers Now Carry iPods and Laser Pointers” made the list.

—————

—————

嗯。乍一看，这些文章似乎没什么共同之处。疲惫的配偶和迪士尼礼服有什么关系？迪士尼和研究自闭症的经济学家又有什么关系？它们之间的联系并不显而易见。

Hmm. On the face of it, these articles had few characteristics in common. What did tired spouses have to do with Disney gowns? And what did Disney have to do with economists studying autism? The connections were not going to be obvious.

此外，一次只读一两篇文章是远远不够的。为了掌握要领，我需要更快、更高效地工作。

Further, reading one or two articles at a time wasn’t going to cut it. To get a handle on things I needed to work faster and more efficiently.

幸运的是，我的同事凯瑟琳·米尔克曼提出了一种改进得多的方法。与其手动从纸质报纸上提取这些信息，为什么不将这个过程自动化呢？

Luckily my colleague Katherine Milkman suggested a vastly improved method. Rather than pull this information from the print newspaper by hand, why not automate the process?

在一位程序员的帮助下，我们创建了一个网络爬虫。就像一位不知疲倦的读者，这个程序每隔十五分钟自动扫描一次《纽约时报》首页，并记录下它看到的内容。不仅包括每篇文章的正文和标题，还包括作者和文章的发布位置（发布在……）。（主屏幕或隐藏在链接路径中）。它还记录了文章在纸质报纸的哪个版块（例如健康或商业）以及出现在哪一页（例如头版或第三版的背面）。

With the help of a computer programmer, we created a Web crawler. Like a never tiring reader, the program automatically scanned The New York Times home page every fifteen minutes, recording what it saw. Not only the text and title of each article, but also who wrote it and where it was featured (posted on the main screen or hidden in a trail of links). It also recorded in which section of the physical paper (health or business, for example) and on what page the article appeared (such as the front page or the back of the third section).

六个月后，我们积累了一个庞大的数据集—— 《纽约时报》在此期间发表的每一篇文章，近七千篇。内容涵盖世界新闻、体育、健康和科技等各个方面，还包括同期“电子邮件发送量最高”的文章榜单。

After six months we had a huge data set—every article published by The New York Times over that period. Almost seven thousand articles. Everything from world news and sports to health and technology, as well as which articles made the Most E-Mailed list for those same six months.

不仅仅是一个人分享的内容，而是衡量所有读者（无论年龄、财富或其他人口统计特征）与他人分享的内容。

Not just what one person shared, but a measure of what all readers, regardless of their age, wealth, or other demographics, were sharing with others.

现在我们可以开始分析了。

Now our analysis could begin.

—————

—————

首先，我们查看了每篇文章的大致主题，例如健康、体育、教育或政治。

First, we looked at the general topic of each article. Things like health, sports, education, or politics.

结果显示，教育类文章比体育类文章更容易登上“邮件转发最多”榜单。健康类文章比政治类文章更容易传播。

The results showed that education articles were more likely to make the Most E-Mailed list than sports articles. Health pieces were more viral than political ones.

不错。但我们更感兴趣的是了解是什么驱动了分享行为，而不是仅仅描述被分享内容的属性。好吧，体育文章的传播速度不如美食文章。但这是为什么呢？这就好比说人们更喜欢分享猫咪照片或谈论彩弹射击，而不是乒乓球一样。这并没有真正告诉我们为什么会发生这种情况，也没有让我们做出超出猫咪相关事物或以字母 P 开头的体育运动的预测。

Nice. But we were more interested in understanding what drives sharing than in simply describing the attributes of content that was shared. Okay, so sports articles are less viral than dining articles. But why? It’s like saying people like to share pictures of cats or talk about paintball more than Ping-Pong. That doesn’t really tell us much about why that is happening or allow us to make predictions beyond the narrow domains of cat stuff or sports that start with the letter P.

人们分享事物的原因有两个：一是它们有趣，二是它们有用。正如我们在“社交货币”一章中所讨论的，有趣的事物既能带来娱乐，又能反映生活。分享信息的人也会因此获得积极的评价。同样地，正如我们将在“实用价值”章节中讨论的那样，分享有用的信息不仅能帮助他人，还能让分享者自身也获得认可。

Two reasons people might share things are that they are interesting and that they are useful. As we discussed in the Social Currency chapter, interesting things are entertaining and reflect positively on the person who shares them. Similarly, as we’ll discuss in the Practical Value chapter, sharing useful information helps others and makes the sharer look good in the process.

为了验证这些理论，我们雇佣了一小队研究助理，对《纽约时报》的文章进行评分，评估其信息实用性和趣味性。例如，关于谷歌如何利用搜索数据追踪流感传播的文章被评为趣味性极高，而一篇关于百老汇戏剧演员阵容变动的文章则被评为趣味性较低。关于如何控制信用评分的文章被评为非常实用，而一篇关于一位名不见经传的歌剧演员的讣告则被评为毫无用处。我们将这些评分输入到一个统计分析程序中，并将其与“最常被发送邮件”列表进行比较。

To test these theories, we hired a small army of research assistants to score New York Times articles on whether they contained useful information and how interesting they were. Articles about things like how Google uses search data to track the spread of the flu were scored as highly interesting, while an article about the change in the cast of a Broadway play was scored as less interesting. Articles about how to control your credit score were scored as being very useful, while the obituary of an obscure opera singer was scored as not useful. We fed these scores into a statistical analysis program that compared them with the Most E-Mailed lists.

正如我们预期的那样，这两个特征都影响了分享。更有趣的文章更有可能出现在“邮件发送最多”的名单中，可能性高出25%。更有用的文章上榜的可能性高出 30%。

As we expected, both characteristics influenced sharing. More interesting articles were 25 percent more likely to make the Most E-Mailed list. More useful articles were 30 percent more likely to make the list.

这些结果有助于解释为什么健康和教育类文章的分享量很高。这类文章通常非常实用，例如如何活得更长久、更快乐，以及如何为孩子争取最好的教育。

These results helped explain why health and education articles were highly shared. Articles about these topics are often quite useful. Advice on how to live longer and be happier. Tips for getting the best education for your kids.

但有一个话题却格外引人注目：科学文章。这些文章大多不像主流文章那样具有社交价值或实用价值。然而，像丹尼斯·格雷迪关于咳嗽的文章这样的科学文章，在“最常被发送的电子邮件”榜单上的排名却超过了政治、时尚或商业新闻。这是为什么呢？

But there was still one topic that stood out like a sore thumb: science articles. For the most part, these articles did not have as much Social Currency or Practical Value as articles from more mainstream sections. Yet science articles, like Denise Grady’s piece about the cough, made the Most E-Mailed list more than politics, fashion, or business news. Why?

事实证明，科学文章经常记录那些能唤起读者某种特定情感的创新和发现。这种情感是什么呢？敬畏。

It turns out that science articles frequently chronicle innovations and discoveries that evoke a particular emotion in readers. That emotion? Awe.

敬畏的力量

THE POWER OF AWE

想象一下，你站在大峡谷的边缘。血红色的峡谷向四面八方延伸，一眼望不到边。峡谷底部陡然下降，直落脚下。你感到一阵眩晕，不由自主地后退了几步。雄鹰在光秃秃的岩石缝隙间盘旋，寸草不生，仿佛置身月球。你惊叹不已，你感到自身的渺小，你感到精神振奋。这就是敬畏。

Imagine standing on the very edge of the Grand Canyon. The bloodred gorge stretches as far as you can see in every direction. The canyon floor drops precipitously below your feet. You feel dizzy and step back from the edge. Hawks circle through rock crevasses so barren and stripped of vegetation you could as well be on the moon. You are amazed. You are humbled. You feel elevated. This is awe.

根据心理学家达彻·凯尔特纳和乔纳森·海特的说法，敬畏是一种惊叹和赞叹之情，它源于人们对渊博的知识、绝世的美、崇高的气魄或强大的力量的感悟。它是面对超越自身之物的体验。敬畏能够拓展人的认知范围，并促使人超越自我。它包含钦佩和灵感，可以从伟大的艺术作品或音乐，到宗教的变革；从令人叹为观止的自然景观，到人类的勇敢探索和发现，任何事物都能唤起敬畏之心。

According to psychologists Dacher Keltner and Jonathan Haidt, awe is the sense of wonder and amazement that occurs when someone is inspired by great knowledge, beauty, sublimity, or might. It’s the experience of confronting something greater than yourself. Awe expands one’s frame of reference and drives self-transcendence. It encompasses admiration and inspiration and can be evoked by everything from great works of art or music to religious transformations, from breathtaking natural landscapes to human feats of daring and discovery.

敬畏是一种复杂的情感，通常包含着惊讶、意外或神秘感。正如阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦本人所指出的那样，“我们所能体验到的最美妙的情感是神秘感。它是所有真正艺术和科学的力量源泉。对这种情感感到陌生的人，如果不能停下来惊叹，不能再沉浸在敬畏之中，那么他就如同行尸走肉一般。

Awe is a complex emotion and frequently involves a sense of surprise, unexpectedness, or mystery. Indeed, as Albert Einstein himself noted, “The most beautiful emotion we can experience is the mysterious. It is the power of all true art and science. He to whom this emotion is a stranger, who can no longer pause to wonder and stand rapt in awe, is as good as dead.”

许多读者在阅读了《纽约时报》的科学文章后，最强烈的感受莫过于敬畏。以《神秘咳嗽，影像捕捉》为例。这张咳嗽照片不仅视觉震撼，更引人深思：如此平凡的咳嗽竟能呈现出如此景象，并揭示出足以解开几个世纪以来医学谜团的秘密。

More than any other emotion, awe described what many readers felt after looking at science pieces from The New York Times. Take “The Mysterious Cough, Caught on Film.” The photo of the cough was stunning both as a visual spectacle and as an idea: that something as mundane as a cough could produce this image and yield secrets capable of solving centuries-old medical mysteries.

我们决定检验敬畏感是否会促使人们分享。我们的研究助理回顾并根据文章引发的敬畏感程度对其进行评分。关于艾滋病新疗法或患有脑癌仍坚持比赛的冰球守门员的文章引发了强烈的敬畏感。而关于假日购物优惠的文章则几乎没有引发敬畏感。随后，我们运用统计分析方法，将这些评分与文章的分享量进行比较。

We decided to test whether awe drove people to share. Our research assistants went back and scored the articles based on how much awe they evoked. Articles about a new treatment for AIDS or a hockey goalie who plays even though he has brain cancer evoked lots of awe. Articles about holiday shopping bargains evoked little or no awe. We then used statistical analyses to compare these scores with whether articles were highly shared.

我们的直觉是对的：敬畏之心促进了分享。

Our intuition was right: awe boosted sharing.

令人叹为观止的文章登上“最受欢迎邮件列表”的概率高出30%。一些此前被认为社交价值和实用价值较低的文章——比如格雷迪关于咳嗽的文章，或者一篇认为大猩猩可能像人类一样，在失去亲人时也会悲伤的文章——却因为它们所引发的敬畏之情而登上了“最受欢迎邮件列表”。

Awe-inspiring articles were 30 percent more likely to make the Most E-Mailed list. Articles previously judged to have low Social Currency and Practical Value—Grady’s cough piece or an article suggesting that gorillas may, like humans, grieve when losing loved ones—nevertheless made the Most E-Mailed list because of the awe they inspired.

—————

—————

网络上一些最热门的视频也令人惊叹。

Some of the Web’s most viral videos also evoke awe.

当那位身材丰腴、老态龙钟的妇人走上舞台时，窃笑声立刻响起。她看起来更像个食堂阿姨，而不是歌手。首先，她年纪太大，不适合参加《英国达人秀》。她已经47岁了，比其他许多参赛选手的年龄都大了一倍多。

The snickering started as soon as the plump, matronly woman walked onto the stage. She looked more like a lunch lady than a vocalist. First, she was too old to be competing on Britain’s Got Talent. At forty-seven, she was more than twice the age of many of the other contestants.

但更重要的是，她看起来……嗯，土气十足。其他参赛者都打扮得光鲜亮丽，力求成为下一个时尚偶像。她们性感、英俊潇洒或时髦前卫，穿着贴身连衣裙、剪裁合身的马甲和夏季围巾。而这位女士的装扮更像是反面教材，她的穿着就像是旧窗帘和二手复活节连衣裙的混合体。

But, more important, she looked, well, frumpy. The other competitors were already dressed to be the next big thing. Sexy, ruggedly handsome, or hip. They wore form-fitting dresses, tailored vests, and summer scarves. But this woman looked more like an example of what not to wear. Her outfit looked like a cross between an old set of drapes and a secondhand Easter dress.

她很紧张。评委们开始提问时，她卡壳了，说话结结巴巴。“你的梦想是什么？”他们问道。当她回答说她想成为一名……简直可以预见他们脑子里的想法。真是讽刺！你？一个专业歌手？镜头拉近，拍到观众席上有人大笑、翻白眼。就连评委们也露出了得意的笑容。他们显然希望她尽快下台。种种迹象表明，她会表现得一塌糊涂，然后立刻被淘汰出局。

And she was nervous. When the judges started asking her questions she got stuck and stumbled on her words. “What’s the dream?” they inquired. When she replied that she wanted to be a professional singer you could just see the thoughts going through their heads. That’s rich! You? A professional singer? The cameras zoomed in on members of the audience laughing and rolling their eyes. Even the judges smirked. They clearly wanted her to get off the stage as soon as possible. All signs pointed to her giving a terrible performance and being booted from the show, pronto.

但正当一切似乎不能更糟的时候，她开始唱歌了。

But just as it seemed that it couldn’t get any worse, she started singing.

时间仿佛静止了。

And time stopped.

真是美得令人窒息。

It was breathtaking.

当《悲惨世界》主题曲《我曾有梦》的前奏从音响中飘荡而出，苏珊·博伊尔那宛如灯塔般闪耀的绝美嗓音便映入耳帘。如此震撼人心，如此动人心弦，令人毛骨悚然。评委们惊叹不已，观众们尖叫连连，掌声雷动。有些人甚至热泪盈眶。这场演出令人叹为观止，久久无法言语。

As the opening chords from “I Dreamed a Dream” from Les Misérables wafted over the speakers, Susan Boyle’s exquisite voice shone through like a beacon. So powerful, so beautiful that it makes the hair on the back of your neck stand up. The judges were awed, the audience screamed, and everyone broke out into wild applause. Some started tearing up as they listened. The performance left people speechless.

苏珊·博伊尔首次亮相《英国达人秀》的视频是有史以来最火爆的视频之一。短短九天内，该视频的点击量就超过了1亿次。

Susan Boyle’s first appearance on Britain’s Got Talent is one of the most viral videos ever. In just nine short days, the clip accumulated more than 100 million views.

看完这段视频，很难不被她的坚强和善良所感动。它不仅令人动容，更令人敬畏。正是这种情感促使人们将视频转发出去。

It’s hard to watch this video and not be awed by her strength and heart. It’s not only moving, it’s awe-inspiring. And that emotion drove people to pass it on.

任何情绪都会促进分享吗？

DOES ANY EMOTION BOOST SHARING?

我们在《纽约时报》上的初步调查结果引出了其他问题。敬畏之心究竟有何魔力促使人们分享？其他情绪是否也会产生同样的效果？

Our initial New York Times findings brought up other questions. What about awe makes people share? Might other emotions have the same effect?

有理由相信，体验任何类型的情绪都可能鼓励人们分享。与他人交谈通常会……情感体验会更好。如果我们升职了，告诉别人可以帮助我们庆祝。如果我们被解雇了，告诉别人可以帮助我们发泄情绪。

There are reasons to believe that experiencing any sort of emotion might encourage people to share. Talking to others often makes emotional experiences better. If we get promoted, telling others helps us celebrate. If we get fired, telling others helps us vent.

分享情感也有助于我们建立联系。比如说，我看了一段非常精彩的视频，比如苏珊·博伊尔的表演。如果我把这段视频分享给朋友，他很可能也会受到同样的启发。而我们彼此的感受相同，这本身就是一种情感上的共鸣。情感分享有助于加深我们的社交联系。它凸显了我们的相似之处，并提醒我们彼此之间有多少共同点。因此，情感分享就像社交粘合剂，维系并加强了人际关系。即使我们身处异地，彼此相同的感受也能将我们紧紧联系在一起。

Sharing emotions also helps us connect. Say I watch a really awe-inspiring video, like Susan Boyle’s performance. If I share that video with a friend, he’s likely to feel similarly inspired. And the fact that we both feel the same way helps deepen our social connection. It highlights our similarities and reminds us how much we have in common. Emotion sharing is thus a bit like social glue, maintaining and strengthening relationships. Even if we’re not in the same place, the fact that we both feel the same way bonds us together.

但分享情感的这些好处并非仅仅源于敬畏之情。它们适用于各种各样的情感。

But these benefits of sharing emotion don’t just arise from awe alone. They happen for all sorts of emotions.

如果你给同事发一个你们俩都觉得好笑的笑话，这会加深你们之间的联系。如果你给表兄弟发一篇你们俩都感到愤怒的评论文章，这会强化你们观点一致的事实。

If you send a coworker a joke that cracks both of you up, it underscores your connection. If you send your cousin an op-ed piece that makes you both angry, it strengthens the fact that you share the same views.

那么，任何类型的情感内容是否都更容易被分享呢？

So would any type of emotional content be more likely to be shared?

为了解答这个问题，我们选择了另一种情绪——悲伤，并重新深入分析了数据。我们请研究助理根据每篇文章引发的悲伤程度进行评分。例如，关于某人缅怀已故祖母的文章被评为引发了较多悲伤情绪，而关于高尔夫球手获胜的文章则被评为引发的悲伤情绪较少。如果某种情绪能够促进分享，那么悲伤——就像敬畏一样——也应该能够促进分享。

To answer this, we picked another emotion, sadness, and dove back into the data. We asked our research assistants to score each article based on how much sadness it evoked. Articles about things like someone paying tribute to his deceased grandmother were scored as evoking a good deal of sadness, while articles about things like a winning golfer were scored as low sadness. If any emotion boosted sharing, then sadness—like awe—should also increase sharing.

但事实并非如此。实际上，悲伤的情绪产生了相反的效果。更悲伤的文章登上“最常被转发”榜单的可能性反而降低了16% 。悲伤似乎降低了人们分享的意愿。这是怎么回事？

But it didn’t. In fact, sadness had the opposite effect. Sadder articles were actually 16 percent less likely to make the Most E-Mailed list. Something about sadness was making people less likely to share. What?

—————

—————

不同情绪之间最明显的区别在于它们的愉悦度或积极性。敬畏感相对令人愉悦，而悲伤感则令人不快。积极情绪是否会促进分享，而消极情绪是否会减少分享？

The most obvious difference between different emotions is their pleasantness or positivity. Awe is relatively pleasant, while sadness is unpleasant. Might positive emotions increase sharing, but negative emotions decrease it?

人们长期以来一直在思考积极和消极情绪如何影响人们谈论和分享的内容。传统观点认为：负面内容更容易传播。想想新闻界那句老话：“血腥新闻最吸睛。” 这句话基于这样的理念：坏消息比好消息更能吸引眼球。这就是为什么晚间新闻总是以这样的开头：“你家地下室里潜藏着一个健康隐患。更多详情，请收看六点新闻。” 编辑和制片人相信，负面新闻有助于吸引并留住观众的注意力。

People have long speculated about how positive and negative emotions influence what people talk about and share. Conventional wisdom suggests that negative content should be more viral. Consider the old news adage “If it bleeds, it leads.” This phrase is based on the notion that bad news generates more attention and interest than good news. That’s why the nightly news always starts with something like: “The hidden health hazard that’s lurking in your basement. Find out more, next, on the six o’clock news.” Editors and producers believe that negative stories will help draw, and keep, viewers’ attention.

话虽如此，你也可以提出相反的观点：人们更倾向于分享好消息。毕竟，我们大多数人难道不想让别人感到快乐或积极，而不是焦虑或悲伤吗？同样，正如我们在“社交货币”那一章讨论的那样，人们是否分享某些东西往往取决于这会让他们在别人眼中显得如何。积极的事情之所以更容易被分享，是因为它们能提升分享者的形象。毕竟，没有人想成为“扫兴鬼”，总是分享一些令人沮丧和悲观的事情。

That said, you could also make a case for the opposite: that people prefer sharing good news. After all, don’t most of us want to make others feel happy or positive rather than anxious or sad? Similarly, as we discussed in the chapter on Social Currency, whether people share something often depends on how it makes them look to others. Positive things may be shared more because they reflect positively on the person doing the sharing. After all, no one wants to be Debbie Downer, always sharing things that are sad and gloomy.

那么究竟是哪种情况呢？是正面信息更容易传播，还是负面信息更容易传播？

So which is it? Is positive information more likely to be shared than negative, or vice versa?

我们重新打开数据库，测量了每篇文章的正面程度。这次我们使用了由……开发的文本分析程序。心理学家杰米·彭尼贝克。该项目量化了一段文字中积极和消极情绪的程度我们统计了数百个不同情感词汇出现的次数。例如，“我喜欢这张卡片；她真好”这句话相对积极，因为它包含“喜欢”和“好”等积极词汇。而“她真恶毒；这真的伤了我的心”这句话则相对消极，因为它包含“伤”和“恶毒”等消极词汇。我们根据每篇文章的积极或消极程度对其进行评分，然后分析评分结果与文章是否进入“邮件发送量最高”榜单之间的关系。

We went back to our database and measured the positivity of each article. This time we used a textual analysis program developed by psychologist Jamie Pennebaker. The program quantifies the amount of positivity and negativity in a passage of text by counting the number of times hundreds of different emotional words appear. The sentence “I loved the card; that was so nice of her,” for example, is relatively positive because it contains positive words like “love” and “nice.” The sentence “That was so nasty of her; it really hurt my feelings,” on the other hand, is relatively negative because of negative words like “hurt” and “nasty.” We scored each article based on its positivity or negativity and then examined how that related to whether it made the Most E-Mailed list.

答案很明确：正面文章比负面文章更容易被广泛分享。例如，关于正面事件的文章。平均而言，爱上纽约市的新来者登上“最常被发送的电子邮件”榜单的可能性比报道诸如一位受欢迎的动物园管理员去世之类的文章高出 13%。

The answer was definitive: positive articles were more likely to be highly shared than negative ones. Stories about things like newcomers falling in love with New York City were, on average, 13 percent more likely to make the Most E-Mailed list than pieces that detailed things like the death of a popular zookeeper.

—————

—————

最终，我们确信自己理解了情绪如何影响信息传播。人们似乎倾向于分享积极的事情，而避免分享消极的事情。

Finally we were feeling confident that we understood how emotion shapes transmission. It seemed like people share positive things and avoid sharing negative ones.

为了确保我们关于负面情绪会减少分享的结论正确，我们给研究助理布置了最后一项任务。我们要求他们根据另外两种主要的负面情绪——愤怒和焦虑——对每篇文章进行评分。

But just to be sure that we were correct that negative emotions decrease sharing, we gave our research assistants one final task. We asked them to score each article on two other major negative emotions: anger and anxiety.

关于华尔街大佬们在经济低迷时期获得巨额奖金之类的文章引发了众怒，而关于夏季T恤衫之类的文章却丝毫没有引起愤怒。关于股市暴跌之类的文章让人们相当焦虑，而关于艾美奖提名之类的文章却没有引起焦虑。如果人们真的倾向于分享积极内容而避免分享消极内容，那么愤怒和焦虑应该像悲伤一样，会减少分享行为。

Articles about things like Wall Street fat cats getting hefty bonuses during the economic downturn induced lots of anger, while articles about topics like summer T-shirts evoked no anger at all. Articles about things like the stock market tanking made people pretty anxious, while articles about things like Emmy Award nominees evoked no anxiety. If it were true that people share positive content and avoid sharing negative content, then anger and anxiety should, like sadness, reduce sharing.

但事实并非如此。实际上，情况恰恰相反。文章那些引发愤怒或焦虑的内容更有可能登上“电子邮件发送最多”的榜单。

But this wasn’t the case. In fact, it was the opposite. Articles that evoked anger or anxiety were more likely to make the Most E-Mailed list.

我们彻底糊涂了。显然，文章的传播范围不仅仅取决于文章是正面还是负面，还受到更复杂因素的影响。但究竟是什么因素呢？

Now we were really confused. Clearly, something more complicated than whether an article was positive or negative determined how widely things were shared. But what?

点燃火焰：科学生理唤醒

KINDLING THE FIRE: THE SCIENCE OF PHYSIOLOGICAL AROUSAL

情绪可以分为积极或愉悦和消极或不愉悦两类，这种观念已经存在了数百年甚至数千年。即使是孩子也能告诉你，快乐或兴奋让人感觉良好，而焦虑或悲伤让人感觉糟糕。

The idea that emotions can be categorized as positive or pleasant and negative or unpleasant has been around for hundreds if not thousands of years. Even a child can tell you that happiness or excitement feels good and anxiety or sadness feels bad.

然而，最近有心理学家提出，情绪还可以根据第二个维度进行分类，即激活或生理唤醒。

More recently, however, psychologists have argued that emotions can also be classified based on a second dimension. That of activation, or physiological arousal.

什么是生理唤醒？想想你上次在众多听众面前演讲的情景，或者你的球队即将赢得一场关键比赛时的感受。你心跳加速，手心冒汗，心脏在胸腔里砰砰直跳。你可能在上次看恐怖电影或露营时听到帐篷外传来奇怪声响时也有过类似的感觉。尽管你的理智告诉你并没有真正的危险，但你的身体却不这么认为。你的所有感官都变得异常敏锐，肌肉紧绷，对每一个声音、气味和动静都异常警觉。这是性兴奋。

What is physiological arousal? Think about the last time you gave a speech in front of a large audience. Or when your team was on the verge of winning a huge game. Your pulse raced, your palms sweated, and you could feel your heart pounding in your chest. You may have had similar feelings the last time you saw a scary movie or went camping and heard a weird noise outside your tent. Though your head kept saying you weren’t really in danger, your body was convinced otherwise. Every sense was heightened. Your muscles were tensed and you were alert to every sound, smell, and movement. This is arousal.

唤醒是一种激活和准备行动的状态。此时心跳加快，血压升高。从进化角度来看，它源于我们祖先的爬行动物大脑。生理唤醒会激发战斗或逃跑反应，帮助生物体捕食或逃离捕食者。

Arousal is a state of activation and readiness for action. The heart beats faster and blood pressure rises. Evolutionarily, it comes from our ancestors’ reptilian brains. Physiological arousal motivates a fight-or-flight response that helps organisms catch food or flee from predators.

我们不再需要追逐食物或担心被吃掉，但兴奋带来的刺激仍然促进着许多日常行为。兴奋时，我们会做各种各样的事情。我们会搓手、来回踱步。我们会挥舞拳头，在客厅里跑来跑去。兴奋点燃了激情。

We no longer have to chase our dinner or worry about being eaten, but the activation arousal provides still facilitates a host of everyday actions. When aroused we do things. We wring our hands and pace back and forth. We pump our fists in the air and run around the living room. Arousal kindles the fire.

有些情绪，比如愤怒和焦虑，会引发高度兴奋。当我们生气时，我们会对客服人员大吼大叫。当我们焦虑时，我们会反复检查各种事情。积极的情绪也会带来兴奋。以兴奋为例。当我们感到兴奋时，我们想要做点什么，而不是静静地坐着。敬畏也是如此。当我们被敬畏所感动时，我们会忍不住想要告诉别人发生了什么。

Some emotions, like anger and anxiety, are high-arousal. When we’re angry we yell at customer service representatives. When we’re anxious we check and recheck things. Positive emotions also generate arousal. Take excitement. When we feel excited we want to do something rather than sit still. The same is true for awe. When inspired by awe we can’t help wanting to tell people what happened.

然而，其他一些情绪则会产生相反的效果：它们会抑制行动。

Other emotions, however, have the opposite effect: they stifle action.

以悲伤为例。无论是经历痛苦的​​分手，还是失去心爱的宠物，悲伤的人往往会选择放松下来。他们会穿上舒适的衣服，蜷缩在沙发上，吃上一碗冰淇淋。满足感也会让人放松。当人们感到满足时，他们会放松身心。心跳减慢，血压降低。他们感到快乐，但却不想做任何事。想想你洗完热水澡或享受完放松的按摩后是什么感觉。你更有可能放松下来，静静地坐着，而不是立刻投入到另一项活动中。

Take sadness. Whether dealing with a tough breakup or the death of a beloved pet, sad people tend to power down. They put on some cozy clothes, curl up on the couch, and eat a bowl of ice cream. Contentment also deactivates. When people are content, they relax. Their heart rates slow, and their blood pressure decreases. They’re happy, but they don’t particularly feel like doing anything. Think of how you feel after a long hot shower or a relaxing massage. You’re more likely to relax and sit still than leap into another activity.

 

 

高度兴奋

HIGH AROUSAL

低唤醒度

LOW AROUSAL

积极的

POSITIVE

敬畏

Awe

激动

Excitement

娱乐（幽默）

Amusement (Humor)

满意

Contentment

消极的

NEGATIVE

愤怒

Anger

焦虑

Anxiety

悲伤

Sadness

当我们意识到情绪唤醒可能扮演的重要角色后，我们重新审视了数据。简单回顾一下，我们之前发现敬畏会增加分享行为，而悲伤会减少分享行为。但我们并没有发现积极情绪增加分享行为、消极情绪减少分享行为这种简单的规律，而是发现某些消极情绪，例如愤怒或焦虑，实际上会增加分享行为。生理唤醒会是解开这个谜题的关键吗？

Once we realized the important role that emotional arousal might play, we returned to our data. Just to recap, so far we had found that awe increased sharing and that sadness decreased it. But rather than finding a simple matter of positive emotions increasing sharing and negative emotions decreasing it, we found that some negative emotions, like anger or anxiety, actually increased sharing. Would physiological arousal be the key to the puzzle?

确实如此。

It was.

了解情绪唤醒有助于整合我们目前为止发现的不同结果。愤怒和焦虑会促使人们分享，因为它们和敬畏一样，都是高唤醒情绪。它们能点燃激情，激发人们的行动力，并促使他们采取行动。

Understanding arousal helps integrate the different results we had found so far. Anger and anxiety lead people to share because, like awe, they are high-arousal emotions. They kindle the fire, activate people, and drive them to take action.

刺激也是人们分享搞笑内容的原因之一。一些视频在YouTube上非常受欢迎，例如：一个孩子在牙医诊所麻醉后的反应（“David After Dentist”）、一个婴儿咬了他哥哥的手指（“Charlie Bit My Finger—Again!”），或者一只独角兽去糖果山玩，结果肾被偷了（“Charlie the Unicorn”）。这些视频加起来的观看次数超过了6亿次。

Arousal is also one reason funny things get shared. Videos about the aftereffects of a kid having anesthesia at the dentist (“David After Dentist”), a baby biting his brother’s finger (“Charlie Bit My Finger—Again!”), or a unicorn going to Candy Mountain and getting his kidney stolen (“Charlie the Unicorn”) are some of the most popular on YouTube. Taken together they have been viewed more than 600 million times.

虽然我们很容易认为这些内容走红仅仅是因为它们有趣，但实际上背后有一个更根本的原因。想想你上次听到一个特别搞笑的笑话，或者收到一段幽默视频，并忍不住想把它分享出去的时候。就像那些鼓舞人心的事物，或者那些让我们愤怒的事物一样，有趣的内容之所以被分享，是因为娱乐是一种高度兴奋的情绪。

But while it is tempting to say that these things went viral simply because they are funny, a more fundamental process is at work. Think about the last time you heard a really hilarious joke or were forwarded a humorous clip and felt compelled to pass it along. Just like inspiring things, or those that make us angry, funny content is shared because amusement is a high-arousal emotion.

然而，低唤醒度的情绪，例如悲伤，会减少分享。满足感也有同样的效果。满足感本身并不是一种糟糕的感觉。感到满足其实很不错。但人们不太愿意谈论或分享让他们感到满足的事情，因为满足感会降低唤醒度。

Low-arousal emotions, however, like sadness, decrease sharing. Contentment has the same effect. Contentment isn’t a bad feeling. Being content feels pretty good. But people are less likely to talk about or share things that make them content because contentment decreases arousal.

—————

—————

联合航空公司惨痛地领悟到，兴奋会驱使人们分享。戴夫·卡罗尔是个相当不错的音乐家。他的乐队“麦克斯韦之子”虽然算不上大红大紫，但靠专辑销售、巡演和周边产品赚的钱足以维持体面的生活。虽然没人会把戴夫的名字纹在手臂上，但他过得还不错。

United Airlines learned the hard way that arousal can drive people to share. Dave Carroll was a pretty good musician. His group, Sons of Maxwell, wasn’t a blockbuster act, but they made enough money from album sales, touring, and merchandising to pull together a decent living. People weren’t tattooing Dave’s name on their arms, but he was doing all right.

戴夫和他的乐队前往内布拉斯加州演出时，需要搭乘美联航经芝加哥转机。即使是小型随身行李，头顶行李架的空间也很难找到，而对于音乐家来说，情况就更加艰难了。戴夫的乐队吉他放不下，所以他们不得不把吉他和其他行李一起托运。

While traveling to a gig in Nebraska, Dave and his band had to take a connecting flight through Chicago with United Airlines.
