It’s hard enough to find overhead space for even a small carry-on, but musicians have it even tougher. Dave’s group couldn’t fit their guitars in the overhead, so they had to check them with the rest of their baggage.

但当他们即将从奥黑尔机场下飞机时，一位女士喊道：“我的天哪，他们把吉他扔出去了！”戴夫惊恐地看向窗外，正好看到行李搬运工粗暴地把他的宝贝乐器扔向空中。

But as they were about to deplane at O’Hare Airport, a woman cried out, “My god, they’re throwing guitars out there!” Dave looked out the window in horror just in time to see the baggage handlers roughly tossing his treasured instruments through the air.

他跳起来向乘务员求助，但无济于事。一位乘务员让他去找领班，但领班说这不是她的职责。另一位乘务员敷衍了他一番，让他抵达最终目的地后再去找登机口工作人员。

He jumped up and pleaded with the flight attendant for help, but to no avail. One flight attendant told him to talk to the lead agent, but that agent said it wasn’t her responsibility. Another employee gave him the run-around and told him to take up the matter with the gate agent when he landed in his final destination.

戴夫凌晨12点30分抵达奥马哈时，发现机场空无一人，一个工作人员都没看到。

When Dave landed in Omaha at 12:30 a.m., he found the airport deserted. No employees in sight.

戴夫走到行李提取处，小心翼翼地打开吉他盒。他最担心的事发生了。他那把价值3500美元的吉他被摔碎了。

Dave made his way to baggage claim and carefully opened his guitar case. His worst fears were confirmed. His $3,500 guitar had been smashed.

但这仅仅是戴夫故事的开始。接下来的九个月里，他一直在与联合航空公司谈判，争取某种赔偿。他提交了一份索赔申请，要求联合航空公司修理吉他，但是……美联航拒绝了他的请求。在列举的一长串理由中，美联航辩称，由于他错过了机票细则中规定的24小时索赔期限，因此无法提供帮助。

But that was only the start of Dave’s story. He spent the next nine months negotiating with United for some kind of compensation. He filed a claim asking United to fix the guitar, but it denied his request. Among a long list of justifications, United argued that it couldn’t help him because he had missed the brief twenty-four-hour window for claiming damages described in the small print of his ticket.

戴夫对自己受到的待遇感到愤怒，于是像任何一位优秀的音乐家一样，将自己的情绪表达出来：他写了一首歌。他描述了自己的经历，谱上了曲，并将其作为一段名为“美联航摔坏吉他”的短片上传到了YouTube上。

Furious with the way he’d been treated, Dave channeled his emotions the way any good musician would: he wrote a song about it. He described his experience, put it to music, and posted it as a short clip on YouTube entitled “United Breaks Guitars.”

视频上传后不到24小时，他就收到了近500条评论，其中大部分来自其他有类似遭遇的愤怒的美联航乘客。不到四天，视频的观看次数就超过了130万。十天之内，观看次数超过300万，评论超过1.4万条。2009年12月，《时代》杂志将“美联航摔坏吉他”列为2009年十大病毒视频之一。

Within twenty-four hours of uploading the video, he’d received almost 500 comments, most of them from other angry United customers who’d had similar experiences. In less than four days the video had more than 1.3 million views. Within ten days, more than 3 million views and 14,000 comments. In December 2009, Time magazine listed “United Breaks Guitars” as one of the Top 10 Viral Videos of 2009.

联合航空似乎几乎立即感受到了负面影响。视频发布后四天内，其股价下跌了10%，相当于1.8亿美元。尽管联合航空最终向塞隆尼斯·蒙克爵士乐学院捐赠了3000美元以示“善意”，但许多业内人士认为，此次事件给该公司造成了永久性损害。

United appears to have felt the negative effects almost immediately. Within four days of the video being posted, its stock price fell 10 percent—the equivalent of $180 million. Although United eventually donated $3,000 to the Thelonious Monk Institute of Jazz as a “gesture of goodwill,” many industry observers felt that it suffered permanent damage as a result of the incident.

关注感受

FOCUS ON FEELINGS

营销信息往往侧重于提供信息。公共卫生官员指出，如果青少年不吸烟或多吃蔬菜，他们的健康状况会好得多。人们认为，只要以清晰简洁的方式陈述事实，就能起到作用。受众会关注这些信息，权衡利弊，并采取相应的行动。

Marketing messages tend to focus on information. Public health officials note how much healthier teens will be if they don’t smoke or if they eat more vegetables. People think that if they just lay out the facts in a clear and concise way, it will tip the scales. Their audience will pay attention, weigh the information, and act accordingly.

但很多时候，信息本身并不足以解决问题。大多数青少年并不……有些人抽烟是因为觉得对身体好。而大多数狼吞虎咽吃下巨无霸汉堡、大份薯条，再来一杯超大可乐的人，并非对健康风险一无所知。所以，再多的信息恐怕也改变不了他们的行为。他们需要的是更有力的改变。

But many times information is not enough. Most teens don’t smoke because they think it’s good for them. And most people who scarf down a Big Mac and large fries and wash it down with a supersized Coke are not oblivious to the health risks. So additional information probably won’t get them to change their behavior. They need something more.

而这正是情感发挥作用的地方。与其纠结于特征或事实，我们更需要关注感受；关注那些促使人们采取行动的潜在情感。

And that is where emotion comes in. Rather than harping on features or facts, we need to focus on feelings; the underlying emotions that motivate people to action.

有些产品或理念似乎比其他产品或理念更容易引发情感共鸣。比起物流管理，人们似乎更容易对一家新潮的休闲场所感到兴奋。宠物和婴儿似乎比银行业务或非营利组织的财务策略更容易打动人心。

Some products or ideas may seem better suited than others for evoking emotion. It seems easier to get people excited about a new, hip lounge than logistics management. Pets and babies seem to lend themselves to emotional appeals more than banking or nonprofit financial strategy does.

但任何产品或服务都可以关注情感，即使那些本身没有任何明显情感吸引力的产品或服务。

But any product or service can focus on feelings, even those that don’t possess any obvious emotional hook.

以在线搜索引擎为例。搜索引擎似乎是你能想到的最不带情感的产品之一。人们想要的是最准确的搜索结果，而且速度越快越好。而这些结果背后，却是一团令人费解的技术：链接权重、索引和PageRank算法。很难让人对它产生热情或感动落泪，对吧？

Take online search engines. Search engines seem like one of the least emotional products you can think of. People want the most accurate search results in the least time possible. And underneath those results is a tangle of confusing technology: link weighting, indexing, and PageRank algorithms. A difficult product to get people fired up or teary eyed about, right?

谷歌正是通过其“巴黎之恋”活动做到了这一点。

Well, Google did exactly that with its “Parisian Love” campaign.

—————

—————

2009年，安东尼·卡法罗从纽约视觉艺术学院毕业时，并没有想到自己会成为谷歌的一员。此前，视觉艺术学院的毕业生中无人进入谷歌工作，而且谷歌一直被认为是技术人员的聚集地，而非设计师的天下。但当卡法罗得知谷歌正在招聘平面设计专业的毕业生时，他决定试一试。

When Anthony Cafaro graduated from New York’s School of Visual Arts in 2009, he wasn’t expecting to become a Googler. No one from Visual Arts had gone to work for Google before, and the company was known as a place for techies, not designers. But when Cafaro learned Google was interviewing graphic-design graduates, he thought he’d give it a shot.

面试过程非常愉快。到最后，面试官们与其说是考官，不如说是老朋友。卡法罗拒绝了众多传统广告公司的邀请，加入了谷歌新成立的设计团队——创意实验室。

The interview was a blast. By the end, the interviewers seemed less like examiners and more like old friends. Cafaro turned down a slew of offers from traditional ad agencies to join a newly formed Google design team called the Creative Lab.

然而几个月后，安东尼意识到创意实验室的方法与公司的整体理念并不完全契合。优秀的平面设计是触动人心的。它如同艺术，能够打动人心，唤起人们内心深处的情感。但谷歌关注的是数据分析，而非情感。

After a few months, though, Anthony realized that the Creative Lab’s approach wasn’t exactly in line with the company’s overall ethos. Great graphic design is visceral. Like art, it moves people and evokes their innermost feelings. But Google was about analytics, not emotion.

一个颇具启发性的故事是，一位设计师曾建议使用某种蓝色作为工具栏的颜色，理由是它视觉效果极佳。但产品经理却反对采用这种颜色，并要求设计师用量化数据来证明其选择的合理性。在谷歌，颜色不仅仅是颜色，它们背后是数学决策。

In a telling story, a designer once suggested using a certain shade of blue for the toolbar based on its visual appeal. But the product manager resisted using the color, asking the designer to justify that choice with quantitative research. At Google, colors aren’t just colors, they’re mathematical decisions.

卡法罗早期的一个项目也遇到了同样的问题。创意实验室受命创作内容，以突出谷歌新搜索界面的功能，例如查找航班、自动纠错和语言翻译。一个可能的解决方案是制作一个关于如何更好地搜索的简短教程，讲解各种功能的使用方法。另一个方案是“每日谷歌”，这是一个在线问答游戏，玩家需要利用搜索功能来解决复杂的谜题。

The same issues came up in one of Cafaro’s first projects. The Creative Lab was asked to create content to highlight the functionality of Google’s new search interface. Features like finding flights, autocorrect, and language translation. One potential solution was a little tutorial on how to search better. A how-to of the different functions. Another was “A Google a Day,” an online trivia game that involved using search features to solve complex puzzles.

卡法罗喜欢这两个想法，但总觉得还缺少些什么。情感。

Cafaro liked both ideas but felt something was missing. Emotion.

谷歌拥有出色的界面和实用的搜索结果，但界面本身并不能让人发笑，也不能让人落泪。演示可以展示界面的工作原理，但也仅此而已。卡法罗想要赋予界面人性化的特征。他不仅想展示功能，更想打动人心，建立情感联系。

Google had a great interface and useful search results, but an interface doesn’t make you laugh. An interface doesn’t make you cry. A demo would show how the interface worked, but that would be it. Cafaro wanted to humanize the interface. He wanted not only to show features, but to move people. Build an emotional connection.

因此，Cafaro 与 Creative Lab 团队一起开发了一款视频标题为“巴黎之恋”。这段短片讲述了一个萌芽的爱情故事，运用了随着时间推移而不断变化的谷歌搜索记录。片中没有人物画面，甚至没有声音——只有搜索栏中输入的词语和出现的搜索结果。

So together with the Creative Lab team, Cafaro developed a video entitled “Parisian Love.” The clip tells a budding love story, using Google searches that evolve over time. No images of people, or even voices—just the phrases entered in the search bar and the results that emerge.

故事从一位男生搜索“法国巴黎留学”开始，他点击搜索结果排名靠前的一个链接了解更多信息。之后，他又搜索“卢浮宫附近的咖啡馆”，浏览网页寻找自己喜欢的。这时，背景中传来一个女生的笑声，因为他接下来输入的是“translate tu es très mignon”，他很快发现这句话的法语意思是“你很可爱”。紧接着，他又开始搜索“如何打动法国女孩”，阅读了各种建议，最后还搜索了巴黎的巧克力店。

It starts when a guy enters “study abroad Paris France” and clicks on one of the top search results to learn more. Later he searches for “cafés near the Louvre,” and scans to find one he thinks he’ll like. You hear a female laugh in the background as his next entry is “translate tu es très mignon,” which he soon learns is French for “you are very cute.” Quickly he then seeks advice on how to “impress a French girl,” reads up on the suggestions, and searches for chocolate shops in Paris.

随着剧情展开，音乐也逐渐增强。我们跟随这位寻觅者，见证他从寻求异地恋建议到在巴黎找工作的转变。我们看到他追踪飞机的降落时间，然后搜索巴黎的教堂（背景中响起教堂的钟声）。最后，当音乐达到高潮时，我们看到他询问如何组装婴儿床。视频以一句简单的信息结束：“继续寻找。”

The music builds as the plot unfolds. We follow the searcher as he transitions from seeking long-distance relationship advice to job hunting in Paris. We see him tracking a plane’s landing time and then searching for Paris churches (to the accompaniment of church bells in the background). Finally, as the music crescendos, we see him asking how to assemble a crib. The video ends with a simple message. “Search on.”

这段视频绝对会让你心潮澎湃。它浪漫、欢乐、鼓舞人心，各种情绪交织在一起。每次看，我依然会感到激动不已，尽管我已经看过几十遍了。

You cannot watch this clip without having your heartstrings tugged. It’s romantic, joyous, and inspiring all at once. I still feel tingles every time I see it, and I’ve watched it dozens of times.

当创意实验室向谷歌搜索营销团队展示这段视频时，所有人都赞不绝口。谷歌CEO的妻子也很喜欢。每个人都想把它分享出去。事实上，这段视频在内部反响如此之好，以至于谷歌决定将其公之于众。通过聚焦情感，谷歌将一条普通的广告变成了病毒式传播的爆款。

When the Creative Lab presented the clip to the Google Search marketing team, everyone loved it. Google’s CEO’s wife loved it. Everyone wanted to pass it on. In fact, the clip did so well internally that Google decided to release it to the larger public. By focusing on feelings, Google turned a normal ad into a viral hit.

—————

—————

无需聘请昂贵的广告公司或花费数百万美元进行焦点小组访谈，就能引发人们的情感共鸣。卡法罗制作了这段视频。卡法罗的团队与另外四名来自全国各地设计专业的学生一起，并没有仅仅着重介绍最新的炫酷功能，而是唤起了人们对谷歌搜索的喜爱之处。正如一位创意实验室团队成员所说：“最好的结果不会出现在搜索引擎中，而是在人们的生活中。” 说得好。

It doesn’t require a costly ad agency or millions of dollars in focus groups to get people to feel emotion. Cafaro created the clip with four other students who had been brought in from design programs across the country. Rather than simply highlighting the latest gee-whiz feature, Cafaro’s team reminded people what they love about Google Search. As one Creative Lab team member put it, “The best results don’t show up in a search engine, they show up in people’s lives.” Well said.

在他们精彩的著作《让创意更有粘性》（Made to Stick）中，奇普·希思和丹·希思探讨了如何运用“三个为什么”来挖掘一个想法的情感核心。写下你认为人们做某件事的原因。然后，连续问自己三次“为什么这件事很重要？”。每次这样做，都记下你的答案，你会发现，你越深入挖掘，不仅能找到想法的核心，还能发现其背后的情感。

In their wonderful book Made to Stick, Chip and Dan Heath talk about using the “Three Whys” to find the emotional core of an idea. Write down why you think people are doing something. Then ask “Why is this important?” three times. Each time you do this, note your answer, and you’ll notice that you drill down further and further toward uncovering not only the core of an idea, but the emotion behind it.

以网络搜索为例。为什么搜索很重要？因为人们希望快速找到信息。

Take online search. Why is search important? Because people want to find information quickly.

他们为什么要这样做？为了找到他们想要的答案。

Why do they want to do that? So they can get answers to what they are looking for.

他们为什么想要这些答案？为了与人建立联系，实现目标，圆梦。这听起来越来越感性了。

Why do they want those answers? So they can connect with people, achieve their goals, and fulfill their dreams. Now that’s starting to get more emotional.

想让人们谈论全球变暖并积极行动起来改变现状吗？不要只是指出问题的严重性或列举关键数据。要想办法让他们真正关心这个问题。谈谈北极熊的死亡，或者他们孩子的健康将受到怎样的影响。

Want people to talk about global warming and rally to change it? Don’t just point out how big the problem is or list key statistics. Figure out how to make them care. Talk about polar bears dying or how their children’s health will be affected.

用高情绪点燃激情

KINDLING THE FIRE WITH HIGH-AROUSAL EMOTIONS

当试图利用情感来推动分享时，记住要选择能够点燃激情的情感：选择能够激发人们行动的高唤醒情感。

When trying to use emotions to drive sharing, remember to pick ones that kindle the fire: select high-arousal emotions that drive people to action.

从积极的角度来看，通过展示来激发人们的兴趣或鼓舞他们。让他们明白自己能做出什么贡献。如果是负面事件，要激起他们的愤怒，而不是悲伤。一定要让北极熊的故事激起他们的热情。

On the positive side, excite people or inspire them by showing them how they can make a difference. On the negative side, make people mad, not sad. Make sure the polar bear story gets them fired up.

仅仅增加故事或广告的煽动性就能显著影响人们的分享意愿。在一项实验中，我们修改了故事的细节，使其更能激起人们的愤怒。在另一项实验中，我们让广告变得更有趣。

Simply adding more arousal to a story or ad can have a big impact on people’s willingness to share it. In one experiment we changed the details of a story to make it evoke more anger. In another experiment, we made an ad funnier.

两种情况下，结果都一样。愤怒或幽默感越强烈，分享量就越高。这些情绪的加入增强了故事或广告所引发的情感唤起程度，从而促进了传播。

In both cases, the results were the same. More anger or more humor led to more sharing. Adding these emotions boosted transmission by boosting the amount of arousal the story or ad evoked.

负面情绪也能促使人们谈论和分享。营销信息通常会力求以最积极的方式描绘产品和理念。从剃须刀到冰箱，广告中常见的都是面带微笑的顾客，他们热情地赞扬使用产品带来的种种好处。营销人员往往会刻意回避负面情绪，唯恐它们会损害品牌形象。

Negative emotions can also drive people to talk and share. Marketing messages usually try to paint products and ideas in the most positive light possible. From razors to refrigerators, ads typically show smiling customers who extol the benefits they derive from using the product. Marketers tend to avoid negative emotions out of fear they could taint the brand.

但如果运用得当，负面情绪实际上可以促进口碑传播。

But if used correctly, negative emotions can actually boost word of mouth.

宝马在2001年的一次营销活动中巧妙地点燃了人们的热情。这家德国汽车公司制作了一系列名为《雇佣》（The Hire）的网络短片。这些短片并非常见的温馨广告，展现宝马汽车行驶在风景如画的乡村道路上，而是充斥着绑架、FBI突袭和生死攸关的惊险场面。尽管这些短片引发的恐惧和焦虑远非积极正面的，但它们却极大地激发了观众的兴奋感，使得该系列短片在短短四个月内就获得了超过1100万的点击量。同期，宝马的销量增长了12%。

BMW kindled the fire beautifully in a 2001 campaign. The German automobile company created a series of short Internet films entitled The Hire. Rather than being typical feel-good commercials showing BMWs driving down various idyllic country roads, the movies were riddled with kidnappings, FBI raids, and near-death experiences. While the fear and anxiety they evoked were far from positive, the clips so highly aroused viewers that the series racked up more than 11 million views within four months. Over the same period, BMW sales increased 12 percent.

或者想想公共卫生宣传。当你试图让人们意识到吸烟会导致肺癌，或者……时，往往很难用积极的方式来表达。肥胖会使预期寿命缩短三年以上。但某些类型的负面影响……情感诉求比其他方式更能有效地促使人们传播信息。

Or consider public health messages. It’s often hard to put a positive spin on things when you’re trying to get people to realize that smoking causes lung cancer, or that obesity reduces life expectancy by more than three years. But certain types of negative emotional appeals should be more effective in getting people to spread the word than others.

回想一下我们在“触发因素”章节中提到的“男子饮脂”公益广告。一大坨白色脂肪糊在盘子里？真恶心！但是因为厌恶是一种高度激发情绪的情绪，它促使人们讨论和分享公益广告。设计能引发人们焦虑或厌恶（高激发度）而非悲伤（低激发度）的信息，将更有利于信息的传播。负面情绪如果运用得当，可以成为推动讨论的强大动力。

Think back to the “Man Drinks Fat” public service announcement we talked about in the Triggers chapter. A huge glob of white fat plopping down on a plate? Gross! But because disgust is a highly arousing emotion, it encouraged people to talk about and share the PSA. Designing messages that make people anxious or disgusted (high arousal) rather than sad (low arousal) will boost transmission. Negative emotions, when used correctly, can be a powerful driver of discussion.

这就引出了背巾育儿的话题。

And that brings us to babywearing.

婴儿背带、抵制活动和消除负面舆论

BABYWEARING, BOYCOTTS, AND BLUNTING BAD BUZZ

2008年诞生了许多“第一”。中国首次举办奥运会，美国迎来了首位非裔美国人当选总统，还有一个你可能不知道的“第一”——首届国际背巾育儿周。

The year 2008 had many firsts. The first time China hosted the Olympics, the first African American elected president of the United States, and one that you might not have been aware of. The inaugural celebration of International Babywearing Week.

用背带或类似背巾背着婴儿的做法已经存在数千年了​​。一些专家甚至认为，这种做法能加强母子间的亲密关系，改善婴儿和母亲的健康。但随着婴儿车和其他育儿用品的普及，许多父母放弃了这种做法。因此，在2008年，举办了一场庆祝活动，旨在提高人们对背巾育儿的认识，并鼓励世界各地的人们重新思考背巾育儿的好处。

The practice of carrying your baby in a sling or similar carrier has been around for thousands of years. Some experts have even argued that the practice strengthens the maternal bond, improving the health of the baby and the mother. But as strollers and other gadgets have been popularized, many parents have moved away from this practice. So in 2008, a celebration was held to raise awareness and encourage people around the world to reconsider the benefits of babywearing.

生产止痛药美林（Motrin）的麦克尼尔消费者保健公司（McNeil Consumer Healthcare）将这股热潮视为绝佳的商机。美林当时的口号是“我们感同身受”。因此，为了表达对母亲们的支持，该公司制作了一则广告，重点讲述母亲们在怀孕期间可能遭受的各种疼痛和不适。她们用背带背着宝宝。广告指出，虽然背巾背着宝宝对宝宝很好，但会给妈妈的背部、颈部和肩膀带来很大的压力。

McNeil Consumer Healthcare, the maker of painkiller Motrin, saw this swell of interest as a perfect opportunity. Motrin’s motto at the time was “We feel your pain.” So in an attempt to show solidarity with mothers, the company created an ad centered on the aches and pains mothers can suffer from carrying their babies in slings. The ad noted that while babywearing can be great for the baby, it can put a ton of strain on the back, neck, and shoulders of the mom.

这家公司原本是想表达支持，想表明他们理解妈妈们的痛苦，并表示Motrin可以提供帮助。但一些所谓的“妈妈博主”却有不同的看法。广告中，一位妈妈的旁白说道：“背着宝宝让我看起来完全像个正式的妈妈。所以，如果我看起来很疲惫、很疯狂，大家就会理解了。”

The company was trying to be supportive. It wanted to show that it understood mom’s pain and Motrin was there to help. But a number of so-called mommy bloggers saw things differently. The mom’s voice-over in the ad said babywearing “totally makes me look like an official mom. And so if I look tired and crazy, people will understand why.”

母亲们感到两方面的极大冒犯——一方面是她们被暗示把孩子当成时尚单品穿在身上，另一方面是她们看起来很奇怪——于是她们纷纷在博客和推特上发泄不满。愤怒的情绪迅速蔓延开来。

Deeply offended on two fronts—by the implication that they wore their babies as fashion statements and that they looked crazy—mothers took to their blogs and Twitter accounts. The anger spread.

很快，成千上万的人参与进来。“婴儿永远不会成为时尚单品。这种想法简直荒谬至极！”有人喊道。帖子数量激增。许多作者表示他们将抵制这家公司。这个话题开始在推特上流行起来，《纽约时报》、《广告时代》和其他众多媒体也纷纷报道了这场运动。很快，谷歌上搜索“Motrin”和“头痛”的前十名中，有七个都指向了这家公司。营销灾难。

Soon thousands of people were involved. “A baby will never be a fashion statement. How outrageous is that thinking!” one cried. The posts multiplied. Many of the writers said they would boycott the company. The topic started to trend on Twitter, and the movement got picked up by The New York Times, Ad Age, and a host of other media outlets. Soon seven out of the top ten searches for “Motrin” and “headache” on Google referred to the marketing debacle.

最终，在拖延了太久之后，Motrin 从其网站上撤下了广告，并发表了一份冗长的道歉声明。

Finally, after too long a delay, Motrin took the advertisement down from its website and issued a lengthy apology.

—————

—————

科技使人们更容易为了共同的兴趣或目标而组织起来。社交媒体让人们能够快速便捷地联系，使志同道合的人能够找到彼此、分享信息并协调行动计划。

Technology has made it easier for people to organize behind a common interest or goal. By allowing people to connect quickly and easily, social media enable like-minded individuals to find one another, share information, and coordinate plans of action.

当人们居住地相距甚远，或者正在处理棘手问题时，这些技术尤其有用。政治或社会意义。许多人认为社交媒体是阿拉伯之春的催化剂，这场席卷阿拉伯世界的反政府抗议浪潮最终推翻了包括突尼斯和埃及在内的多个国家的政府。

These technologies are particularly useful when people either live far apart or are dealing with an issue that has delicate political or social meaning. Many people point to social media as the catalyst behind the Arab Spring, the wave of antigovernment protests that broke out across the Arab world, eventually toppling the governments of Tunisia and Egypt, among others.

这些新兴的社会运动中，有些是积极的。例如，它们使公民能够奋起反抗独裁统治，或者帮助遭受骚扰的青少年认识到生活会变得更好。

Some of these burgeoning social movements are positive. Enabling citizens to rise up against dictatorships or helping teens facing harassment to realize that life gets better.

但在其他情况下，评论和行动则具有负面性质。虚假谣言可能会开始传播，恶毒的流言蜚语可能会四处流传并愈演愈烈。我们能否预测哪些冲突会止步于孤立的评论，哪些会像滚雪球一样越滚越大？

But in other cases the comments and movements are negative in nature. False rumors may start to gain traction. Vicious gossip may circulate and build. Is it possible to predict which flare-ups will remain isolated comments and which will snowball?

部分答案在于生理唤醒。某些类型的负面情绪更容易升级，因为它们会引发生理唤醒，从而更容易传播开来。例如，对糟糕客户服务的愤怒抨击，或者关于新医保计划可能削减福利的焦虑谣言，都比表达悲伤或失望的情绪更容易传播。

Part of the answer comes back to physiological arousal. Certain types of negativity may be more likely to escalate because they evoke arousal and are thus more likely to go viral. Angry tirades about bad customer service, or anxious rumors about how a new health plan may take away benefits, should be more likely to circulate than expressions of sadness or disappointment.

因此，教师和校长们应该格外警惕那些煽动性强、容易传播的谣言，因为它们更容易扩散。同样，Motrin的生产商本可以通过监控网络舆论来阻止抵制活动的发生。通过查看人们的帖子、推文或状态更新中是否存在“生气”、“愤怒”或“恼火”之类的词语，该公司本可以在愤怒情绪蔓延之前就安抚不满的顾客。及早处理这些高情绪可以有效缓解负面影响，防止其像滚雪球一样越滚越大。

So teachers and principals should be particularly wary of hurtful rumors that carry an arousing punch because they are more likely to get passed around. Similarly, Motrin’s maker could have stemmed the boycott before it started by monitoring online chatter. By looking for words like “pissed off,” “angry,” or “mad” in people’s posts, tweets, or status updates the company could have addressed unsatisfied customers before the anger built. Fixing these high-arousal emotions early can mitigate the negativity before it snowballs.

运动使人们乐于分享

EXERCISE MAKES PEOPLE SHARE

我们的情感之旅还有最后一站。

Our emotional odyssey has one last stop.

在沃顿商学院，我们有一个行为实验室，人们在那里工作并获得报酬。进行各种心理学和市场营销实验。这些任务通常包括在线调查中点击方框或在纸上圈出项目。

At Wharton, we have a behavioral lab where people are paid to do various psychology and marketing experiments. These tasks often involve clicking boxes in an online survey or circling items on a sheet of paper.

但几年前的某个十一月，当人们来参加我的一项实验时，实验说明却有些不同寻常。

But when people came in for an experiment of mine one November a few years ago, the instructions were a bit more unusual.

一半的参与者被要求在椅子上静坐60秒，放松身心。这很简单。

Half the participants were asked to sit still in their chairs for sixty seconds and relax. Easy enough.

另一半受试者则被要求原地慢跑一分钟。无论他们穿的是运动鞋还是平底鞋，牛仔裤还是休闲裤，都被要求在实验室中央原地跑步六十秒。

The other half, however, were asked to jog lightly in place for a minute. Regardless of whether they were wearing sneakers or pumps, jeans or slacks, they were asked to run in place for sixty seconds in the middle of the laboratory.

好的，当然可以。我想也是。当我们提出要求时，一些参与者一脸疑惑，但最终都同意了。

Okay. Sure. I guess. Some participants gave us a puzzled look when we made the request, but all complied.

实验结束后，他们又参与了一个看似无关的实验。实验人员告诉他们，他们对人们与他人分享的内容很感兴趣，并给他们提供了一篇近期刊登在校报上的文章。读完之后，他们可以选择将文章通过电子邮件发送给任何他们想分享的人。

After they were done, they participated in what seemed like a second, unrelated experiment. They were told the experimenters were interested in what people share with others and were given a recent article from the school newspaper. Then, after reading it, they were given the option of e-mailing it to anyone they liked.

事实上，这项“无关研究”是我最初实验的一部分。我想验证一个简单却引人入胜的假设。当时我们知道，能唤起情感的内容或经历更容易被分享。但我很好奇，这种唤起效应是否可能更为广泛。如果唤起会促使分享，那么任何生理上的唤起体验是否都会驱使人们与他人分享故事和信息呢？

In actuality, this “unrelated study” was part of my initial experiment. I wanted to test a simple but intriguing hypothesis. At this point we knew that emotionally arousing content or experiences would be more likely to be shared. But I wondered whether the effects of arousal might be even broader than that. If arousal induces sharing, then might any physiologically arousing experience drive people to share stories and information with others?

原地跑步提供了一个完美的测试。跑步本身不会引发情绪，但它在生理上同样具有刺激作用。它能加快心率、升高血压等等。因此，如果任何形式的刺激都能促进分享，那么原地跑步应该会促使人们与他人分享信息。即使人们分享的内容并不相同。关于或分享的内容与他们产生性兴奋的原因无关。

Running in place provided the perfect test. Running doesn’t evoke emotion, but it is just as physiologically arousing. It gets your heart rate up, increases blood pressure, etc. So if arousal of any sort boosts sharing, then running in place should lead people to share things with others. Even if the things people are talking about or sharing have nothing to do with the reason they are experiencing arousal.

事实证明确实如此。在被要求慢跑的学生中，75%的人分享了这篇文章——这一比例是“放松”组学生的两倍多。由此可见，任何形式的唤醒，无论是情绪上的还是生理上的，甚至是情境本身（而非内容）引起的唤醒，都能促进信息的传播。

And it did. Among students who had been instructed to jog, 75 percent shared the article—more than twice as many as the students who had been in the “relaxed” group. Thus any sort of arousal, whether from emotional or physical sources, and even arousal due to the situation itself (rather than content), can boost transmission.

—————

—————

了解刺激情境会驱使人们分享信息，有助于我们理解所谓的过度分享——即人们透露了超出自身意愿的信息。你是否曾在飞机上遇到过旁边的人不停地分享看似极其私密的细节？或者在谈话中，事后意识到自己可能透露了远超预期的信息？为什么会发生这种情况？

Understanding that arousing situations can drive people to pass things on helps shed light on so-called oversharing, when people disclose more than they should. Ever been stuck next to someone on a plane who won’t stop sharing what seem like extremely personal details? Or find yourself in a conversation where later on you realize that you may have shared way more than you meant to? Why does this happen?

当然，我们可能比预想中更自在地与某人相处，或者可能喝多了玛格丽塔酒。但还有第三个原因。如果情境因素最终导致我们生理上被唤起，我们最终可能会分享比计划中更多的信息。

Sure, we may feel more comfortable with someone than we thought we would or we may have had one too many margaritas. But there is also a third reason. If situational factors end up making us physiologically aroused, we may end up sharing more than we planned.

所以，下次当你从跑步机上下来、险些遭遇车祸或经历颠簸的飞行时，一定要小心。因为这些经历让你兴奋，事后你可能会过度向他人透露信息。

So be careful the next time you step off the treadmill, barely avoid a car accident, or experience a turbulent plane ride. Because you’ve been aroused by these experiences, you may overshare information with others in the aftermath.

这些观点还表明，口碑传播的一种方法是找到那些情绪高涨的人群。像《交易还是不交易》（Deal or No Deal）这样激动人心的游戏节目，或者像《犯罪现场调查》（CSI）这样令人焦虑的犯罪剧，比关于历史人物的纪录片更容易激发人们的兴趣。这些节目应该能够……当然，运动本身会引发更多闲聊，但运动带来的心率提升也会让人们更愿意谈论休息期间播放的广告。健身房里的广告之所以能引发热烈讨论，仅仅是因为人们当时已经非常兴奋。工作团队一起散步或许也能从中受益，因为这会鼓励大家分享想法和观点。

These ideas also suggest that one way to generate word of mouth is to find people when they are already fired up. Exciting game shows like Deal or No Deal or anxiety-inducing crime dramas like CSI are more likely to get people aroused than documentaries about historical figures. These shows should get more chatter themselves, sure, but the boosted heart rate they induce should also spill over and make people more likely to talk about the commercials that appear during the break. Ads at the gym may provoke lots of discussion simply because people are already so amped. Work groups may benefit from taking walks together because it will encourage people to share their ideas and opinions.

同样的道理也适用于网络内容。某些网站、新闻文章或YouTube视频比其他内容更能激发用户的兴奋感。关于金融市场的博客、关于政治裙带关系的文章以及搞笑视频都可能提升用户的活跃度，而活跃度的提升反过来又会增加这些页面上出现的广告或其他内容的传播。

The same idea holds for online content. Certain websites, news articles, or YouTube videos evoke more arousal than others. Blogs about financial markets, articles about political cronyism, and hilarious videos are all likely to boost activation, which, in turn, should increase the transmission of ads or other content that appears on those pages.

广告投放时机也很重要。虽然一档节目整体上可能具有一定的刺激性，但其中某些特定场景的刺激效果可能更胜一筹。例如，在犯罪剧中，焦虑感往往在剧情中段达到顶峰。当案件最终告破时，所有紧张感都会消散。在游戏节目中，当参赛者即将得知自己赢得多少奖金时，兴奋感——以及由此而来的刺激感——达到最高点。我们或许会更多地讨论那些出现在这些激动人心时刻附近的广告。

Ad timing also matters. Although a show may be generally arousing, a specific scene in that show may be more activating than others. In crime shows, for example, the anxiety often peaks somewhere in the middle. When the crime is solved at the end, all tension dissipates. In game shows, excitement—and therefore arousal—is highest when contestants are about to find out how much they’ve won. We may end up talking more about ads that show up close to these exciting moments.

—————

—————

情感驱使人们采取行动。它们让我们欢笑、呐喊、哭泣，也让我们交谈、分享和购买。因此，与其引用统计数据或提供信息，我们更需要关注感受。正如曾参与谷歌“巴黎之恋”视频开发的安东尼·卡法罗所指出的那样：

Emotions drive people to action. They make us laugh, shout, and cry, and they make us talk, share, and buy. So rather than quoting statistics or providing information, we need to focus on feelings. As Anthony Cafaro, the designer who helped develop the “Parisian Love” video at Google, noted:

无论是像谷歌这样的数字产品，还是像运动鞋这样的实体产品，你都应该创造出能够打动人心的产品。 他们不想感觉自己被灌输什么——他们想要娱乐，他们想要被感动。

Whether it’s a digital product, like Google, or a physical product, like sneakers, you should make something that will move people. People don’t want to feel like they’re being told something—they want to be entertained, they want to be moved.

有些情绪比其他情绪更能激发信息传播。正如我们之前讨论的，激发情绪是信息传递的关键。生理唤醒或激活会驱使人们交谈和分享。我们需要让人们兴奋或开怀大笑。我们需要让他们愤怒而不是悲伤。即使在人们积极参与的场合，也能让他们更愿意将信息传递给他人。

Some emotions kindle the fire more than others. As we discussed, activating emotion is the key to transmission. Physiological arousal or activation drives people to talk and share. We need to get people excited or make them laugh. We need to make them angry rather than sad. Even situations where people are active can make them more likely to pass things on to others.

流体动力学和网络搜索似乎是两个最枯燥乏味的话题。但丹尼斯·格雷迪和安东尼·卡法罗将这些抽象的概念与人们的生活联系起来，唤起人们内心深处的情感，让我们开始关注并分享。

Fluid dynamics and online search seem like two of the least moving topics out there. But by relating these abstract topics to people’s own lives and evoking underlying emotion, Denise Grady and Anthony Cafaro got us to care, and share.

4.公众

4. Public

肯·西格尔是史蒂夫·乔布斯的得力助手。他曾在乔布斯的广告公司担任创意总监长达十二年。上世纪八十年代初，他开始负责苹果公司的广告业务。乔布斯被解雇并创办NeXT电脑公司后，肯也加入了NeXT。1997年乔布斯重返苹果公司时，肯也随之加入。他参与了“非同凡想”（Think Different）广告活动的策划，是“疯狂的一代”（Crazy Ones）广告的创意团队成员之一，并因将苹果那款外形圆润、酷似鸡蛋的一体式台式电脑命名为iMac而掀起了一股“i热潮”。

Ken Segall was Steve Jobs’s right hand man. For twelve years, Ken worked as creative director at Jobs’s ad agency. He started with Apple’s account in the early 1980s. When Jobs was fired and started NeXT Computer, Ken moved to be part of the project. And when Jobs returned to Apple in 1997, Ken came along as well. Ken worked on the “Think Different” campaign, was on the team that developed the “Crazy Ones” ad, and started the iCraze by naming Apple’s bulbous all-in-one egg-looking desktop the iMac.

在那些后期岁月里，肯的团队每两周都会和乔布斯坐下来开一次会。这有点像是一次进度汇报会。肯的团队会分享他们在广告方面的工作进展：有前景的想法、新的文案和潜在的版面设计。乔布斯也会这样做。他会向肯的团队汇报苹果公司的运营情况，哪些产品畅销，以及是否有任何即将推出的新产品需要相应的广告宣传。

During those later years, Ken’s team would sit down with Jobs every two weeks. It was a status meeting of sorts. Ken’s team would share everything they were working on advertisingwise: promising ideas, new copy, and potential layouts. Jobs would do the same. He would update Ken’s team on how Apple was doing, which products were selling, and whether anything new was coming down the pipeline that they might need a campaign for.

有一周，乔布斯带着一个难题找到了肯的团队。乔布斯痴迷于打造极致的用户体验。他始终把客户放在第一位。客户们为此慷慨解囊。他们花了那么多钱，理应得到应有的待遇。因此，苹果将这一理念贯穿于产品设计的方方面面，从开箱到拨打技术支持电话，无一例外。你有没有注意到，当你第一次打开新iPhone的包装盒时，会感觉有一段缓慢的延迟？那是因为苹果一直在努力设计这种体验，力求带来奢华而厚重的完美感受。

One week, Jobs approached Ken’s team with a conundrum. Jobs was obsessed with the absolute best possible user experience. He always put the customer first. Customers shelled out all that money; they should be treated right. So Apple carried this mantra into all aspects of product design. From opening the box to calling for tech support. Ever notice the slow delay when you first pull the cover off the box of your new iPhone? That’s because Apple has been hard at work designing that experience to provide the perfect feeling of luxury and heft.

难题在于新款 PowerBook G4 的设计。这款笔记本电脑本应是科技与设计的奇迹。其钛金属机身堪称革命性创新——比钢更坚固，比铝更轻。而且，厚度不足一英寸，它将成为有史以来最薄的笔记本电脑之一。

The conundrum concerned the design of the new PowerBook G4. The laptop was going to be a marvel of technology and design. Its titanium body was revolutionary—stronger than steel yet lighter than aluminum. And, at less than one inch thick, it would be one of the thinnest laptops ever.

但乔布斯并不关心笔记本电脑的强度或重量，他关心的是logo的方向。

But Jobs wasn’t concerned about the laptop’s strength or weight. He was concerned about the direction of the logo.

PowerBook 笔记本电脑的顶盖上总是印着一个侧面被咬了一口的苹果。秉承以用户为中心的理念，苹果希望电脑用户能够直观地看到这个标志。考虑到笔记本电脑频繁的开合，这一点尤为重要。人们常常把笔记本电脑塞进背包或手提包里，之后才拿出来开始工作。而当你拿出笔记本电脑时，很难分辨哪一面朝上。哪一面有锁扣，也就是当你把笔记本电脑放在桌子上时应该朝向自己的一面？

The cover of PowerBook laptops always had a small apple with a bite taken out of the side. Consistent with their user focus, Apple wanted the logo to look right to the owner of the computer. This was particularly important given the frequency with which laptops are opened and closed. People stuff the laptops in their backpacks or bags only to pull them out later and start working. And when you pull the laptop out it’s hard to know which way is up. Which side has the latch and so should face toward you when you set the laptop down on a desk or table?

乔布斯希望这种体验尽可能流畅，所以他把logo设计成指南针的形状。电脑合上时，logo朝向用户，这样用户放下笔记本电脑时就能轻松辨别方向。

Jobs wanted this experience to be as fluid as possible, so he used the logo as a compass. It faced the user when the computer was closed so that the user could easily orient the laptop when he set it down.

但问题出在用户打开笔记本电脑的时候。用户在咖啡馆找到座位，点上玛奇朵后，打开电脑准备工作。然而，一旦打开笔记本电脑，logo就会翻转。在周围所有人看来，logo都是倒过来的。

But the problem came when a person opened the laptop. Once the users had found a seat at the coffee shop and sat down with their macchiato, they would open their computer to start working. And once they opened the laptop the logo would flip. To everyone around them the logo would be upside down.

乔布斯非常重视品牌建设，看到那些倒过来的标志让他感觉很糟糕。他甚至担心这会损害品牌形象。

Jobs was a big believer in branding, and seeing all those upside-down logos wasn’t a great feeling. He was even worried it might be hurting the brand.

于是乔布斯问肯的团队一个问题：哪个更重要——是让顾客在打开PowerBook之前就能正确看到logo，还是让笔记本电脑在使用过程中，logo在其他人看来都正确？

So Jobs asked Ken’s team a question. Which is more important—to have the logo look right to the customers before they opened their PowerBook, or to make it look right to the rest of the world when the laptop was in use?

—————

—————

下次你看到苹果笔记本电脑时就会发现，肯·乔布斯和乔布斯颠覆了他们长期以来的信念，把苹果的标志翻转了过来。原因何在？可观察性。乔布斯意识到，看到别人做某件事，会促使人们更愿意效仿。

As you can see the next time you glance at an Apple laptop, Ken and Jobs reversed their long-held beliefs and flipped the logo. The reason? Observability. Jobs realized that seeing others do something makes people more likely to do it themselves.

但关键在于“看见”。如果很难看到别人在做什么，就很难模仿。让事物更容易被观察，就更容易模仿。因此，推动产品流行起来的关键因素之一是公众的可见性。如果某件事物是为了展示而设计的，那么它就更容易发展壮大。

But the key word here is “seeing.” If it’s hard to see what others are doing, it’s hard to imitate it. Making something more observable makes it easier to imitate. Thus a key factor in driving products to catch on is public visibility. If something is built to show, it’s built to grow.

模仿心理学

THE PSYCHOLOGY OF IMITATION

想象一下，你身处一座陌生的城市。你出差或和朋友度假，终于抵达目的地，办理入住手续，快速冲了个澡后，你已经饥肠辘辘。该吃晚饭了。

Imagine you’re in an unfamiliar city. You’re out of town on a business trip or vacationing with a friend and by the time you finally land, check into the hotel, and take a quick shower you’re famished. It’s time for dinner.

你想去个好地方，但对这座城市不太熟悉。酒店礼宾部很忙，你也不想花太多时间在网上看评论，所以决定就在附近找个地方住。

You want to go somewhere good, but you don’t know the city that well. The concierge is busy and you don’t want to spend a lot of time reading reviews on the Internet, so you decide to just find a place nearby.

但当你踏上熙熙攘攘的街道，映入眼帘的是琳琅满目的选择。一家可爱的泰国餐厅，有着紫色的招牌。遮阳篷。一家时髦的西班牙小吃酒吧。一家意大利小酒馆。你该如何选择？

But when you step out onto the bustling street you’re struck by dozens of options. A cute Thai place with a purple awning. A hip-looking tapas bar. An Italian bistro. How do you choose?

如果你和大多数人一样，你可能会遵循一条久经考验的经验法则：找一家坐满了人的餐厅。如果很多人在那里吃饭，那这家餐厅可能不错。如果一家餐厅空无一人，你最好还是继续往前走。

If you’re like most people you’d probably follow a time-tested rule of thumb: look for a restaurant full of people. If lots of people are eating there, it’s probably good. If a place is empty, you should probably keep on walking.

这只是众多现象中的一个例子。人们常常模仿身边的人。他们会穿和朋友一样的衣服，选择其他食客喜欢的菜肴，并且在认为其他人都在重复使用酒店毛巾时，更多地重复使用酒店毛巾。如果配偶投票，人们更有可能投票；如果朋友戒烟，人们更有可能戒烟；如果朋友肥胖，人们更有可能变胖。无论是做出诸如此类的微不足道的选择，例如无论是买什么牌子的咖啡，还是像缴税这样的重要决定，人们往往都会随大流。电视节目使用罐头笑声也是出于这个原因：当听到别人笑的时候，人们更容易跟着笑。

This is just one example of a much broader phenomenon. People often imitate those around them. They dress in the same styles as their friends, pick entrées preferred by other diners, and reuse hotel towels more when they think others are doing the same. People are more likely to vote if their spouse votes, more likely to quit smoking if their friends quit, and more likely to get fat if their friends become obese. Whether making trivial choices like what brand of coffee to buy or important decisions like paying their taxes, people tend to conform to what others are doing. Television shows use canned laugh tracks for this reason: people are more likely to laugh when they hear others laughing.

人们之所以会模仿，部分原因在于他人的选择能够提供信息。我们每天做出的许多决定，就像在异国他乡选择一家餐厅一样，只不过我们掌握的信息更多一些。哪个是沙拉叉来着？度假时带什么书比较好？我们不知道正确答案，即使我们大致知道该怎么做，也无法完全确定。

People imitate, in part, because others’ choices provide information. Many decisions we make on a daily basis are like choosing a restaurant in a foreign city, albeit with a little more information. Which one is the salad fork again? What’s a good book to take on vacation? We don’t know the right answer, and even if we have some sense of what to do, we’re not entirely sure.

为了消除疑虑，我们常常会观察别人在做什么，然后效仿。我们假设，如果别人都在做某件事，那它肯定是个好主意。他们可能知道一些我们不知道的事情。如果同桌的人似乎都在用小叉子吃芝麻菜，我们也会照做。如果很多人都在读约翰·格里沙姆的新悬疑小说，我们也会买一本，准备在即将到来的假期里读。

So to help resolve our uncertainty, we often look to what other people are doing and follow that. We assume that if other people are doing something, it must be a good idea. They probably know something we don’t. If our tablemates seem to be using the smaller fork to pick at the arugula, we do the same. If lots of people seem to be reading that new John Grisham thriller, we buy it for our upcoming vacation.

心理学家称这种观点为“社会认同。” 这就是为什么咖啡师酒吧招待会在上班之初往小费罐里放几张一美元的钞票，或许再加一张五美元的。如果小费罐是空的，顾客可能会认为其他人都不怎么给小费，自己也就不会给太多。但如果小费罐已经装满了钱，他们就会认为大家都给小费了，所以自己也应该给。

Psychologists call this idea “social proof.” This is why baristas and bartenders seed the tip jar at the beginning of their shift by dropping in a handful of ones and maybe a five. If the tip jar is empty, their customers may assume that other people aren’t really tipping and decide not to tip much themselves either. But if the tip jar is already brimming with money, they assume that everyone must be tipping, and thus they should tip as well.

社会认同甚至在生死攸关的事情上也发挥着作用。

Social proof even plays a role in matters of life and death.

想象一下，如果你的一个肾脏衰竭了。人体依靠这个器官过滤血液中的毒素和代谢废物，但一旦它停止工作，你的整个身体都会受到影响。钠会积聚，骨骼会变得脆弱，你还可能患上贫血或心脏病。如果不及时治疗，你会死亡。

Imagine one of your kidneys fails. Your body relies on this organ to filter the toxins and waste products from your blood, but when it stops working, your whole body suffers. Sodium builds up, your bones weaken, and you’re at risk of developing anemia or heart disease. If not treated quickly, you will die.

美国每年有超过4万人患上终末期肾病。他们的肾脏由于各种原因衰竭，他们有两种选择：要么每周三次往返治疗中心进行耗时的五小时透析治疗，要么接受肾移植。

More than 40,000 people in the United States come down with end-stage renal disease every year. Their kidneys fail for one reason or another and they have two options: either go through time-consuming back-and-forth visits to a treatment center three times a week for five-hour dialysis treatments, or get a kidney transplant.

但可供移植的肾源严重不足。目前，超过10万名患者在等待肾移植，每月新增患者超过4000人。毫不奇怪，等待肾移植的患者都渴望尽快得到肾源。

But there are not enough kidneys available for transplant. Currently more than 100,000 patients are on the wait list; more than 4,000 new patients are added each month. Not surprisingly, people on the wait list for a kidney are eager to get one.

想象一下，你就在那份名单上。名单是按先到先得的原则分配的，可用的肾脏会优先提供给名单上排名靠前的人，他们通常等待的时间也最长。你自己已经等了好几个月了，虽然你的排名比较靠后，但终于有一天，你获得了一个可能匹配的肾脏。你会接受的，对吗？

Imagine you are on that list. It is managed on a first-come, first-served basis, and available kidneys are offered first to people at the top of the list, who usually have been waiting the longest. You yourself have been waiting for months for an available kidney. You’re fairly low on the list, but finally one day you’re offered a potential match. You’d take it, right?

显然，需要肾脏移植才能保命的人应该接受捐赠。但令人惊讶的是，97.1%的肾脏捐赠都被拒绝了。

Clearly, people who need a kidney to save their lives should take one when offered. But surprisingly, 97.1 percent of kidney offers are refused.

现在，很多移植手术被拒的原因都是因为肾脏不匹配。从这个角度来看，器官移植有点像修车。你不可能把本田的化油器装到宝马车上。肾脏移植也是如此。如果组织或血型与你的不匹配，器官就无法发挥作用。

Now, many of those refusals are based on the kidney not being a good match. In this respect, getting an organ transplant is a bit like getting your car repaired. You can’t put a Honda carburetor in a BMW. Same with a kidney. If the tissue or blood type doesn’t match yours, the organ won’t work.

但麻省理工学院教授张娟娟在研究数百例肾脏捐赠案例后发现，社会认同也会导致人们拒绝可用的肾脏。假设你是名单上的第一百位，那么肾脏会先提供给名单上的第一人，然后是第二人，以此类推。所以，最终轮到你的时候，它肯定已经被其他九十九个人拒绝了。这就是社会认同发挥作用的地方。如果这么多人拒绝了这个肾脏，人们就会认为它肯定不太好。他们会推断肾脏质量低下，因此更有可能拒绝。事实上，这种推断导致每十个拒绝肾脏的人中就有一个人是误判。成千上万的患者拒绝了他们本应该接受的肾脏。即使人们无法直接与名单上的其他人交流，他们也会根据其他人的行为做出决定。

But when she looked at hundreds of kidney donations, MIT professor Juanjuan Zhang found that social proof also leads people to turn down available kidneys. Say you are the one hundredth person on the list. A kidney would have first been offered to the first person on the list, then the second, and so on. So to finally reach you, it must have been turned down by ninety-nine other people. This is where social proof comes into play. If so many others have refused this kidney, people assume it must not be very good. They infer it is low quality and are more likely to turn it down. In fact, such inferences lead one in every ten people who refuse a kidney to do so in error. Thousands of patients turn down kidneys they should have accepted. Even though people can’t communicate directly with others on the list, they make their decisions based on others’ behavior.

—————

—————

类似的现象时有发生。

Similar phenomena play out all the time.

在纽约市，Halal Chicken and Gyro 提供美味的鸡肉和羊肉拼盘，搭配清淡调味的米饭和皮塔饼。《纽约》杂志将其评为纽约市二十佳美食餐车之一，人们为了品尝 Halal 美味又实惠的餐点，甚至愿意排队等候长达一个小时。在一天中的某些时段前往，队伍会一直排到街区尽头。

In New York City, Halal Chicken and Gyro offers delicious platters of chicken and lamb, lightly seasoned rice, and pita bread. New York magazine ranked it as one of the top twenty food carts in the city, and people wait up to an hour to get one of Halal’s tasty but inexpensive meals. Go during certain times of day and the line will stretch all the way down the block.

我知道你在想什么。人们肯定在等那么久，因为这里的食物真的很好吃。你说的也对了一部分：这里的食物确实相当不错。

Now I know what you are thinking. People must wait that long because the food is really good. And you’re partially right: the food is quite good.

但同一老板在街对面经营着一家几乎一模一样的餐车，名叫“清真兄弟”（Halal Guys）。同样的食物，同样的包装，基本上就是同一款产品。但“清真兄弟”却从未排队。事实上，“清真兄弟”从未像它的姐妹店那样拥有众多忠实顾客。为什么呢？

But the same owners operate an almost identical food cart called Halal Guys right across the street. Same food, same packaging, basically an identical product. But there is no line. In fact, Halal Guys has never developed the same devout following as its sibling. Why?

社会认同。人们通常认为，队伍越长，食物就越好吃。

Social proof. People assume that the longer the line, the better the food must be.

这种从众心理甚至会影响人们选择的职业类型。每年我都会让我的二年级MBA学生做一个小练习。一半的学生被问到，在他们刚开始读MBA的时候，他们想做什么；另一半学生则被问到，他们现在想做什么。两组学生都看不到对方的问题，而且他们的回答都是匿名的。

This herd mentality even affects the type of careers people consider. Every year I ask my second-year MBA students to do a short exercise. Half the students are asked what they thought they wanted to do with their life right when they started the MBA program. The other half are asked what they want to do now. Neither group gets to see the question the other was asked and responses are anonymous.

结果令人瞩目。在开始MBA课程之前，学生们有着各种各样的抱负。一位学生想改革医疗保健系统，一位学生想创建一个新的旅游网站，还有一位学生想投身娱乐行业。有人想竞选公职，还有一位学生考虑成为一名企业家。少数学生表示他们想进入投资银行或咨询行业。总而言之，他们拥有多元化的兴趣、目标和职业规划。

The results are striking. Before they start the MBA program, students have a broad range of ambitions. One wanted to reform the health care system, another wanted to build a new travel website, and a third wanted to get involved in the entertainment industry. Someone wanted to run for political office and another student thought about becoming an entrepreneur. A handful say they want to go into investment banking or consulting. Overall, they possess a diverse set of interests, goals, and careers paths.

当被问及入学一年后的职业规划时，学生们的回答更加趋于一致和集中。超过三分之二的学生表示希望进入投资银行或咨询行业，只有少数人考虑其他职业。

The responses from students when asked what they want to do a year into the program are much more homogeneous and concentrated. More than two-thirds say they want to get into investment banking or consulting, with a small sprinkling of other careers.

这种趋同现象令人瞩目。诚然，人们在MBA课程期间可能会了解到不同的机会，但这种趋同行为部分源于社会影响。人们不确定自己应该选择什么职业，所以会参考他人的意见。而这种趋势会像滚雪球一样越滚越大。虽然入学之初可能只有不到20%的人对投资银行和咨询感兴趣，但这个比例仍然高于其他任何职业。一些人看到这20%的人选择转行。又有一些人看到这些人转行，也纷纷效仿。很快，这个比例就达到了30%。这使得其他人更有可能转行。很快，这20%的比例就大幅增长。因此，通过社会影响，这种最初微小的优势被放大。社交互动引导着原本倾向于不同道路的学生走向了同一个方向。

The convergence is remarkable. Sure, people may learn about different opportunities during the MBA program, but part of this herding is driven by social influence. People aren’t sure what career to choose, so they look to others. And it snowballs. While less than 20 percent of people might have been interested in investment banking and consulting going into the program, that number is larger than any other career. A few people see that 20 percent and switch. A few more see those people switch, and they follow along. Soon the number is 30 percent. Which makes other people even more likely to switch. Soon that 20 percent has become much larger. So through social influence this initially small advantage gets magnified. Social interaction led students who originally preferred different paths to go in the same direction.

社会影响力对行为有着巨大的影响，但要了解如何利用它来帮助产品和理念流行起来，我们需要了解它的影响何时最为显著。这就引出了科琳·约翰内森。

Social influence has a big effect on behavior, but to understand how to use it to help products and ideas catch on, we need to understand when its effects are strongest. And that brings us to Koreen Johannessen.

可观察性的力量

THE POWER OF OBSERVABILITY

科琳·约翰内森最初在亚利桑那大学担任临床社会工作者。起初，她受聘于心理健康小组，帮助学生应对抑郁症和药物滥用等问题。但多年治疗学生后，约翰内森意识到自己找错了方向。诚然，她可以尝试解决学生面临的现有问题，但防患于未然才是更好的选择。于是，约翰内森转到校园健康小组，负责健康教育工作，最终成为健康促进和预防服务部门的主任。

Koreen Johannessen started at the University of Arizona as a clinical social worker. Originally, she was hired by the mental health group to help students deal with problems like depression and drug abuse. But after years of treating students, Johannessen realized that she was working on the wrong end of the problem. Sure, she could try to fix the ongoing issues that afflicted students, but it would be much better to prevent them before they started. So Johannessen moved over to the campus health group and took over health education, eventually becoming the director of health promotion and preventive services.

与美国大多数大学一样，亚利桑那大学面临的最大问题之一是酗酒。超过四分之三的美国人未达到法定饮酒年龄的大学生报告称饮酒。但更令人担忧的是饮酒量。学生饮酒问题十分严重。44%的学生有酗酒行为，每年有超过1800名美国大学生死于与酒精相关的伤害。另有60万人因酒后受伤。这是一个非常严重的问题。

As at most universities in the United States, one of the biggest issues at Arizona was alcohol abuse. More than three-quarters of American college students under the legal drinking age report drinking alcohol. But the bigger concern was the quantity that students consume. Forty-four percent of students binge-drink, and more than 1,800 U.S. college students die every year from alcohol-related injuries. Another 600,000 are injured while under the influence of alcohol. It’s a huge issue.

约翰内森直面这个问题。她把传单贴满了校园，详细列举了酗酒的负面后果。她还在校报上刊登广告，介绍酒精如何影响认知功能和学习表现。她甚至在学生中心摆放了一口棺材，上面贴着与酒精相关的死亡人数统计数据。但所有这些举措似乎都收效甚微。仅仅教育学生了解酒精的风险似乎还不够。

Johannessen addressed the problem head-on. She papered the campus with flyers detailing the negative consequences of bingeing. She placed ads in the school paper with information about how alcohol affects cognitive functioning and performance in school. She even set up a coffin at the student center with statistics about the number of alcohol-related deaths. But none of these initiatives seemed to put much of a dent in the problem. Simply educating students about the risks of alcohol didn’t seem to be enough.

于是约翰内森试着去问学生们他们对饮酒的看法。

So Johannessen tried asking the students how they felt about drinking.

出乎意料的是，她发现大多数学生都表示对同龄人的饮酒习惯感到不适。当然，他们偶尔也会像大多数成年人一样小酌几杯。但他们并不像其他学生那样酗酒。他们厌恶地谈起自己照顾宿醉室友或在别人呕吐时帮她扶着头发的经历。所以，尽管同龄人似乎对这种饮酒文化习以为常，但他们自己却并不认同。

Surprisingly, she found that most students said they were not comfortable with the drinking habits of their peers. Sure, they might enjoy a casual drink once in a while, just like most adults. But they weren’t into the heavy binge drinking they saw among other students. They spoke distastefully about the times they nursed a hungover roommate or held someone’s hair while she threw up in the toilet. So while their peers seemed fine with the drinking culture, they weren’t.

约翰内森很高兴。大多数学生反对酗酒这一事实似乎预示着消除酗酒问题指日可待——直到她仔细思考之后才发现并非如此。

Johannessen was pleased. The fact that most students were against binge drinking seemed to bode well for eliminating the drinking problem—until she thought about it closely.

如果大多数学生对饮酒文化感到不适，那么这种文化当初为何会出现？如果学生真的不喜欢喝酒，为什么他们还要喝那么多？

If most students were uncomfortable with the drinking culture, then why was it happening in the first place? Why were students drinking so much if they don’t actually like it?

因为行为是公开的，而思想是私密的。

Because behavior is public and thoughts are private.

设身处地地想一想，如果你是一名大学生，你会怎么想？周围到处都是喝酒的景象。你会看到橄榄球赛前的尾门派对，兄弟会宿舍里的啤酒桶派对，以及姐妹会舞会上的开放式酒吧。你会看到同龄人喝酒，而且看起来很开心，于是你就会觉得自己是个异类，觉得其他人都比你更爱喝酒。所以你也会再喝一杯。

Put yourself in a college student’s situation. When you look around, you’d see a lot of drinking. You’d see tailgates at the football games, keg parties at the frat house, and open bars at the sorority formal. You’d witness your peers drinking and seeming happy about it, so you’d assume that you are the outlier and that everyone else likes drinking more than you do. So you’d have another drink.

但学生们没有意识到的是，其实每个人都有类似的想法。他们的同龄人也有着同样的经历。他们看到别人喝酒，所以自己也跟着喝。这种循环往复，是因为人们无法读懂彼此的心思。如果他们能读懂，就会意识到每个人都有同样的感受。他们也不会感到这种社会认同感驱使他们喝那么多酒了。

But what students don’t realize is that everyone is having similar thoughts. Their peers are having the same experience. They see others drinking, so they drink, too. And the cycle continues because people can’t read one another’s thoughts. If they could, they’d realize that everyone felt the same way. And they wouldn’t feel all this social proof compelling them to drink as much.

举个更常见的例子，想想你上次听完一场令人费解的PPT演示是什么时候。可能是关于股权多元化或者供应链重组之类的。演讲结束时，演讲者很可能会问听众有没有什么问题。

For a more familiar example, think about the last time you sat through a bewildering PowerPoint presentation. Something about equity diversification or supply chain reorganization. At the end of the talk, the speaker probably asked the audience if anyone had any questions.

回应是什么？

The response?

沉默。

Silence.

但并非因为其他人都听懂了。其他人可能和你一样一头雾水。虽然他们很想举手提问，但却没有，因为每个人都担心自己是唯一一个没听懂的人。为什么？因为没有人提问。没有人看到其他人感到困惑的迹象，所以每个人都把自己的疑惑藏在心里。因为行为是公开的，而思想是私密的。

But not because everyone else understood the presentation. The others were probably just as bewildered as you were. But while they would have liked to raise their hands, they didn’t because each one is worried that he or she is the only person who didn’t understand. Why? Because no one else was asking questions. No one saw any public signal that others were confused so everyone keeps his doubts to him- or herself. Because behavior is public and thoughts are private.

—————

—————

“有样学样”这句名言不仅仅反映了人类的模仿天性。人们可以模仿……只有当他们看到别人在做什么时，才会效仿。大学生可能个人反对酗酒，但他们酗酒是因为他们看到别人都在这样做。一家餐厅可能非常受欢迎，但如果很难看到里面的情况（例如，临街的窗户是磨砂玻璃），路人就无法利用这些信息来做出自己的选择。

The famous phrase “Monkey see, monkey do” captures more than just the human penchant for imitation. People can imitate only when they can see what others are doing. College students may personally be against binge drinking, but they binge because that is what they observe others doing. A restaurant might be extremely popular, but if it’s hard to see inside (e.g., the front windows are frosted), there is no way passersby can use that information to inform their own choices.

可观察性对产品和创意能否流行起有着巨大的影响。比如说，一家服装公司推出了一款新衬衫。如果你看到有人穿着它，并且觉得不错，你就可以去买同款或类似的衬衫。但这种情况在袜子上发生的可能性就小得多。

Observability has a huge impact on whether products and ideas catch on. Say a clothing company introduces a new shirt style. If you see someone wearing it and decide you like it, you can go buy the same shirt, or something similar. But this is much less likely to happen with socks.

为什么？

Why?

因为衬衫是公开的，而袜子是私密的，所以更难被人看到。

Because shirts are public and socks are private. They’re harder to see.

牙膏和汽车的情况也类似。你可能不知道邻居用的是什么牌子的牙膏。它可能藏在他们家里的某个角落，浴室的某个柜子里。但你更有可能知道他们开什么车。而且，由于汽车偏好更容易观察，邻居的购买行为更有可能影响你的选择。

The same goes for toothpaste versus cars. You probably don’t know what kind of toothpaste your neighbors use. It’s hidden inside their house, inside their bathroom, inside a cabinet. You’re more likely to know what car they drive. And because car preferences are easier to observe, it’s much more likely that your neighbors’ purchase behavior can influence yours.

我和我的同事布莱克·麦克沙恩、埃里克·布拉德洛利用以下数据检验了这个想法：150万辆汽车销量。邻居买了辆新车，这足以让你也买辆新车吗？

My colleagues Blake McShane, Eric Bradlow, and I tested this idea using data on 1.5 million car sales. Would a neighbor buying a new car be enough to get you to buy a new one?

果不其然，我们发现了一个相当显著的影响。例如，如果丹佛的其他居民近期购买了新车，那么居住在丹佛的居民购买新车的可能性也会更高。而且这种影响相当大。大约每售出八辆车中就有一辆是受社会影响而购买的。

Sure enough, we found a pretty impressive effect. People who lived in, say, Denver, were more likely to buy a new car if other Denverites had bought new cars recently. And the effect was pretty big. Approximately one out of every eight cars sold was because of social influence.

更令人印象深刻的是可观察性在这些效应中的作用。不同城市之间，人们更容易看到其他人驾驶的车辆。洛杉矶的人们倾向于开车通勤，所以他们与乘坐地铁通勤的纽约人相比，洛杉矶居民更容易看到其他人开的车。在迈阿密等阳光明媚的地方，你比在西雅图等多雨的城市更容易看到旁边的人开的车。这些因素通过影响可观察性，也决定了社会影响对汽车购买行为的影响。在洛杉矶和迈阿密等更容易看到他人驾驶车辆的城市，人们更容易受到他人购买行为的影响。当行为更易观察时，社会影响也更强。

Even more impressive was the role of observability in these effects. Cities vary in how easy it is to see what other people are driving. People in Los Angeles tend to commute by car, so they are more likely to see what others are driving than New Yorkers, who commute by subway. In sunny places like Miami, you can more easily see what the person next to you is driving than in rainy cities like Seattle. By affecting observability, these conditions also determined the effect of social influence on auto purchases. People were more influenced by others’ purchases in places like Los Angeles and Miami, where it is easier to see what others were driving. Social influence was stronger when behavior was more observable.

显而易见的事物更容易被人们谈论。你有没有走进过别人的办公室或家里，问起过桌上一个造型奇特的镇纸，或是客厅墙上一幅色彩鲜艳的艺术画？想象一下，如果这些东西被锁在保险箱里，或是藏在地下室里，人们还会像现在这样谈论它们吗？恐怕不会。公开可见的事物更容易引发口碑传播。越容易看到的东西，人们就越会谈论它。

Observable things are also more likely to be discussed. Ever walked into someone’s office or home and inquired about a weird paperweight on the desk or a colorful art print on the living room wall? Imagine if those items were locked in a safe or tucked away in the basement. Would they get talked about as much? Probably not. Public visibility boosts word of mouth. The easier something is to see, the more people talk about it.

可观察性也能促进购买和行动。正如我们在“触发因素”一章中所讨论的，环境中的线索不仅能增强口碑传播，还能提醒人们他们原本就想买或想做的事情。你可能原本打算吃得更健康，或者访问朋友提到的那个新网站，但如果没有明显的触发因素来唤起你的记忆，你很可能就会忘记。产品或服务的曝光度越高，就越能促使人们采取行动。

Observability also spurs purchase and action. As we discussed in the Triggers chapter, cues in the environment not only boost word of mouth but also remind people about things they already wanted to buy or do. You may have meant to eat healthier or visit that new website your friend mentioned, but without a visible trigger to jog your memory, you’re more likely to forget. The more public a product or service is, the more it triggers people to take action.

那么，如何才能让产品或理念更容易被公众看到呢？

So how can products or ideas be made more publicly observable?

将私密之事公之于众……用胡子来表达

MAKING THE PRIVATE PUBLIC . . . WITH MOUSTACHES

每年秋季，我在沃顿商学院教授大约六十名MBA学生，到十月底，我对大多数学生的情况都有了一些了解。我知道班上所有学生的情况。我知道谁每天都会迟到五分钟，谁会第一个举手，谁会穿得像个明星。

Every fall I teach about sixty MBA students at the Wharton School, and by the end of October I’ve gotten some sense of most of the students in the class. I know who is going to be five minutes late every day, who will be the first to raise a hand, and who will be dressed like a prima donna.

几年前的十一月初，我走进教室时，看到一个我一直以为很拘谨的男生留着浓密的胡子，着实让我吃了一惊。他可不是忘了刮胡子那么简单；他的胡子浓密得像个八字胡，两鬓都快要卷起来了。他看起来就像是罗利·芬格斯和老黑白电影里反派的混合体。

So I was a bit surprised a few years ago when I walked into class in early November to see what I’d thought was a pretty buttoned-down guy sporting a big moustache. It wasn’t simply that he had forgotten to shave; he had a full handlebar with ends almost ready to curl up on the sides. He looked like a cross between Rollie Fingers and a villain in an old black-and-white movie.

起初我以为他只是在尝试留胡子。但当我环顾四周时，我发现房间里还有另外两位新晋的胡子爱好者。看来这股潮流正在兴起。究竟是什么引发了这股胡子热潮呢？

At first I thought he must be trying a facial hair experiment. But then when I looked around the room I noticed two other new moustache devotees. A trend seemed to be catching on. What precipitated the sudden outburst of moustaches?

—————

—————

每年，癌症每年夺走全球超过420万男性的生命。每年新增病例超过600万例。得益于慷慨的捐助，癌症的研究和治疗取得了巨大进展。但是，致力于抗击癌症的组织如何才能利用社会影响力来增加捐款呢？

Every year, cancer claims the lives of more than 4.2 million men worldwide. More than 6 million new cases are diagnosed each year. Thanks to generous donations, great headway has been made in research and treatment. But how can organizations that work to fight this disease leverage social influence to increase donations?

不幸的是，和许多其他公益事业一样，是否支持某个癌症基金通常属于个人私事。如果你和大多数人一样，你可能根本不知道你的邻居、同事甚至朋友中有哪些人捐款帮助对抗这种疾病。因此，他们的行为无法影响你的行为，反之亦然。

Unfortunately, as with many causes, whether you support a particular cancer fund is typically a private matter. If you’re like most people, you probably have little idea which of your neighbors, coworkers, or even friends have donated to help fight this disease. So there is no way for their behavior to influence yours or vice versa.

而这正是胡子的用武之地。

And that is where the moustaches come in.

这一切始于2003年的一个星期天下午。一群来自澳大利亚墨尔本的朋友围坐在一起喝啤酒。谈话漫无边际地聊着，最后话题转到了七八十年代的时尚。“胡子都去哪儿了？”有人问道。几杯啤酒下肚后，他们想出了一个挑战：看谁能留出最漂亮的胡子。消息很快传到了其他朋友耳中，最终他们组成了一个三十人的小团体。整个十一月，他们都留起了胡子。

It all started one Sunday afternoon in 2003. A group of friends from Melbourne, Australia, were sitting around drinking beers. The conversation meandered in various directions and finally ended up on 1970s and 80s fashion. “What ever happened to the moustache?” one guy asked. A few beers more and they came up with a challenge: to see who could grow the best moustache. The word spread to their other friends, and eventually they had a small group of thirty people. All grew moustaches for the thirty days of November.

大家玩得非常开心，于是第二年十一月他们决定再次举办这项活动。但这一次，他们决定为这项活动赋予意义。受到乳腺癌防治工作的启发，他们希望为男性健康做类似的事情。因此，他们成立了Movember基金会，并采用了“改变男性健康面貌”的口号。那一年，450名男士为澳大利亚前列腺癌基金会筹集了54,000美元。

Everyone had so much fun that the next November they decided to do it again. But this time they decided to put a cause behind their efforts. Inspired by the work being done with breast cancer awareness, they wanted to do something similar for men’s health. So they formed the Movember Foundation and adopted the tagline “Changing the face of men’s health.” That year 450 guys raised $54,000 for the Prostate Cancer Foundation of Australia.

活动由此发展壮大。第二年，参与人数超过9000人。第三年，超过5万人。很快，这项年度盛事开始在全球各地传播。2007年，从爱尔兰和丹麦到南非和台湾，各地都举办了相关活动。该组织迄今已在全球范围内筹集了超过1.74亿美元的善款。对于几撮胡须来说，这成绩相当不错。

It grew from there. Next year there were more than 9,000 participants. The following year, more than 50,000. Soon the annual event started spreading around the world. In 2007, events were launched everywhere from Ireland and Denmark to South Africa and Taiwan. The organization has since raised more than $174 million worldwide. Not bad for a few tufts of facial hair.

现在，每年十一月，男士们都会承诺通过蓄须来提高公众意识并筹集资金。规则很简单：月初要刮干净胡子，然后在接下来的一个月里蓄须并精心打理。哦，对了，还要举止得体，像个真正的乡村绅士。

Now, every November, men pledge to raise awareness and money by growing moustaches. The rules are simple. Start the first of the month with a clean-shaven face. For the rest of the month, grow and groom a moustache. Oh—and along the way, conduct yourself like a true country gentleman.

Movember基金会的成功在于他们找到了将私人活动公开化的方法。他们找到了如何将对一项抽象事业——通常难以观察的事物——的支持，转化为人人都能看到的结果。在十一月的三十天里，留着胡子的人们……实际上，他们成为了这项事业的活广告牌。正如Movember网站上所指出的那样，

The Movember Foundation succeeded because they figured out how to make the private public. They figured out how to take support for an abstract cause—something not typically observable—and make it something that everyone can see. For the thirty days of November people who sport a moustache effectively become walking, talking billboards for the cause. As noted on Movember’s website,

他们通过自己的行动和言语，引发了关于男性健康这一经常被忽视的问题的私人和公共对话，从而提高了人们的意识。

Through their actions and words they [participants] raise awareness by prompting private and public conversations around the often-ignored issue of men’s health.

这的确能引发话题。看到熟人突然长出胡子，自然会引发讨论。人们通常会私下议论一番，直到有人鼓起勇气询问对方为何蓄须。而当他解释之后，便能分享社交资本，吸引新的追随者。每年到了十一月，我都会看到越来越多的学生留起了胡子。将这种风潮公开化，无疑让它比其他任何方式都更快地流行起来。

And start conversation it does. Seeing someone you know suddenly sprout a moustache generates discussion. People usually gossip a bit among themselves until someone gets up the courage to ask the wearer what prompted the new facial hair. And when he explains, he shares the social currency and generates new devotees. Each year I see more and more of my students sporting moustaches come November. Making the cause public helped it catch on more quickly than it ever could have otherwise.

—————

—————

大多数产品、理念和行为都是私下进行的。你的同事喜欢哪些网站？你的邻居支持哪些投票提案？除非他们告诉你，否则你可能永远不会知道。虽然这对你个人来说可能无关紧要，但对组织、企业和理念的成功却至关重要。如果人们看不到其他人的选择和行为，他们就无法模仿。就像那些酗酒的大学生一样，人们可能会因为觉得自己的观点不被支持而改变自己的行为，甚至变得更糟。*

Most products, ideas, and behaviors are consumed privately. What websites do your coworkers like? Which ballot initiatives do your neighbors support? Unless they tell you, you may never know. And though that might not matter to you personally, it matters a lot for the success of organizations, businesses, and ideas. If people can’t see what others are choosing and doing, they can’t imitate them. And, like the binge-drinking college students, people might change their behavior for the worse because they feel their views aren’t supported.*

解决这个问题需要将私人事务公开化，即为私人选择、行为和观点发出公开信号，将原本不可见的想法或行为转化为更容易观察的现象。

Solving this problem requires making the private public. Generating public signals for private choices, actions, and opinions. Taking what was once an unobservable thought or behavior and transforming it into a more observable one.

科琳·约翰内森（Koreen Johannessen）通过将私密的饮酒行为公之于众，成功降低了亚利桑那州学生的饮酒量。她在校报上刊登广告，仅仅陈述了真实的饮酒常态：大多数学生在聚会时只喝一两杯，69%的学生只喝四杯或更少。她没有强调饮酒对健康的危害，而是着重传递社会信息。通过向学生展示大多数同龄人并没有酗酒，她帮助他们意识到其他人也有同样的感受——大多数学生并不想酗酒。这纠正了学生们对他人行为的错误认知，并最终促使他们减少了自己的饮酒量。通过将私密的饮酒行为公之于众，约翰内森成功地将酗酒现象减少了近 30%。

Koreen Johannessen was able to reduce Arizona students’ drinking by making the private public. She created ads in the school newspaper that merely stated the true norm. That most students had only one or two drinks, and 69 percent have four or fewer drinks, when they party. She didn’t focus on the health consequences of drinking, she focused on social information. By showing students that the majority of their peers weren’t bingeing, she helped them realize that others felt the same way. That most students didn’t want to binge. This corrected the false inferences students had made about others’ behavior and led them to reduce their own drinking as a result. By making the private public, Johannessen was able to decrease heavy drinking by almost 30 percent.

广告本身：与世界分享 Hotmail

ADVERTISING ITSELF: SHARING HOTMAIL WITH THE WORLD

让事物更广为人知的一种方法是设计能够自我宣传的理念。

One way to make things more public is to design ideas that advertise themselves.

1996年7月4日，萨比尔·巴蒂亚和杰克·史密斯推出了名为Hotmail的全新电子邮件服务。当时，大多数人都是通过AOL等互联网服务提供商获取电子邮件。每月支付一定费用，用家里的电话线拨号上网，然后通过 AOL 界面访问邮件。这种方式限制很多。你只能在安装了服务的地方连接，被束缚在一台电脑前。

On July 4, 1996, Sabeer Bhatia and Jack Smith introduced a new e-mail service called Hotmail. At the time, most people got their e-mail through Internet service providers like AOL. You’d pay a monthly fee, dial up from home using a phone line, and access your messages through the AOL interface. It was restricting. You could connect only from the place where you had the service installed. You were chained to one computer.

但Hotmail与众不同。它是最早的基于网络的电子邮件服务之一，允许用户从世界任何地方的任何电脑访问自己的收件箱。他们只需要一台连接互联网的设备和一个网络浏览器。选择在独立日发布这项服务，象征着它使用户摆脱了对现有服务提供商的束缚。

But Hotmail was different. It was one of the first Web-based e-mail services, which allowed people to access their inbox from any computer anywhere in the world. All they needed was an Internet connection and a Web browser. Independence Day was chosen for the announcement to symbolize how the service freed people from being locked into their current provider.

Hotmail 是一款很棒的产品，而且在我们之前讨论过的几个口碑传播因素中也表现出色。在当时，能够随时随地访问电子邮件确实是一件了不起的事情。因此，早期用户乐于谈论它，因为它能让他们获得社交资本。与其他电子邮件服务相比，Hotmail 还为用户提供了显著的优势（首先，它是免费的！），因此许多人出于其实用价值而分享它。

Hotmail was a great product, and it also scored well on a number of the word-of-mouth drivers we’ve talked about so far. At the time, it was quite remarkable to be able to access e-mail from anywhere. So early adopters liked talking about it because it gave them Social Currency. The product also offered users significant benefits over other e-mail services (for starters, it was free!), so many people shared it for its Practical Value.

但Hotmail的创始人不仅仅创造了一款优秀的产品。他们还巧妙地利用了可观察性，帮助他们的产品获得了成功。

But the creators of Hotmail did more than just create a great product. They also cleverly leveraged observability to help their product catch on.

每一封从Hotmail账户发出的电子邮件都像是在为这个正在崛起的品牌做宣传。邮件底部会附上一条信息和一个链接，上面写着“访问www.hotmail.com ，获取您的Hotmail私人免费邮箱”。每当Hotmail现有用户发送邮件时，他们也同时向潜在客户传递了一份社会认同——一种对这项此前鲜为人知的服务的隐性认可。

Every e-mail sent from a Hotmail account was like a short plug for the growing brand. At the bottom was a message and link that simply said “Get Your Private, Free E-mail from Hotmail at www.hotmail.com.” Every time current Hotmail customers sent an e-mail, they also sent prospective customers a bit of social proof—an implicit endorsement for this previously unknown service.

事实证明，这个策略奏效了。短短一年多时间，Hotmail 的用户就超过了 850 万。不久之后，微软以 4 亿美元的价格收购了这项蓬勃发展的服务。自那以后，Hotmail 的用户数量更是超过了 850 万。已有3.5亿用户注册。

And it worked. In a little over a year Hotmail signed up more than 8.5 million subscribers. Soon after, Microsoft bought the burgeoning service for $400 million. Since then more than 350 million users have signed up.

苹果和黑莓都采用了相同的策略。它们电子邮件底部的签名栏通常会显示“使用黑莓发送”或“来自我的iPhone”。用户可以轻松地将此默认信息更改为其他内容（我的一位同事就把他的签名改成了“信鸽发送”），但大多数人不会这样做，部分原因是他们喜欢这种备注带来的社交价值。通过在电子邮件中留下这些备注，人们还有助于传播品牌信息，并影响其他人尝试使用该产品。

Apple and BlackBerry have adopted the same strategy. The signature lines at the bottom of their e-mails often say “Sent using BlackBerry” or “Sent from my iPhone.” Users can easily change this default message to something else (one of my colleagues changed his signature to say “Sent by Carrier Pigeon”), but most people don’t, in part because they like the Social Currency the notes provide. And by leaving these notes on their e-mail, people also help spread awareness about the brand and influence others to try it.

—————

—————

所有这些例子都涉及能够自我宣传的产品。每次人们使用这些产品或服务时，他们也都在传递社会认同或被动认可，因为使用情况是可以观察到的。

All these examples involve products that advertise themselves. Every time people use the product or service they also transmit social proof or passive approval because usage is observable.

许多公司都通过醒目的品牌宣传来运用这一理念。Abercrombie & Fitch、Nike 和 Burberry 等品牌都在其产品上印有品牌名称或独特的标志和图案。“出售”标牌则会明确标明卖家正在与哪位房地产经纪人合作。

Many companies apply this idea through prominent branding. Abercrombie & Fitch, Nike, and Burberry all garnish their products with brand names or distinctive logos and patterns. For Sale signs broadcast which Realtor the seller is working with.

秉持着“越大越好”的理念，一些公司纷纷增大了品牌标识的尺寸。拉夫·劳伦 (Ralph Lauren) 一直以其标志性的马球运动员形象而闻名，但其“大马标”衬衫更是将这一著名标志放大了十六倍。为了在标识霸主地位的争夺中不甘示弱，拉科斯特 (Lacoste) 也采取了类似的策略。其“超大鳄鱼”马球衫上的鳄鱼图案硕大无比，仿佛随时会咬掉穿着者的手臂。

Following the notion that more is better, some companies have increased the size of their logos. Ralph Lauren has always been known for its characteristic polo player, but its Big Pony shirts made this famous emblem sixteen times larger. Not to be outdone in the escalation for logo supremacy, Lacoste made a similar move. The alligator on its Oversized Croc polo shirt is so large it looks as if it will bite the arm off of any person wearing it.

但醒目的标志并非产品宣传的唯一途径。以苹果公司决定将 iPod 耳机设计成白色为例。苹果公司最初推出 iPod 时，数字音乐播放器市场竞争激烈。Diamond Multimedia、Creative、Compaq 和 Archos 等公司都生产播放器，而且一家公司设备上的音乐很难转移到另一家。此外，这些相互竞争的标准中哪些（如果有的话）会保留下来，以及从便携式 CD 播放器或随身听换成这款新的昂贵设备是否值得，目前尚不清楚。

But large logos aren’t the only way products can advertise themselves. Take Apple’s decision to make iPod headphones white. When Apple first introduced the iPod, there was lots of competition in the digital music player space. Diamond Multimedia, Creative, Compaq, and Archos all offered players, and music on one company’s device couldn’t easily be transferred to another. Further, it wasn’t clear which, if any, of these competing standards would stick around, and whether it was worth switching from a portable CD player or Walkman to buy this new, expensive device.

但由于大多数设备都配备黑色耳机，苹果的白色耳机线格外醒目。耳机本身就像一个广告，让人们很容易看到有多少人正在放弃传统的随身听，转而使用iPod。这种直观的社会认同表明iPod是一款优秀的产品，也让潜在用户更有信心购买。

But because most devices came with black headphones, Apple’s white headphone cords stood out. By advertising themselves, the headphones made it easy to see how many other people were switching away from the traditional Walkman and adopting the iPod. This was visible social proof that suggested the iPod was a good product and made potential adopters feel more comfortable about purchasing it as well.

形状、声音以及其他诸多独特特征都能帮助产品进行自我宣传。品客薯片就装在独特的圆筒里。使用微软操作系统的电脑在启动时会发出独特的声音。1992年，法国鞋履设计师克里斯提·鲁布托(Christian Louboutin)觉得自己的鞋子缺乏活力。环顾四周，他注意到一位员工涂着醒目的红色香奈儿指甲油。就是它了！他心想，于是把这种指甲油涂在了自己的鞋底上。如今，鲁布托的鞋子都配有红色漆底，使其极具辨识度。即使是对这个品牌不太了解的人，也能一眼认出它们。

Shapes, sounds, and a host of other distinctive characteristics can also help products advertise themselves. Pringles come in a unique tube. Computers using the Microsoft operating system make a distinctive sound when they boot up. In 1992, French footwear designer Christian Louboutin felt his shoes lacked energy. Looking around, he noticed the striking red Chanel nail polish an employee was wearing. That’s it! he thought, and applied the polish to his shoes’ soles. Now Louboutin shoes always come with red-lacquered soles, making them instantly recognizable. They’re distinctive and easy to see, even for people who know little about the brand.

类似的理念可以应用于众多产品和服务。裁缝会赠送印有自己名字的西装袋。夜总会用烟花来宣告有人购买了酒水服务。门票通常放在人们的口袋里，但如果剧团和小型联盟球队能用徽章或贴纸代替“门票”，那么“门票”就会更容易被公众注意到。

Similar ideas can be applied to a host of products and services. Tailors give away suit bags imprinted with the tailor’s name. Nightclubs use sparklers to broadcast when someone pays to get bottle service. Tickets usually sit in people’s pockets, but if theater companies and minor league teams could use buttons or stickers as the “ticket” instead, “tickets” would be much more publicly observable.

设计能够自我宣传的产品，对于那些没有广告宣传渠道的小公司或组织来说，是一种特别有效的策略。我们拥有丰富的资源。即使没有资金购买电视广告或在当地报纸上刊登广告，如果产品本身具有宣传作用，现有客户也可以充当广告人。这就像是无需广告预算的广告。

Designing products that advertise themselves is a particularly powerful strategy for small companies or organizations that don’t have a lot of resources. Even when there is no money to buy television ads or a spot in the local paper, existing customers can act as advertisements if the product advertises itself. It’s like advertising without an advertising budget.

—————

—————

当人们消费某种产品、理念或行为时，它就相当于在进行自我宣传。当人们穿着特定的衣服、参加集会或使用某个网站时，他们的朋友、同事和邻居更有可能看到他们的行为并加以模仿。

A product, idea, or behavior advertises itself when people consume it. When people wear certain clothes, attend a rally, or use a website, they make it more likely that their friends, coworkers, and neighbors will see what they are doing and imitate it.

如果一家公司或机构足够幸运，人们会经常使用其产品或服务。但其他时间呢？当消费者穿着其他衣服、支持其他事业或做其他事情时呢？是否存在某种社会认同，即使在产品未被使用或该理念不再被人们关注的情况下，这种认同仍然能够持续存在？

If a company or organization is lucky, people consume its product or service often. But what about the rest of the time? When consumers are wearing other clothes, supporting a different cause, or doing something else entirely? Is there something that generates social proof that sticks around even when the product is not being used or the idea is not top of mind?

是的。它被称为行为残留。

Yes. And it’s called behavioral residue.

Livestrong腕带作为行为残留

LIVESTRONG WRISTBANDS AS BEHAVIORAL RESIDUE

斯科特·麦克伊彻恩面临着一个艰难的抉择。2003年，兰斯·阿姆斯特朗炙手可热。作为耐克公司的赞助商，麦克伊彻恩正努力寻找最佳方式，充分利用兰斯所受到的关注。

Scott MacEachern had a tough decision to make. In 2003, Lance Armstrong was a hot commodity. As his sponsor at Nike, MacEachern was trying to figure out the best way to harness all the attention Lance was getting.

兰斯的故事令人动容。七年前，他被诊断出患有危及生命的睾丸癌，医生当时认为他只有40%的生存几率。但他不仅重返赛场，而且比以往任何时候都更加强大，这让所有人感到惊讶。复出后，他连续五次赢得环法自行车赛冠军，并在此过程中激励了数百万人，从与癌症抗争的十五岁少年到大学生，无不深受鼓舞。为了保持体形，兰斯帮助人们重拾信念。如果他能战胜癌症，他们也能克服生活中的挑战。（值得注意的是，自2003年以来的十年间，人们逐渐意识到阿姆斯特朗的成功可能与服用兴奋剂有关。但鉴于Livestrong腕带以及兰斯·阿姆斯特朗基金会的巨大成功，我们有必要探究它们为何能够流行起来，而不必纠结于阿姆斯特朗的个人经历是否蒙上污点。）

Lance had a powerful story. Diagnosed with life-threatening testicular cancer seven years earlier, Lance had been given only a 40 percent chance of survival. But he surprised everyone not only by returning to cycling, but by coming back stronger than ever. Since his return, he had won the Tour de France an astounding five times in a row and inspired millions of people along the way. From fifteen-year-olds dealing with cancer to college students trying to stay in shape, Lance helped people to believe. If he could come back from cancer, they could overcome the challenges in their own lives. (Note that in the decade since 2003, it has become apparent that Armstrong may have achieved his success through the use of performance-enhancing drugs. But given the powerful success of Livestrong wristbands, and the Lance Armstrong Foundation more generally, it is worth considering how they became popular, outside of whether Armstrong’s personal story is tainted or not.)

麦克伊彻恩希望利用这股热情。兰斯已经超越了体育的范畴，他不仅是一位英雄，更是一位文化偶像。麦克伊彻恩想要表彰兰斯的成就，并庆祝他即将冲击第六次环法自行车赛冠军的壮举。他还希望借助这股热潮，为兰斯·阿姆斯特朗基金会筹集资金并提高公众意识。

MacEachern wanted to capitalize on this enthusiasm. Lance had transcended sports. He had become not only a hero, but a cultural icon. MacEachern wanted to recognize Lance’s achievements and celebrate his upcoming attempt at a record sixth Tour de France victory. He also wanted to use the outpouring of interest and support to raise funds and awareness for the Lance Armstrong Foundation.

麦克伊彻恩提出了两个潜在方案。

MacEachern developed two potential ideas.

最初的想法是组织一次横跨美国的自行车骑行活动。参与者可以设定自己的骑行里程目标，并邀请亲朋好友赞助他们的行程。这项活动旨在鼓励更多人锻炼身体，提升人们对自行车运动的兴趣，并为兰斯·阿姆斯特朗基金会筹款。兰斯本人甚至有可能参与其中。这项活动将持续数周，并有望在骑行途经的所有城市获得全国乃至地方媒体的广泛报道。

The first idea was a bike ride across America. People would set a mileage goal for themselves and get friends or family members to sponsor their ride. It would get more people to exercise, boost interest in cycling, and raise money for the Lance Armstrong Foundation. Lance might even do part of the trip. The event would take weeks and likely garner significant media coverage both nationally and locally in all the cities the ride covered.

第二个想法是推出一款腕带。耐克公司最近开始销售“篮球手环”（Baller Bands），这是一种硅胶橡胶腕带，内侧印有“团队”（TEAM）或“尊重”（RESPECT）等励志标语。篮球运动员佩戴这种腕带是为了保持专注和提升动力。为什么不推出一款以阿姆斯特朗为主题的腕带呢？耐克可以生产500万条这样的腕带，每条售价1美元，并将所有收益捐赠给兰斯·阿姆斯特朗基金会。

The second idea was a wristband. Nike had recently begun selling Baller Bands, silicone rubber bands with inspirational messages like “TEAM” or “RESPECT” on the inside. Basketball players wore them to stay focused and increase motivation. Why not make a wristband focused on Armstrong? Nike could make 5 million of the bands, sell them for a dollar each, and give all the proceeds to the Lance Armstrong Foundation.

麦克伊彻恩很喜欢腕带这个想法，但当他向兰斯的顾问们提出时，他们并不信服。基金会认为这些腕带会失败。阿姆斯特朗的经纪人比尔·斯台普顿认为它们毫无成功的机会，并称它们“真是个愚蠢的想法。就连阿姆斯特朗都难以置信，他说：“我们卖不出去的 490 万辆怎么办？”

MacEachern liked the wristband idea, but when he pitched it to Lance’s advisors they weren’t convinced. The foundation thought the bands would be a dud. Bill Stapleton, Armstrong’s agent, thought they had no chance of success and called them “a stupid idea.” Even Armstrong was incredulous, saying, “What are we going to do with the 4.9 million that we don’t sell?”

麦克伊彻恩陷入了困境。虽然他很喜欢腕带这个想法，但他并不确定它能否成功。然而，他做出了一个看似无关紧要的决定，却对产品的成功产生了巨大的影响。麦克伊彻恩将腕带设计成了黄色。

MacEachern was stuck. While he liked the wristband idea, he wasn’t sure it would fly. But then he made one seemingly innocuous decision that had a big impact on the product’s success. MacEachern made the wristbands yellow.

—————

—————

之所以选择黄色，是因为它是环法自行车赛领骑衫的颜色。而且，黄色并不带有明显的性别特征，因此男女皆宜穿着。

Yellow was chosen because it is the color of the race leader’s jersey in the Tour de France. It’s also not strongly associated with either gender, making it easy for both men and women to wear.

但从可观察性的角度来看，这也是一个明智的决定。黄色是一种人们几乎看不到的颜色。

But it was also a smart decision from an observability perspective. Yellow is a color people almost never see.

而且它非常醒目。黄色几乎与人们穿的任何颜色都能形成鲜明对比，因此即使从远处也能轻易看到Livestrong腕带。

And it is striking. Yellow stands out against almost anything people wear, making it easy to see a Livestrong wristband from far away.

这种公众曝光度助力产品取得了巨大成功。耐克不仅在上市后的前六个月就售出了首批500万条腕带，产量根本无法满足需求。这些腕带如此抢手，以至于人们开始在eBay上以零售价十倍的价格竞拍。最终，腕带的销量超过了8500万条。你甚至可能认识一些至今仍在佩戴这款腕带的人。对于一块小小的塑料来说，这成绩相当不错。

This public visibility helped make the product a huge success. Not only did Nike sell the first 5 million bands, but it did so within the first six months of release. Production couldn’t keep up with demand. The wristbands were such a hot item that people started bidding ten times the retail price to snag them on eBay. In the end, more than 85 million wristbands were sold. You might even know someone who wears one to this day. Not bad for a little piece of plastic.

很难说如果耐克当时实施了横跨美国的骑行活动，效果会如何。事后诸葛亮很容易，说一个成功的策略显然行不通。更好的选择。但无论如何，有一点很明确：腕带产生的行为影响比长途骑行要大得多。正如麦克伊彻恩敏锐地指出的那样：

It’s hard to know how well the ride across America would have done if Nike had implemented it. And it’s easy to Monday-morning-quarterback a successful strategy and say it was obviously the better choice. But regardless, one thing is clear: the wristband creates more behavioral residue than the cross-country ride ever could have. As MacEachern keenly noted:

手环的好处在于它能长久保存。骑行活动本身却不会。人们会拍下骑行的照片，也会谈论骑行，但除非每年都举办——即便每年都举办，它也无法像手环那样，每天都提醒我们曾经做过这件事。但手环却可以。

The nice thing about a wristband is that it lives on. The bike ride doesn’t. There’ll be pictures of the bike ride and people will talk about the bike ride, but unless it goes on every year—even if it does go on every year, it doesn’t live on as a reminder every day of this sort of stuff. But the wristband does.

行为残留是指大多数行为或举动在其身后留下的物理痕迹或遗迹。推理小说爱好者的书架上摆满了推理小说。政治家们会把与著名政治人物握手的照片裱起来。跑步爱好者会收藏参加5公里跑的奖杯、T恤或奖牌。

Behavioral residue is the physical traces or remnants that most actions or behaviors leave in their wake. Mystery lovers have shelves full of mystery novels. Politicos frame photos of themselves shaking hands with famous politicians. Runners have trophies, T-shirts, or medals from participating in 5Ks.

正如“社交货币”一章中所讨论的，像Livestrong腕带这样的物品能够帮助我们了解人们的身份和喜好。甚至包括一些原本难以观察的信息，例如一个人是否向某个特定慈善机构捐款，或者更喜欢悬疑小说还是历史小说。
