As discussed in the chapter on Social Currency, items like the Livestrong wristband provide insight into who people are and what they like. Even things that would otherwise be difficult to observe, like whether a person donates to a particular cause or prefers mysteries to historical fiction.

但当这些遗迹公开可见时，它们会助长模仿，并为人们谈论相关产品或想法提供机会。

But when publicly visible, these remnants facilitate imitation and provide chances for people to talk about related products or ideas.

以投票为例。要让人们积极参与投票并非易事。他们必须弄清楚投票站的位置，请假半天，然后排队等候，有时甚至要排上几个小时，才能有机会投出自己的一票。但这些障碍还因为投票是一种私人行为而变得更加复杂。除非你亲眼看到所有去投票的人，否则你根本无法知道有多少人认为投票值得付出努力。因此，缺乏社会认同。

Take voting. It’s hard to get people to turn out to vote. They have to figure out where their polling stations are located, take the morning off from work, and stand in line, sometimes for hours, until they get the chance to cast their ballots. But these hurdles are compounded by the fact that voting is a private act. Unless you actually happen to see all the people who go to the polls, you have no idea how many other people decided voting was worth the effort. So there is not much social proof.

但在20世纪80年代，选举官员想出了一个巧妙的办法，让投票行为更加引人注目：“我已投票”贴纸。这方法虽然简单，但通过留下行为痕迹，即使人们离开投票站后，这种贴纸也能将原本私密的投票行为公开化。它时刻提醒着人们：今天是投票日，其他人都在投票，你也应该去投票。

But in the 1980s election officials came up with a nice way to make voting more observable: the “I Voted” sticker. Simple enough, but by creating behavioral residue, the sticker made the private act of voting much more public, even after people left the polling station. It provided a ready reminder that today is the day to vote, others are doing it, and you should too.

—————

—————

行为残留效应存在于所有类型的产品和理念中。蒂芙尼、维多利亚的秘密以及许多其他零售商都会向顾客提供一次性购物袋，方便他们将购买的商品带回家。但由于某些零售商所代表的社会地位，许多消费者会重复使用这些购物袋，而不是将其丢弃。他们会用维多利亚的秘密的购物袋装运动服，用蒂芙尼的购物袋装午餐，或者用布鲁明戴尔百货公司著名的棕色中号购物袋装文件。人们甚至会重复使用餐厅、折扣店和其他一些并非身份象征场所的购物袋。

Behavioral residue exists for all types of products and ideas. Tiffany, Victoria’s Secret, and a host of other retailers give customers disposable shopping bags to carry their purchases home. But because of the Social Currency associated with some of these retailers, many consumers reuse the bags rather than tossing them. They use the Victoria’s Secret bags to carry their gym clothes, toss their lunch into a Tiffany bag, or use Bloomingdale’s famous medium brown bag to carry papers around town. People even reuse bags from restaurants, discount stores, and other places that are not status symbols.

服装零售商 Lululemon 将这一理念更进一步。它不生产相对耐用的纸袋，而是生产不易丢弃的购物袋。这些购物袋采用类似可重复使用购物袋的结实塑料制成，显然是为方便重复使用而设计的。因此，人们用它们来装杂货或办其他事。而这种行为的持续性也为品牌提供了社会认同。

Clothing retailer Lululemon takes this idea one step further. Rather than make paper bags that are relatively durable, it makes shopping bags that are hard to throw away. Made of sturdy plastic like reusable grocery bags, these bags are clearly meant to be reused. So people use them to carry groceries or do other errands. But along the way this behavioral residue helps provide social proof for the brand.

赠品还能产生行为残留效应。去参加任何会议、招聘会或大型活动，只要演讲者设立了展位，你都会被他们赠送的礼品数量之多震惊。马克杯、钢笔、T恤衫，还有饮料保温套、压力球和冰铲。几年前，沃顿商学院甚至还送了我一条领带。

Giveaways can also provide behavioral residue. Go to any conference, job fair, or large meeting where presenters have set up booths and you’ll be stunned by the amount of swag they give away. Mugs, pens, and T-shirts. Beverage cozies, stress balls, and ice scrapers. A couple of years ago the Wharton School even gave me a tie.

但有些赠品比其他赠品更能产生行为上的后续影响。赠送化妆包固然不错，但女性通常在私密的浴室里化妆，因此品牌曝光度不高。咖啡杯和健身包的使用频率可能较低，但它们的使用场景更容易被公众看到。

But some of these giveaways provide better behavioral residue than others. Giving away a makeup carrying case is fine, but women usually apply makeup in the privacy of their bathrooms, so it doesn’t make the brand that observable. Coffee mugs and gym bags might be used less frequently, but their use is more publicly visible.

人们在网上发布的观点和行为也会留下行为痕迹。评论、博客、帖子或其他类型的内容都会留下证据，供他人日后查找。因此，许多企业和组织鼓励人们在Facebook上点赞他们或他们的内容。只需点击“赞”按钮，人们不仅表达了对某个产品、理念或组织的喜爱，还有助于传播信息，让更多人知道某件事物值得关注或值得重视。ABC新闻发现……安装这些按钮后，其 Facebook 流量增长了 250%。

People posting their opinions and behavior online also provide behavioral residue. Reviews, blogs, posts, or other sorts of content all leave evidence that others can find later. For this reason, many businesses and organizations encourage people to Like them—or their content—on Facebook. By simply clicking the Like button, people not only show their affinity with a product, idea, or organization, they also help spread the word that something is good or worth paying attention to. ABC News found that installing these buttons boosted its Facebook traffic by 250 percent.

其他网站会将用户的活动推送或自动发布到他们的社交网络页面上。音乐一直以来都带有一定的社交属性，而 Spotify 更进一步。该系统不仅允许你收听任何你喜欢的歌曲，还会将你正在收听的内容发布到你的 Facebook 页面上，方便你的朋友了解你的喜好（并让他们知道 Spotify）。许多其他网站也采用了类似的功能。

Other sites push, or automatically post, what people do to their social networking pages. Music has always been a somewhat social activity, but Spotify takes this a step further. The system allows you to listen to whatever songs you like but also posts what you’re listening to on your Facebook page, making it easier for your friends to see what you like (and letting them know about Spotify). Many other websites do the same.

但是我们应该总是把事情公开吗？有没有一些情况下，公开某些事情反而会适得其反？

But should we always try to make things public? Are there ever instances when making something public could be a bad idea?

反毒品广告？

ANTI-DRUG COMMERCIALS?

一位活泼的黑发少女走下公寓楼的楼梯。她戴着一条漂亮的银项链，她手里拿着一件毛衣。她可能正要去上班，也可能要去和朋友喝咖啡。突然，邻居家的门开了，一个声音低声说：“我给你弄了点好货。”

A sprightly, dark-haired teenager walks down the stairs of her apartment building. She’s wearing a pretty silver necklace and carrying a sweater in her hand. She could be on her way to work or to meet up with a friend for coffee. Suddenly a neighbor’s door opens and a voice whispers, “I got some good pot for you.”

“不！”她皱着眉头，匆匆下楼。

“No!” She scowls and hurries down the stairs.

一个面容稚嫩的男孩坐在外面。他穿着蓝色运动衫，留着以前男孩间流行的锅盖头。他正全神贯注地玩着电子游戏，这时一个声音打断了他。“可卡因？”那声音问道。“不用了，谢谢。”男孩回答道。

A fresh-faced kid is sitting outside. He is wearing a blue sweatshirt and sports a bowl haircut that used to be popular among boys. He appears deeply engrossed in a video game when a voice interrupts him. “Cocaine?” the voice asks. “No thanks,” the kid replies.

一个年轻男子靠墙站着，嚼着口香糖。“哟，哥们儿，想来点儿安眠药吗？”一个声音问道。“想都别想！”男子怒目而视，大声说道。

A young man is standing against a wall chewing gum. “Yo, my man, want some ’ludes?” the voice inquires. “No way!” the man exclaims, glaring back.

“对毒品说不”是有史以来最著名的反毒品运动之一。该运动由第一夫人南希·里根在其丈夫担任总统期间发起，在20世纪80年代和90年代，作为一项旨在劝阻青少年吸食娱乐性毒品的全国性行动的一部分，投放了公益广告。

“Just Say No” is one of the most famous anti-drug campaigns of all time. Created by First Lady Nancy Reagan during her husband’s presidency, the campaign ran public service announcements as part of a national effort to discourage teens from recreational drug use in the 1980s and 1990s.

逻辑很简单。孩子们迟早都会被问到是否想吸毒，无论是朋友、陌生人还是其他人。他们需要知道如何拒绝。因此，政府花费数百万美元制作禁毒公益广告，希望这些广告能教会孩子们如何应对这种情况，从而减少毒品使用。

The logic was simple. One way or another, kids are going to be asked if they want to use drugs. Whether by a friend, a stranger, or somebody else. And they needed to know how to say no. So the government spent millions of dollars on anti-drug public service announcements. It hoped that the messages would teach kids how to react in these situations and, as a result, decrease drug use.

近期的宣传活动也沿用了同样的理念。1998年至2004年间，国会拨款近10亿美元用于“全国青少年反毒品媒体宣传活动”。该活动旨在教育12至18岁的青少年，使他们能够远离毒品。

More recent campaigns have relied on the same idea. Between 1998 and 2004, Congress appropriated almost $1 billion for the National Youth Anti-Drug Media Campaign. The goal was to educate kids ages twelve to eighteen to enable them to reject drugs.

传播学教授鲍勃·霍尼克想看看他想了解禁毒广告是否真的有效。于是，他收集了数千名青少年在禁毒广告宣传期间的吸毒数据。广告投放后，他调查了青少年是否看过这些广告，以及他们是否吸食过大麻。然后，他分析了这些公益广告是否能降低大麻的使用率。

Communications professor Bob Hornik wanted to see whether anti-drug ads were actually effective. So he collected data on the drug use of thousands of teens over the time the anti-drug ads ran. Whether teens had seen the ads and whether they had ever smoked marijuana. Then he looked at whether the public service announcements seemed to decrease marijuana use.

他们没有。

They didn’t.

事实上，这些信息似乎反而增加了毒品使用。12岁半到18岁的青少年如果看过这些广告，吸食大麻的可能性反而更高。为什么呢？

In fact, the messages actually seemed to increase drug use. Kids aged twelve and a half to eighteen who saw the ads were actually more likely to smoke marijuana. Why?

因为它让吸毒行为更加公开化。

Because it made drug use more public.

想想可观察性和社会认同。在看到这条信息之前，有些孩子可能从未想过吸毒。另一些孩子可能考虑过，但一直担心做错事。

Think about observability and social proof. Before seeing the message, some kids might never have thought about taking drugs. Others might have considered it but have been wary about doing the wrong thing.

但禁毒广告往往同时传达两种信息。它们一方面说毒品不好，另一方面又说其他人都在吸毒。正如我们在本章讨论过的，当其他人似乎都在做某件事时，人们就越有可能认为这件事是正确的、正常的，也是他们应该做的。

But anti-drug ads often say two things simultaneously. They say that drugs are bad, but they also say that other people are doing them. And as we’ve discussed throughout this chapter, the more others seem to be doing something, the more likely people are to think that thing is right or normal and what they should be doing as well.

想象一下，你是一个十五岁的少年，从未想过要碰毒品。一天下午，你正坐在家里看动画片，突然电视上播放了一则公益广告，告诉你吸毒的危害。有人会问你想不想试试毒品，你必须做好拒绝的准备。更糟糕的是，问你的可能是那些酷小孩。但你绝对不能答应。

Imagine you’re a fifteen-year-old who has never considered using drugs. You’re sitting at home watching cartoons one afternoon when a public service announcement comes on telling you about the dangers of drug use. Someone’s going to ask you if you want to try drugs and you need to be ready to say no. Or even worse, the cool kids are going to be the ones asking. But you shouldn’t say yes.

你从没见过政府发布过如何避免用锯子锯断手或如何避免被公交车撞到的公益广告，所以如果政府花时间和金钱来宣传毒品，那你的很多同龄人肯定都在吸毒，对吧？他们当中有些人显然还是学校里最酷的孩子呢。而你却一无所知！

You never see public service announcements for avoiding cutting off your hand with a saw or not getting hit by a bus, so if the government spent the time and money to tell you about drugs, a lot of your peers must be doing them, right? Some of them are apparently the coolest kids in school. And you had no idea!

正如霍尼克所说，

As Hornik said,

我们的基本假设是，孩子们看到的这类广告越多，就越会相信很多其他孩子都在吸食大麻。而他们越相信其他孩子都在吸食大麻，就越会对吸食大麻产生兴趣。

Our basic hypothesis is that the more kids saw these ads, the more they came to believe that lots of other kids were using marijuana. And the more they came to believe that other kids were using marijuana, the more they became interested in using it themselves.

就像许多强大的工具一样，如果使用不当，公开信息也会产生意想不到的后果。如果你想让人们不要做某件事，就不要告诉他们很多同龄人都在做这件事。

As with many powerful tools, making things more public can have unintended consequences when not applied carefully. If you want to get people not to do something, don’t tell them that lots of their peers are doing it.

以音乐产业为例。它原本以为只要向人们展示非法下载问题的严重性就能阻止这种行为。因此，该行业协会的网站严厉警告人们，“美国消费者购买的音乐中只有37%是付费的”，而且在过去几年里，“大约有……”300亿首歌曲被非法下载。

Take the music industry. It thought it could stop illegal downloads by showing people how big the problem is. So the industry association’s website sternly warns people that “only 37 percent of music acquired by U.S. consumers . . . was paid for” and that in the past few years “approximately 30 billion songs were illegally downloaded.”

但我不太确定这条信息是否达到了预期效果。如果真要说有什么影响的话，那可能恰恰相反。只有不到一半的人会付费听音乐？哇。这么说来，只有傻子才会付费听音乐，对吧？

But I’m not sure that message has the desired effect. If anything, it may have the opposite effect. Less than half of people are paying for their music? Wow. Seems like you’d have to be an idiot to pay for it then, right?

即使在大多数人都在做正确的事情的情况下，谈论少数做错事的人也会鼓励人们屈服于诱惑。

Even in cases where most people are doing the right thing, talking about the minority who are doing the wrong thing can encourage people to give in to temptation.

与其将私密信息公开，不如采取相反的做法来阻止某种行为：将公开信息私密化，使他人的行为更难被观察到。

Rather than making the private public, preventing a behavior requires the opposite: making the public private. Making others’ behavior less observable.

一种方法是强调人们应该做什么。心理学家鲍勃·西奥迪尼及其同事希望减少……的数量。有人从亚利桑那州石化森林国家公园偷走了石化木。于是，公园周围张贴了各种告示，试图阻止这种情况发生。其中一条告示呼吁人们不要带走石化木，因为“很多以前的游客都曾带走过石化木”。从公园里偷窃，改变了石化森林的自然状态。”但是，通过提供其他人也在偷窃的社会证据，这一信息产生了适得其反的效果，几乎使偷窃木材的人数翻了一番！

One way is to highlight what people should be doing instead. Psychologist Bob Cialdini and colleagues wanted to decrease the number of people who stole petrified wood from Arizona’s Petrified Forest National Park. So they posted signs around the park that tried different strategies. One asked people not to take the wood because “many past visitors have removed petrified wood from the Park, changing the natural state of the Petrified Forest.” But by providing social proof that others were stealing, the message had a perverse effect, almost doubling the number of people taking wood!

强调人们应该做什么远比指责别人做什么有效得多。他们在另一组步道上尝试了不同的标牌，上面写着：“为了保护石化森林的自然状态，请勿将石化木从公园带走。” 通过强调不带走木材的积极影响，而不是指责其他人的行为，公园管理处成功地减少了盗窃行为。

Highlighting what people should do was much more effective. Over a different set of trails they tried a different sign that stated, “Please don’t remove the petrified wood from the Park, in order to preserve the natural state of the Petrified Forest.” By focusing on the positive effects of not taking the wood, rather than on what others were doing, the park service was able to reduce theft.

—————

—————

有人说，当人们可以自由行事时，他们通常会互相模仿。我们会从他人那里获取信息，以判断在特定情况下什么是正确的或有益的做法，这种社会认同影响着我们生活的方方面面，从我们购买的产品到我们投票支持的候选人。

It’s been said that when people are free to do as they please, they usually imitate one another. We look to others for information about what is right or good to do in a given situation, and this social proof shapes everything from the products we buy to the candidates we vote for.

但正如我们之前讨论的，“有样学样”不仅仅反映了我们模仿他人的倾向。如果人们看不到别人在做什么，就无法模仿。因此，为了让我们的产品和理念流行起来，我们需要让它们更容易被公众看到。对苹果公司来说，这就像翻转一下它的标志一样简单。通过巧妙地利用胡须元素，Movember（胡须月）吸引了大量关注，并为男性癌症研究筹集了大量捐款。

But as we discussed, the phrase “Monkey see, monkey do” captures more than just our tendency to follow others. If people can’t see what others are doing, they can’t imitate them. So to get our products and ideas to become popular we need to make them more publicly observable. For Apple this was as easy as flipping its logo. By cleverly leveraging moustaches, Movember drew huge attention and donations for men’s cancer research.

所以我们需要像Hotmail和苹果那样，设计出能够自我宣传的产品。我们需要像Lululemon和Livestrong那样，创造行为残留，创造可辨识的证据，即使人们使用过我们的产品或参与过我们的理念之后，这些证据仍然会存在。我们需要将私密信息公开。如果某样东西是为了展示而设计的，那么它就注定会发展壮大。

So we need to be like Hotmail and Apple and design products that advertise themselves. We need to be like Lululemon and Livestrong and create behavioral residue, discernible evidence that sticks around even after people have used our product or engaged with our ideas. We need to make the private public. If something is built to show, it’s built to grow.

*将公共领域转化为私人领域，对于那些人们原本可能不愿公开谈论的话题来说尤为重要。以网上约会为例。许多人都尝试过，但它在主流文化中仍然带有某种程度的污名。这种污名部分源于人们并不知道他们认识的许多人都尝试过。网上约会是一种相对私密的行为，因此，为了使其流行起来，在线约会公司需要让人们更多地了解有多少人正在这样做。类似的问题也出现在其他领域。伟哥的生产商创造了“ED”（勃起功能障碍）一词，旨在让人们更自在地谈论这个曾经的私密话题。许多大学发起了“如果你是同性恋就穿牛仔裤”的活动日，部分原因也是为了提高人们对LGBT群体的认识，并促进相关讨论。

* Making the public private is particularly important for things that people may not have originally felt comfortable talking about. Take online dating. Many people have tried it, but it is still somewhat stigmatized in the culture at large. And part of this stigma is due to the fact that people are unaware that many people they know have tried it. Online dating is relatively private behavior, so to help it catch on, online dating companies need to make people more aware how many others are doing it. Similar issues pop up in other domains. The makers of Viagra coined the term “ED” (erectile dysfunction) to get people more comfortable talking about what was once a private issue. Many colleges started a “wear jeans if you’re gay” day, in part just to raise awareness and discussion for the LGBT community.

5.实用价值

5. Practical Value

如果要你选一个能拍出爆款视频的人，肯·克雷格可能不会是你的首选。大多数爆款视频都是青少年制作，也都是青少年观看的。比如有人骑摩托车表演各种疯狂特技，或者把卡通人物剪辑成跳说唱舞的样子。这些都是年轻人喜欢的东西。

If you had to pick someone to make a viral video, Ken Craig probably wouldn’t be your first choice. Most viral videos are made by adolescents and watched by adolescents. Crazy tricks someone did on his motorcycle or cartoon characters edited to look as if they are dancing to rap songs. Things young people love.

但肯·克雷格已经86岁了。而那段爆红的视频呢？是关于剥玉米的。

But Ken Craig is eighty-six years old. And the video that went viral? It’s about shucking corn.

肯出生在俄克拉荷马州的一个农场，家中兄弟姐妹五人。他们家以种植棉花为生，也种菜为生，其中就包括玉米。肯从上世纪20年代就开始吃玉米了，从玉米砂锅、玉米浓汤到玉米饼、玉米沙拉，各种做法他都吃过。他最喜欢的吃法之一就是直接从玉米棒上剥下来吃，鲜嫩可口。

Ken was born on a farm in Oklahoma, one of five brothers and sisters. His family’s livelihood was built around growing cotton. They also kept a garden to grow things for the family to eat. And among those things was corn. Ken’s been eating corn since the 1920s. He’s eaten everything from corn casserole and corn chowder to corn fritters and corn salad. One of his favorite ways to eat corn is straight off the cob. Nice and fresh.

但如果你吃过玉米棒，就会知道有两个问题。除了玉米粒会卡在牙缝里，还有那些讨厌的细丝（叫做玉米须）总是粘在玉米上。用力拉几下就能轻松剥掉外皮，但玉米须却怎么也剥不掉。天哪。你可以揉搓玉米粒，用镊子小心地挑出来，或者尝试几乎任何你喜欢的方法，但无论你做什么，似乎总会残留几根不听话的玉米须。

But if you’ve ever eaten corn on the cob you know that there are two problems. In addition to kernels getting stuck in your teeth, there are those pesky threadlike strands (called corn silk) that always seem stuck to the corn. A couple of strong pulls and you can easily peel the husk off, but the silk seems to cling on for dear life. You can rub the corn, carefully pick at it with tweezers, or try almost anything else you like, but whatever you do there always seem to be a couple of wayward silk strands left over.

这时肯就出场了。

And this is where Ken comes in.

和大多数八十六岁的老人一样，肯对互联网并不感兴趣。他没有博客，没有YouTube频道，也没有任何形式的网络存在感。事实上，直到今天，他只制作过一个YouTube视频。真的，一个都没有。

Like most eighty-six-year-olds, Ken’s not really into the Internet. He doesn’t have a blog, a channel on YouTube, or any sort of online presence. In fact, to this day he has made only one YouTube video. Ever.

几年前，肯的儿媳妇来他家做晚饭。主菜快做好时，快到吃饭时间了，她告诉肯玉米可以剥皮了。肯说，好的，不过我教你个小窍门。

A couple of years ago, Ken’s daughter-in-law was over at his house making dinner. She had almost finished cooking the main dish, and when it got close to time to eat, she told him that the corn was ready to be shucked. Okay, Ken said, but let me show you a little trick.

他拿来几根没剥皮的玉米棒，放进微波炉里，每根加热四分钟。加热好后，他用菜刀切掉底部大约半英寸（1.3厘米）。然后他抓住玉米棒顶端的苞叶，轻轻抖了几下，玉米棒就掉了出来。干净得一尘不染，没有丝丝。

He took unshucked ears of corn and tossed them in a microwave. Four minutes an ear. Once they were done, he took a kitchen knife and cut a half inch or so off the bottom. Then he grabbed the husk at the top of the corn, gave it a quick couple of shakes, and out popped the ear of corn. Clean as a whistle. No silk.

他的儿媳妇非常佩服，说他们得拍个视频发给她在韩国教英语的女儿。于是第二天，她就拍了一段肯在厨房里讲解他清洗玉米棒的秘诀的视频。为了方便女儿观看，她把视频上传到了YouTube。期间，她还把这段视频发给了几个朋友。

His daughter-in-law was so impressed she said they’d have to make a video to send to her daughter who was teaching English in Korea. So the next day she shot a clip of Ken in his kitchen, talking through his trick for clean ears of corn. To make it easier for her daughter to see, she posted it on YouTube. And along the way she sent the clip to a couple of friends.

就这样，那些朋友把视频转发给了几个朋友，那几个朋友又转发给了几个朋友。很快，肯的“每次都洗干净耳朵”视频就火了起来，点击量超过了500万。

Well, those friends sent it to a couple of friends, who also sent it to a couple of friends. Soon Ken’s “Clean Ears Everytime” video took off. It collected more than 5 million views.

但与大多数主要面向年轻人的病毒视频不同，这个视频的受众群体恰恰相反，在55岁以上人群观看次数最多的视频排行榜上名列榜首。事实上，如果更多老年人上网，这段视频的传播速度可能会更快。

But unlike most viral videos that skew toward young people, this one skewed in the opposite direction. Topping the charts of the videos viewed most by people above the age of fifty-five. In fact, the video might have spread even faster if more senior citizens were online.

人们为什么会分享这段视频？

Why did people share this video?

—————

—————

几年前，我和哥哥去北卡罗来纳州的山区徒步旅行。当时他正结束医学院一年的艰苦学业，而我也需要休息一下，所以我们在罗利-达勒姆机场碰面，然后驱车向西。我们途经教堂山那片焦油脚跟蓝的田园风光，经过曾经烟草遍地的温斯顿-塞勒姆，一路来到环绕北卡罗来纳州最西端的蓝岭山脉。第二天清晨，我们早早起床，打包好当天的食物，沿着蜿蜒的山脊小径出发，最终到达了雄伟高原的顶峰。

A couple of years ago I went hiking with my brother in the mountains of North Carolina. He was wrapping up a tough year of medical school, and I needed a break from work, so we met at Raleigh-Durham Airport and drove west. Past the Tar Heel blue of Chapel Hill, past the once tobacco-saturated city of Winston-Salem, and all the way to the Blue Ridge Mountains that hug the westernmost portion of the state. The next morning we woke up early, packed food for the day, and set out on a winding mountain ridge path that led to the top of a majestic plateau.

人们徒步旅行的主要原因是为了远离尘嚣，逃离城市的喧嚣，沉浸于大自然之中。没有广告牌，没有车流，没有宣传，只有你和大自然。

The main reason people go hiking is to get away from it all. To escape from the hustle and bustle of the city and to immerse themselves in nature. No billboards, no traffic, no advertising, just you and nature.

那天早上我们在树林里徒步时，遇到了一件非常奇怪的事情。当我们沿着下坡路绕过弯道时，前面有一群徒步者。我们跟在他们后面走了几分钟，出于好奇，我无意中听到了他们的谈话。我以为他们可能在谈论美好的天气，或者我们刚刚走过的漫长下坡路。

But that morning while we were hiking in the woods, we came across the most peculiar thing. As we rounded the bend on a downhill portion of the trail there was a group of hikers in front of us. We walked behind them for a couple of minutes, and being a curious guy, I happened to eavesdrop on their conversation. I thought they might be talking about the beautiful weather, or the long descent we had just covered.

但事实并非如此。

But they weren’t.

他们在谈论吸尘器。

They were talking about vacuum cleaners.

某个型号是否真的物有所值，而另一个型号是否也能同样出色地完成任务。

Whether one particular model was really worth its premium price, and whether another model would do the job just as well.

吸尘器？这些徒步旅行者还有成千上万其他话题可以聊。比如，在哪里停下来吃午饭。他们刚刚走过的六十英尺高的瀑布，甚至政治，都比不上它。但是吸尘器呢？

Vacuum cleaners? There were thousands of other things these hikers could have talked about. Where to stop for lunch, the rushing sixty-foot falls they had just passed, even politics. But vacuum cleaners?

—————

—————

用本书目前讨论过的维度来解释肯·克雷格的爆款玉米视频并不容易，但要解释徒步旅行者们关于吸尘器的闲聊就更难了。他们谈论的并非什么特别引人注目的话题，因此社交货币并没有发挥多大作用。虽然在家里，甚至在城市里，有很多关于吸尘器的线索，但在森林里，关于吸尘器的触发因素却不多。最后，虽然巧妙的营销活动可以找到如何让吸尘器更具情感吸引力的方法，但徒步旅行者们只是在就不同吸尘器的功能进行简单的对话。那么，是什么驱使他们谈论这些呢？

It’s not easy to explain Ken Craig’s viral corn video using the dimensions we’ve talked about so far in this book, but it’s even harder to explain the hikers chatting about vacuum cleaners. They weren’t talking about anything particularly remarkable, so Social Currency wasn’t playing much of a role. While there are lots of cues for vacuums in the home, or even in a city, there aren’t many Triggers for vacuum cleaners in the forest. Finally, while a clever campaign could figure out how to make vacuum cleaners more Emotional, the hikers were just having a basic conversation about features different vacuums offered. So what was driving them to talk?

答案很简单。人们喜欢分享实用、有用的信息，也就是其他人能用得上的新闻。

The answer is simple. People like to pass along practical, useful information. News others can use.

在《触发》或《请勿告知》这类隐藏酒吧的语境下，实用价值或许并非最吸引人或最令人兴奋的概念。有些人甚至会说它显而易见或不言而喻。但这并不意味着它无关紧要。当作家兼编辑小威廉·F·巴克利被问及如果只能带一本书去荒岛，他会带哪一本时，他的回答直截了当：“一本关于造船的书。”

In the context of Triggers or hidden bars like Please Don’t Tell, practical value may not seem like the sexiest or most exciting concept. Some might even say it’s obvious or intuitive. But that doesn’t mean that it’s not consequential. When writer and editor William F. Buckley Jr. was asked which single book he would take with him to a desert island, his reply was straightforward: “A book on shipbuilding.”

有用的东西很重要。

Useful things are important.

此外，正如肯的玉米故事和徒步旅行者讨论吸尘器的故事所表明的那样，人们不仅重视实用信息，而且还乐于分享。提供实用价值有助于信息的传播。

Further, as the stories of Ken’s corn and the vacuum-discussing hikers illustrate, people don’t just value practical information, they share it. Offering practical value helps make things contagious.

—————

—————

人们分享具有实际价值的信息来帮助他人。无论是为朋友节省时间，还是确保同事节省时间。下次他去超市的时候带上几块钱，有用的信息很有帮助。

People share practically valuable information to help others. Whether by saving a friend time or ensuring a colleague saves a couple of bucks next time he goes to the supermarket, useful information helps.

这样看来，分享实用价值的内容就像现代版的“谷仓建造”。谷仓是大型且造价昂贵的建筑，单个家庭很难独自承担建造或建造的费用。因此，在十八、十九世纪，社区会集体为社区成员建造谷仓。人们聚集在一起，贡献时间，帮助邻里。下次，谷仓又会为其他人建造。你可以把它看作是如今“传递爱心”这一亲社会理念的早期版本。

In this way, sharing practically valuable content is like a modern-day barn raising. Barns are large and costly structures that are difficult for one family to pay for or to assemble by itself. So in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, communities would collectively build a barn for one of their members. People would get together, volunteer their time, and help their neighbor. Next time around, the barn would be built for someone else. You can think of it as an early version of the current prosocial ideal of “pay it forward.”

如今，直接帮助他人的机会越来越少。现代郊区生活使我们与朋友和邻居渐行渐远。我们住在长长的车道尽头或高层公寓里，往往对隔壁邻居都知之甚少。许多人为了工作或求学而远离家人，减少了与最亲密的社交圈面对面交流的机会。雇佣劳动力取代了社区共同建造谷仓的传统。

Today, these direct opportunities to help others are fewer and farther between. Modern suburban life has distanced us from our friends and neighbors. We live at the end of long driveways or high up in apartment buildings, often barely getting to know the person next door. Many people move away from their families for work or school, reducing face-to-face contact with our strongest social ties. Hired labor has taken the place of community barn raising.

但与他人分享有用的东西是一种快速简便的帮助方式，即使我们身处异地。父母即使远隔千里，也能给孩子提供有用的建议。分享有用的东西也能加强社交联系。如果我们知道朋友喜欢烹饪，把我们找到的新食谱分享给他们，就能拉近彼此的距离。朋友们会感受到我们的关心和了解，我们也会因为帮助他人而感到快乐，而这种分享也巩固了我们的友谊。

But sharing something useful with others is a quick and easy way to help them out. Even if we’re not in the same place. Parents can send their kids helpful advice even if they are hundreds of miles away. Passing along useful things also strengthens social bonds. If we know our friends are into cooking, sending them a new recipe we found brings us closer together. Our friends see we know and care about them, we feel good for being helpful, and the sharing cements our friendship.

如果说社交货币关注的是信息发送者以及分享如何提升他们的形象，那么实用价值则主要关注信息的接收者。它关乎如何帮助人们节省时间或金钱，或者帮助他们获得良好的体验。当然，分享有用的信息对分享者自身也有益处。帮助他人会让人感到快乐。分享甚至还能提升分享者的声誉，带来一些社交资本。但从本质上讲，分享实用价值是为了帮助他人。“情感”一章指出，当我们关心他人时，我们会分享。反之亦然。分享即是关爱。

If Social Currency is about information senders and how sharing makes them look, Practical Value is mostly about the information receiver. It’s about saving people time or money, or helping them have good experiences. Sure, sharing useful things benefits the sharer as well. Helping others feels good. It even reflects positively on the sharer, providing a bit of Social Currency. But at its core, sharing practical value is about helping others. The Emotions chapter noted that when we care, we share. But the opposite is also true. Sharing is caring.

你可以把分享实用价值看作是提供建议。人们会讨论哪种退休计划最划算，哪位政治家能平衡预算，哪种药能治感冒，哪种蔬菜的β-胡萝卜素含量最高。想想你上次做决定时，是什么时候需要收集和筛选大量信息。你可能问过一两个人该怎么做。他们可能分享了自己的意见，或者给你发了一个对你有帮助的网站链接。

You can think about sharing practical value as akin to advice. People talk about which retirement plan is cheapest and which politician will balance the budget. Which medicine cures a cold and which vegetable has the most beta carotene. Think about the last time you made a decision that required you to gather and sift through large amounts of information. You probably asked one or more people what you should do. And they probably either shared their opinion or sent you a link to a website that helped you out.

那么，究竟是什么让某样东西看起来足够有价值，值得传承下去呢？

So what makes something seem practically valuable enough to pass along?

省几块钱

SAVING A COUPLE OF BUCKS

当大多数人想到实际价值时，省钱是首先想到的事情之一——以低于原价的价格买到东西，或者以相同的价格买到比平时更多的东西。

When most people think about practical value, saving money is one of the first things that comes to mind—getting something for less than its original price or getting more of something than you usually would for the same price.

像 Groupon 和 LivingSocial 这样的网站已经建立了以向消费者提供从修脚到飞行员课程等各种折扣为核心的商业模式。

Websites like Groupon and LivingSocial have built business models around offering consumers discounts on everything from pedicures to pilot lessons.

人们是否分享促销优惠的最大决定因素之一是优惠是否划算。如果我们看到非常划算的优惠，就会忍不住想和别人分享，或者转发给可能感兴趣的人。但如果优惠一般，我们就会选择自己保留。

One of the biggest drivers of whether people share promotional offers is whether the offer seems like a good deal. If we see an amazing deal we can’t help but talk about it or pass it on to someone we think would find it useful. If the offer is just okay, though, we keep it to ourselves.

那么，什么因素决定了促销活动是否划算呢？

So what determines whether or not a promotional offer seems like a good deal?

不出所料，折扣幅度的大小会影响人们对优惠的感知。例如，省下100美元往往比省下1美元更令人兴奋；省下50%比省下10%更让人激动。显而易见，人们更喜欢（也更乐于分享）更大的折扣，而不是更小的折扣。

Not surprisingly, the size of the discount influences how good a deal seems. Saving a hundred dollars, for example, tends to be more exciting than saving one dollar. Saving 50 percent is more exciting than saving 10 percent. You don’t have to be a brain surgeon to realize that people like (and share) bigger discounts more than smaller ones.

但实际情况要复杂得多。请考虑以下示例中你会怎么做：

But it’s actually more complicated than that. Consider what you would do in the following example:

情景A：想象一下，你正在商店里想买一台新的烧烤炉。你发现了一台Weber Q 320烧烤炉，看起来很不错，而且令你高兴的是，它还在打折。原价350美元，现在降价到250美元。

Scenario A: Imagine you’re at a store looking to buy a new barbecue grill. You find a Weber Q 320 grill that looks pretty good, and to your delight it’s also on sale. Originally priced at $350, it is now marked down to $250.

你会买这款烧烤炉，还是会开车去另一家店看看别的款式？花点时间想想你的答案。想好了吗？好，我们换一家零售商再做一遍这个练习。

Would you buy this barbecue grill or drive to another store to look at others? Take a second to think about your answer. Got it? Okay, let’s do the exercise again for a different retailer.

情景 B：想象一下，你正在商店里想买一台新的烧烤炉。你发现了一台 Weber Q 320 烧烤炉，看起来很不错，而且令你欣喜的是，它还在打折。原价 255 美元，现在降价到 240 美元。

Scenario B: Imagine you’re at a store looking to buy a new barbecue grill. You find a Weber Q 320 grill that looks pretty good and to your delight it’s also on sale. Originally priced at $255, it is now marked down to $240.

你会怎么做？你会买下这台烧烤炉，还是开车去另一家店看看别的款式？想好之后再继续阅读。

What would you do in this case? Would you buy this barbecue grill or drive to another store to look at others? Wait until you have an answer and then read on.

如果你和大多数人一样，方案A看起来相当不错。烧烤炉便宜一百美元，而且还是你喜欢的型号？听起来很划算。你可能早就决定买下它，而不是继续寻找其他方案了。

If you’re like most people, scenario A looked pretty good. One hundred dollars off a barbecue grill and it’s a model you like? Seems like a good deal. You probably said you’d buy it rather than keep looking.

然而，方案B可能看起来并不那么好。毕竟，只便宜了十五美元，远不如第一个优惠。你大概说过你会继续找找，而不是买这个。

Scenario B, however, probably didn’t look so good. After all, it’s only fifteen dollars off, nowhere near as good as the first deal. You probably said you’d keep looking rather than buy that one.

我分别向一百位不同的受访者展示了两种情景，结果类似。在接受情景A的受访者中，75%的人表示会购买烤架而不是继续寻找，而接受情景B的受访者中，只有22%的人表示会购买烤架。

I found similar results when I gave each scenario to one hundred different people. While 75 percent of the people who received scenario A said they’d buy the grill rather than keep looking, only 22 percent of people who received scenario B said they’d buy the grill.

这一切听起来都很有道理——直到你考虑到每家店的最终价格。两家店卖的都是同一款烤架。所以，按理说，人们应该更倾向于在价格更低的店（情景B）购买。但事实并非如此。实际上，情况恰恰相反。更多的人表示会在情景A下购买烤架，即使这意味着他们要支付更高的价格（250美元而不是240美元）。这是怎么回事呢？

This all makes perfect sense—until you think about the final price at each store. Both stores were selling the same grill. So if anything, people should have been more likely to say they would buy it at the store where the price was lower (scenario B). But they weren’t. In fact, the opposite happened. More people said they would purchase the grill in scenario A, even though they would have had to pay a higher price ($250 rather than $240) to get it. What gives?

交易心理学

THE PSYCHOLOGY OF DEALS

2002年12月一个寒冷的冬日，丹尼尔·卡尼曼走上瑞典斯德哥尔摩大学座无虚席的阶梯教室，向听众发表演讲。听众席上坐满了瑞典外交官、政要以及一些世界顶尖的学者。卡尼曼此行的目的是就有限理性发表演讲，这是一种关于直觉判断和选择的新视角。多年来，他曾多次就此主题发表演讲，但这一次略有不同。卡尼曼此次来到斯德哥尔摩是为了领取诺贝尔经济学奖。

On a cold, wintry day in December 2002, Daniel Kahneman walked onstage to address a packed lecture hall at Sweden’s Stockholm University. The audience was filled with Swedish diplomats, dignitaries, and some of the world’s most prominent academics. Kahneman was there to give a talk on bounded rationality, a new perspective on intuitive judgment and choice. He had given related talks over the years, but this one was slightly different. Kahneman was in Stockholm to accept the Nobel Prize in Economics.

诺贝尔奖是世界上最负盛名的奖项之一，授予那些在其学科领域做出卓越贡献的研究人员。阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦因其在理论物理学领域的工作而获得诺贝尔奖。沃森和克里克也曾获得诺贝尔奖。他们因在DNA结构方面的工作而获得诺贝尔医学奖。在经济学领域，诺贝尔奖授予那些其研究对推动经济学思想发展产生重大影响的人。

The Nobel Prize is one of the world’s most prestigious awards and is given to researchers who have contributed great insight to their disciplines. Albert Einstein received a Nobel Prize for his work on theoretical physics. Watson and Crick received a Nobel in medicine for their work on the structure of DNA. In economics, the Nobel Prize is awarded to a person whose research has had a large impact on advancing economic thinking.

但卡尼曼并非经济学家，而是一位心理学家。

But Kahneman isn’t an economist. He is a psychologist.

卡尼曼因与阿莫斯·特沃斯基共同提出的“前景理论”而荣获诺贝尔经济学奖。该理论内容极其丰富，但其核心却基于一个非常基本的理念：人们实际的决策方式往往违背了经济学中关于决策方式的标准假设。判断和决策并非总是理性或最优的，而是基于人们感知和处理信息的心理学原理。正如感知过程会影响我们对某件毛衣的判断（例如是否认为它是红色的）或对地平线上的物体的感知（例如是否认为它是远的），它们也会影响我们对价格高低或交易好坏的判断。卡尼曼和特沃斯基的研究与理查德·塞勒的研究一起，是最早探索我们现在所说的“行为经济学”的领域之一。

Kahneman received the Nobel for his work with Amos Tversky on what they called “prospect theory.” The theory is amazingly rich, but at its core, it’s based on a very basic idea. The way people actually make decisions often violates standard economic assumptions about how they should make decisions. Judgments and decisions are not always rational or optimal. Instead, they are based on psychological principles of how people perceive and process information. Just as perceptual processes influence whether we see a particular sweater as red or view an object on the horizon as far away, they also influence whether a price seems high or a deal seems good. Along with Richard Thaler’s work, Kahneman and Tversky’s research is some of the earliest studying what we now think of as “behavioral economics.”

—————

—————

前景理论的核心观点之一是，人们不会用绝对值来评价事物，而是会参照某个比较标准或“参考点”来评价。五毛钱一杯咖啡，不仅仅是五毛钱一杯咖啡。这个价格是否合理，取决于你的预期。如果你住在纽约，五毛钱一杯咖啡似乎很便宜。你会为自己的好运而窃笑，然后每天都去那家店买咖啡。你甚至可能会告诉你的朋友。

One of the main tenets of prospect theory is that people don’t evaluate things in absolute terms. They evaluate them relative to a comparison standard, or “reference point.” Fifty cents for coffee isn’t just fifty cents for coffee. Whether that seems like a fair price or not depends on your expectations. If you live in New York City, paying fifty cents for a cup of coffee seems pretty cheap. You’d chuckle at your good luck and buy coffee from that place every day. You might even tell your friends.

但如果你住在印度农村，50美分可能看起来贵得离谱。这远远超出你想象中买咖啡的价格，你肯定不会买。如果你跟朋友说起这件事，一定会义愤填膺地谴责这种哄抬物价的行为。

If you live in rural India, though, fifty cents might seem hugely expensive. It would be way more than you would dream of paying for coffee and you’d never buy it. If you told your friends anything it would be your outrage at the price gouging.

如果你和七八十岁的老人一起去看电影或逛商店，就会发现同样的现象。他们经常抱怨物价。“什么？”他们惊呼道，“我才不会花十一美元买张电影票呢！这简直是抢钱！”

You see the same phenomenon at work if you go to the movies or the store with people in their seventies or eighties. They often complain about the prices. “What?” they exclaim. “No way am I paying eleven dollars for a movie ticket. That’s such a rip-off!”

老年人似乎比我们其他人更吝啬。但他们觉得物价不公还有一个更根本的原因。他们的参照点不同。他们记得以前电影票只要40美分，牛排每磅95美分，牙膏29美分，纸巾10美分。正因如此，他们很难觉得现在的价格合理。物价似乎比他们记忆中的高得多，所以他们不愿付款。

It might seem that old people are stingier than the rest of us. But there is a more fundamental reason that they think the prices are unfair. They have different reference points. They remember the days when a movie ticket was forty cents and steak was ninety-five cents a pound, when toothpaste was twenty-nine cents and paper towels cost a dime. Because of that, it’s hard for them to see today’s prices as fair. The prices seem so much higher than what they remember, so they balk at paying them.

参考点有助于解释我们前几页讨论的烧烤炉案例。人们会以自己预期支付的价格作为参考点。因此，即使是同一款烧烤炉，从 350 美元降价到 250 美元也比从 255 美元降价到 240 美元更划算。这是因为人们设定了更高的参考点，使得前者看起来更划算，即使它的总价更高。

Reference points help explain the barbecue grill scenarios we discussed a few pages ago. People use the price they expect to pay for something as their reference point. So the grill seemed like a better deal when it was marked down from $350 to $250 rather than when it was discounted from $255 to $240, even though it was the same grill. Setting a higher reference point made the first deal seem better even though the price was higher overall.

电视购物广告通常也采用同样的方法。

Infomercials often use the same approach.

神奇的Miracle Blade刀具经久耐用！看看它们切开菠萝、汽水罐，甚至是一枚硬币！你可能觉得这样一套刀具要100美元甚至200美元，但现在你只需39.99美元就能拥有这套令人难以置信的刀具！

The amazing Miracle Blade knives last forever! Watch them slice through a pineapple, soda can, or even a penny! You might expect to pay $100 or even $200 for a set of knives like these, but right now you can get this incredible knife set for only $39.99!

听起来是不是很熟悉？的确如此。大多数电视购物广告都会使用这种手法，让他们的产品看起来非常划算。通过提及100美元或200美元作为预期价格，电视购物广告设定了一个较高的参考点，从而使最终的39.99美元价格显得格外便宜。

Sound familiar? It should. Most infomercials use this technique to make whatever they are offering seem like a great deal. By mentioning $100 or $200 as the price you might expect to pay, the infomercial sets a high reference point, making the final price of $39.99 seem like a steal.

这也是为什么零售商即使在促销时也常常会列出“常规”或厂商标准零售价的原因。他们希望消费者将这些价格作为参考价格，从而使促销价显得更具吸引力。消费者如此执着于寻找物美价廉的商品，以至于正如烧烤炉的例子所示，他们有时最终甚至会为此支付更高的价格。

This is also why retailers often list a “regular” or manufacturer’s standard retail price even when something is on sale. They want consumers to use those prices as the reference price, making the sale price look even better. Consumers are so focused on getting a good deal that, as the barbecue grill example showed, they sometimes even end up paying more to get it.

参考点也适用于数量。

Reference points also work with quantities.

等等，还有更多惊喜！如果您现在打电话，我们将免费赠送第二套刀具！没错，同样的价格，额外赠送一套。而且我们还会赠送这款便捷的磨刀器。完全免费！

But wait, there’s more! If you call now, we’ll throw in a second set of these knives absolutely free! That’s right, an extra set for the same price. And we’ll even throw in this handy knife sharpener. No extra charge!

这则电视购物广告以参考数量为基础，并在此基础上进行了增加。你原本以为一套 Miracle Blade 刀具的价格是 39.99 美元，但现在同样的价格，你不仅能买到一套刀具，还能额外获得一个磨刀器。除了价格低于你的预期（而你的预期正是他们最初设定的），额外的赠品也让这笔交易看起来更加划算。

Here the infomercial is taking the reference quantity and augmenting it. You expected to pay $39.99 for one set of Miracle Blade knives, but now you are getting an extra set, and a knife sharpener, for the same price. In addition to the price being lower than your expectations (which was set by them in the first place), the additional goods makes the offer seem like an even better deal.

—————

—————

促销活动究竟能带来多大的销售增长？市场营销科学家埃里克·安德森和邓肯·西梅斯特想要找到答案。几年前，他们与一家向全美各地家庭寄送服装目录的公司合作，例如LL Bean、Spiegel或Lands' End。这些目录中的大部分服装都是原价，但有时也会推出一些特价商品，降低价格。不出所料，这显著提升了销量。人们都喜欢物美价廉，所以降价会增强商品的吸引力。

How far will the effect of putting something on sale go? Marketing scientists Eric Anderson and Duncan Simester wanted to find out. So a few years ago they paired up with a company that sends clothing catalogs to homes across the United States. Think L.L. Bean, Spiegel, or Lands’ End. Most of the clothes in these catalogs are full price, but sometimes the catalog features certain sale items and drops its prices. Not surprisingly, this increases sales. People like to pay less, so dropping the price makes things more desirable.

但安德森和西梅斯特心中却想着另一个问题。他们想知道消费者是否觉得折扣的概念如此强大，以至于仅仅将商品贴上“促销”的标签就能增加购买量。

But Anderson and Simester had a different question in mind. They wondered whether consumers find the idea of a discount so powerful that merely labeling something as “on sale” would increase purchase.

为了验证这种可能性，安德森和西梅斯特制作了两个不同版本的商品目录，并分别寄给了超过五万名收件人。其中一个版本中，部分商品（我们姑且称之为连衣裙）贴有“季前促销”的标签。而另一个版本中，这些连衣裙则没有标明促销信息。

To test this possibility, Anderson and Simester created two different versions of the catalog and mailed each to more than fifty thousand people. In one version some of the products (let’s call them dresses) were marked with signs that said “Pre-Season SALE.” In the other version the dresses were not marked as on sale.

果不其然，将这些商品标价促销后，需求量确实增加了，增幅超过50%。

Sure enough, marking those items as on sale increased demand. By more than 50 percent.

关键是什么？

The kicker?

两版产品目录中连衣裙的价格相同。因此，即使价格本身没有变化，在价格旁边加上“促销”字样也能提高销量。

The prices of the dresses were the same in both versions of the catalog. So using the word “sale” beside a price increased sales even though the price itself stayed the same.

—————

—————

前景理论的另一个基本原则是所谓的“敏感度递减”。想象一下，你正在寻找……想买一台新的收音机闹钟。在你打算购买的商店里，你发现价格是35美元。店员告诉你，同一家商店的另一家分店有同样的商品，价格只要25美元。那家分店开车二十分钟就能到，店员向你保证那里肯定有你想要的商品。

Another tenet of prospect theory is something called “diminishing sensitivity.” Imagine you are looking to buy a new clock radio. At the store where you expect to buy it, you find that the price is $35. A clerk informs you that the same item is available at another branch of the same store for only $25. The store is a twenty-minute drive away and the clerk assures you that they have what you want there.

你会怎么做？你会选择在第一家店购买收音机闹钟，还是开车去第二家店？

What would you do? Would you buy the clock radio at the first store or drive to the second store?

如果你和大多数人一样，你可能愿意去另一家店。毕竟，开车过去很近，而且收音机还能省下近30%。这看起来简直是明智之举。

If you’re like most people, you’re probably willing to go to the other store. After all, it’s only a short drive away and you save almost 30 percent on the radio. It seems like a no-brainer.

但考虑一个类似的例子。假设你要买一台新电视。在你打算购买电视的商店里，你发现价格是 650 美元。一位店员告诉你，同一商品在同一家商店的另一家分店只需 640 美元。那家分店距离这里开车需要 20 分钟，店员向你保证那里有你想要的商品。

But consider a similar example. Imagine you are buying a new television. At the store where you expect to buy it, you find that the price is $650. A clerk informs you that the same item is available at another branch of the same store for only $640. The store is a twenty-minute drive away and the clerk assures you that they have what you want there.

你遇到这种情况会怎么做？你愿意开车二十分钟来省10美元买电视吗？

What would you do in this situation? Would you be willing to drive twenty minutes to save $10 on the television?

如果你和大多数人一样，这次你可能也会拒绝。为什么要为了省几块钱买台电视而开车二十分钟呢？你花在汽油上的钱可能比省下的钱还多。事实上，当我把这两种情况分别告诉一百个人时，87%的人表示会在第一家店买电视，而只有17%的人表示会买收音机闹钟。

If you’re like most people, this time around you probably said no. Why drive twenty minutes to save a few bucks on a TV? You’d probably spend more on gas than what you’d save on the product. In fact, when I gave each scenario to one hundred different people, 87 percent said they’d buy the television at the first store while only 17 percent said the same for the clock radio.

但仔细想想，这两种情况本质上是一样的。都是关于开车二十分钟来省十美元。所以，在这两种情况下，人们都应该同样愿意开车。

But if you think about it, these two scenarios are essentially the same. They’re both about driving twenty minutes to save $10. So people should have been equally willing to take the drive in each scenario.

但事实并非如此。虽然几乎每个人都愿意为了买便宜的收音机闹钟而驱车前往，但几乎没有人愿意为了买电视而这样做。为什么呢？

Except they weren’t. While almost everyone is willing to endure the drive for the cheaper clock radio, almost no one is willing to do it when buying a TV. Why?

敏感度递减反映了这样一个概念：同样的改变，距离参考点越远，其影响就越小。想象一下，你在办公室或孩子学校参加抽奖。你原本没指望能中多少奖，但出乎意料的是，你中了10美元。你真幸运！中奖总是好事，所以你肯定会非常高兴。

Diminishing sensitivity reflects the idea that the same change has a smaller impact the farther it is from the reference point. Imagine that you enter a lottery at your office or your child’s school. You’re not expecting to get much out of it, but to your surprise you win $10. Lucky you! Winning anything is great, so you’d probably be pretty happy about it.

现在想象一下，如果你赢了20美元，你可能会更开心。也许两种情况下你都不会高兴得翻跟头，但赢20美元的感觉肯定比只赢10美元好得多。

Now imagine you won $20 instead. You’d probably feel even happier. Maybe you wouldn’t be doing backflips in either case, but winning $20 would feel significantly better than winning only $10.

好的，现在我们还是用同样的彩票，奖金也还是增加10美元，但这次我们稍微提高一下赌注。想象一下，你赢了120美元而不是110美元。或者更好的是，赢了1020美元而不是1010美元。突然间，那额外的10美元就没那么重要了。如果你赢了120美元而不是110美元，你的感觉可能基本相同。如果你赢了1020美元而不是1010美元，你可能根本不会注意到。同样的改变——多赢10美元——在你离零美元或一无所获的参考点越远时，其影响就越小。

Okay, now let’s take that same lottery and that same $10 increase in winnings and let’s raise the stakes a little. Imagine you won $120 rather than $110. Or even better, $1,020 rather than $1,010. Suddenly that extra $10 wouldn’t matter as much. You’d probably feel essentially the same if you won $120 rather than $110. If you won $1,020 rather than $1,010 you probably wouldn’t even notice. The same change—gaining ten more dollars—has a smaller and smaller impact the farther you move from your reference point of zero dollars or not winning anything.

敏感度下降有助于解释为什么人们更愿意开车去买闹钟收音机，以节省开支。闹钟收音机本身就便宜得多，所以从35美元降到25美元看起来相当划算。但即使电视机也降价10美元，考虑到电视机原本的价格要高得多，这似乎也算不上什么便宜。

Diminishing sensitivity helps explain why people are more willing to drive to save the money on the clock radio. The clock radio was much cheaper, so a discount from $35 to $25 seems like a pretty good deal. But even though the television is also $10 off, it doesn’t seem like a bargain given how much more expensive the television was in the first place.

突出超值

HIGHLIGHTING INCREDIBLE VALUE

当优惠信息凸显出惊人的价值时，它们似乎更具吸引力。正如“社交货币”章节所述，越是引人注目的事物，就越容易被讨论。我们无时无刻不被各种优惠信息轰炸。如果我们每次超市罐头汤降价10美分都分享出去，恐怕没人会再跟我们做朋友了。优惠信息必须足够吸引人，才能在众多信息中脱颖而出，被分享出去。

Deals seem more appealing when they highlight incredible value. As discussed in the Social Currency chapter, the more remarkable something is, the more likely it will be discussed. We’re bombarded with deals all the time. If we shared every time the grocery store knocked ten cents off a can of soup no one would be friends with us anymore. A deal needs to cut through the clutter to get shared.

正如前景理论所揭示的，凸显超值优惠的关键因素之一在于人们的预期。那些看似出乎意料或超出预期的促销活动更容易被分享。这可能是因为实际优惠本身就超出了预期（例如，折扣力度大得惊人），也可能是因为优惠的宣传方式使其看起来如此。

As prospect theory illustrates, one key factor in highlighting incredible value is what people expect. Promotional offers that seem surprising or surpass expectations are more likely to be shared. This can be because the actual deal itself exceeds expectations (for example, the percentage off is so unbelievable) or because the way the deal is framed makes it seem that way.

影响优惠是否划算的另一个因素是优惠的可用性。与直觉有些相反，促销活动往往更容易被消费者忽略。更严格的限制实际上可能会让它们更有效。正如我们在“社交货币”章节中讨论的 Please Don't Tell 和 Rue La La 的例子一样，通过稀缺性和排他性来限制可用性，会使事物显得更有价值。

Another factor that affects whether deals seem valuable is their availability. Somewhat counterintuitively, making promotions more restrictive can actually make them more effective. Just as in the examples of Please Don’t Tell and Rue La La that we discussed in the Social Currency chapter, restricting availability through scarcity and exclusivity makes things seem more valuable.

以促销的时机或频率为例。打折促销会让商品看起来很划算。但如果一件商品总是打折，人们就会开始调整预期。他们不再以原价作为参考，而是把促销价当作预期价格。这种情况经常发生在那些总是打七折的地毯店。人们逐渐意识到“促销”是常态，不再将其视为真正的优惠。即使是“促销”这个词本身，也会产生同样的效果。虽然标明商品正在促销可以增加需求，但如果商店里太多商品都在促销，实际上反而会减少购买量。

Take timing or frequency. Putting something on sale can make it seem like a good deal. But if a product is always on sale people start to adjust their expectations. Rather than the full, “regular” price being their reference point, the sale price becomes the expected price. This happens with rug stores that always offer 70 percent off. People come to realize that “sales” are the norm and no longer see them as deals. The same is true even with the word “sale.” While noting something is on sale can increase demand, if too many items in a store are listed as being on sale, it can actually reduce purchase.

但限时优惠往往更具吸引力，正是因为这种限制性。就像人为制造稀缺性一样，优惠不会永远存在这一事实，会让人们觉得它一定非常划算。

But offers that are available for only a limited time seem more appealing because of the restriction. Just like making a product scarce, the fact that a deal won’t be around forever makes people feel that it must be a really good one.

数量限制的原理也类似。零售商有时会限制特定顾客购买特定折扣商品的数量，例如“每户限购一件”或“每人限购三件”。你可能会认为，限制人们购买所需数量会抑制需求。但实际上，数量限制反而会起到相反的效果，让促销显得更加划算。“哇，如果我只能买一件，那肯定意味着这件商品太好，商店都担心卖光了。赶紧下手！” 事实上，研究发现……限购数量可使销售额增长超过 50%。

Quantity limits work the same way. Retailers sometimes create limits around the number of a given discounted item a given customer can buy. “One per household” or “Limit three per customer.” You might think that by making it harder for people to get as many as they want these restrictions would hurt demand. But they actually have the opposite effect by making the promotion seem like an even better deal. “Wow, if I can only get one of these, it must mean that the deal is so good that the store is worried about running out of them. Better get one fast!” Indeed, research finds that quantity purchase limits increase sales by more than 50 percent.

即使限制访问权限也能让促销优惠看起来更有吸引力。有些优惠是面向所有人的。任何人顾客可以像在当地酒吧享受欢乐时光一样，直接走到Gap的折扣区购买卡其裤。但其他优惠则是定制的，或仅限特定顾客群体。酒店会为忠实会员提供“专属”房价，餐厅也会为特定顾客群体提供“试营业”服务。

Even restricting who has access can make a promotional offer seem better. Some deals are available to everyone. Anyone can walk up to the discount rack at the Gap and get money off chinos, just as any patron can take advantage of happy hour at his or her local pub. But other deals are customized, or restricted to a certain set of customers. Hotels reward loyal members with “exclusive” hotel rates and restaurants have “soft openings” for a certain clientele.

这些优惠看起来很特别。这不仅能通过增加社交货币来促进分享，还能让优惠本身显得更有吸引力。就像数量或时间限制一样，并非所有人都能享受到这项促销活动，这本身就让它显得更有价值。这提高了实用价值，进而促进了分享。

These offers seem special. This boosts sharing not only by increasing Social Currency, but also by making the deal itself seem better. Like restrictions on quantity or timing, the mere fact that not everyone can get access to this promotion makes it seem more valuable. This increases Practical Value, which in turn, boosts sharing.

百数法则

The Rule of 100

影响实际价值的另一个框架因素是促销优惠的表达方式。有些优惠以美元折扣或绝对折扣（例如减5美元或减50美元）来表示，而另一些优惠则以百分比折扣或相对折扣（例如减5%或减50%）来表示。促销优惠是以美元金额还是百分比折扣来表达，是否会影响折扣力度的视觉效果？

Another framing factor that impacts practical value is how promotional offers are expressed. Some offers are expressed in dollars off, or absolute discounts ($5 or $50 off). Other offers are expressed in percentage off, or relative discounts (5 percent or 50 percent off). Could whether a promotion is framed as money or as a percentage off affect how big the discount seems?

一件25美元的衬衫打八折。同样的折扣也可以表示为八折或减5美元。哪种方式更划算？

Take twenty percent off a $25 shirt. The same reduction can be represented as 20 percent off or $5 off. Which seems like a better deal?

或者想想一台价值 2000 美元的笔记本电脑。同样的折扣，可以表示为打九折，也可以表示为减 200 美元。哪种折扣方式更能凸显这笔交易的吸引力呢？

Or think about a $2,000 laptop. The same reduction on a $2,000 laptop can be represented as 10 percent off or $200 off. Does one method of framing the discount make the deal seem better than the other?

研究人员发现折扣以金额还是百分比来衡量显得更大，取决于原价。对于书籍或食品杂货等低价商品，以百分比形式呈现的降价幅度看起来更明显。一件25美元的衬衫打八折似乎比打五折更划算。但对于高价商品来说，情况恰恰相反。对于笔记本电脑或其他大件商品，用美元金额（而不是百分比）来表示降价幅度，会显得更有吸引力。笔记本电脑直接降价200美元比打九折更划算。

Researchers find that whether a discount seems larger as money or percentage off depends on the original price. For low-priced products, like books or groceries, price reductions seem more significant when they are framed in percentage terms. Twenty percent off that $25 shirt seems like a better deal than $5 off. For high-priced products, however, the opposite is true. For things like laptops or other big-ticket items, framing price reductions in dollar terms (rather than percentage terms) makes them seem like a better offer. The laptop seems like a better deal when it is $200 off rather than 10 percent off.

判断哪个折扣幅度更大的一个简单方法是使用所谓的“100法则”。

A simple way to figure out which discount frame seems larger is by using something called the Rule of 100.

如果商品价格低于 100 美元，根据“百元法则”，百分比折扣看起来会更大。对于一件 30 美元的 T 恤或一份 15 美元的主菜来说，即使 3 美元的折扣也相对较小。但如果换算成百分比（10% 或 20%），同样的折扣看起来就大得多。

If the product’s price is less than $100, the Rule of 100 says that percentage discounts will seem larger. For a $30 T-shirt or a $15 entrée, even a $3 discount is still a relatively small number. But percentagewise (10 percent or 20 percent), that same discount looks much bigger.

如果产品价格超过 100 美元，情况则相反。数字上的折扣看起来会更大。例如，750 美元的度假套餐或 2000 美元的笔记本电脑。虽然 10% 的折扣看起来可能不多，但换算成美元（75 美元或 200 美元）后，折扣金额就显得大得多。

If the product’s price is more than $100, the opposite is true. Numerical discounts will seem larger. Take a $750 vacation package or the $2,000 laptop. While a 10 percent discount may seem like a relatively small number, it immediately seems much bigger when translated into dollars ($75 or $200).

因此，在判断促销优惠是否真的划算，或者如何调整促销方案使其更具吸引力时，请使用“100法则”。思考价格相对于100美元的位置，以及这种位置关系如何影响绝对折扣或相对折扣的吸引力。

So when deciding how good a promotional offer really is, or how to frame a promotional offer to make it better, use the Rule of 100. Think about where the price falls relative to $100 and how that shifts whether absolute or relative discounts seem more attractive.

—————

—————

关于促销活动，最后一点是，其实际价值越容易被人们看到，效果就越好。以你在当地超市或药店获得的购物折扣卡为例。这些卡当然很有用。它们能帮消费者省钱，有时如果累计消费达到一定金额，还能获得免费赠品。但问题在于，这些折扣卡的实际价值往往难以被人们所察觉。这种优惠的价值并不显而易见。人们只能在冗长的收据上看到隐藏的五六项信息，才能了解到自己省了多少钱。而且，考虑到大多数人不会把收据给别人看，除了刷卡人本人之外，几乎没人能看到自己省了多少钱。这就使得这种信息很难传播开来。

One last point about promotional offers is that the practical value is more effective the easier it is for people to see. Take the shopper discount cards that you get at your local grocery store or pharmacy. These cards are certainly useful. They save consumers money and sometimes even give them free gifts if they have accumulated enough purchases. But one problem is that the practical value is not very visible. The only information people get about how much they saved is hidden among a half dozen other pieces of information on a lengthy receipt. And given that most people don’t show their receipts to others, it’s unlikely that anyone but the person who used the card will see how much they saved. That makes it less likely that the information will be contagious.

但如果商店让实际价值更容易被看到呢？他们可以在收银台附近竖个牌子，让排队的顾客看到结账的人省了多少钱。或者，商店可以在有人节省超过25美元时响铃。这样做会带来两方面的好处。首先，人们会更清楚地了解办卡能省多少钱，从而鼓励那些还没有卡的人去办一张。其次，人们可以看到其他顾客省下了多少钱，从而鼓励他们去分享这些实际价值的惊人故事。正如“公众”章节所讨论的，你很难谈论你看不见的东西。

But what if stores made the practical value easier to see? They could put up a sign at checkout that shows other people in line how much the person checking out saved. Or the store might ring a bell every time someone saved more than twenty-five dollars. This would make two things happen. First, people would get a better sense of how much they could save by getting the card, encouraging anyone who doesn’t have one yet to get one. Second, it would allow people to see the impressive amounts that some other shoppers were able to save, encouraging them to transmit these remarkable stories of practical value. As discussed in the Public chapter, it’s hard to talk about something you can’t see.

不仅仅是金钱

MORE THAN MONEY

我投资能力很差。选择太多，波动太大，风险也太大。我宁愿把钱放在床底下的纸箱里，也不愿投到可能亏损的共同基金里。我第一次买股票的时候只是浅尝辄止。我挑了两三只看起来不错的长期投资标的，因为它们都是知名品牌，然后就没再继续投资了。

I am terrible at investing. Too many options, too much daily volatility, and too much risk. I’d rather keep my money in a cardboard box under my bed than put it in some mutual fund that could lose money. The first time I bought stocks I barely dipped my toe in. I picked two or three that seemed like good long-term investments based on being strong brands and tried to leave it at that.

但我的好奇心战胜了理智。我每天都疯狂地查看每只股票的走势。今天涨了一美元？太棒了！第二天跌了35美分？我彻底泄气，开始考虑以后再也不投资了。

But my curiosity got the best of me. I frantically checked every day how each stock was doing. A dollar up today? Huge success! Thirty-five cents down the next day? Hopelessly despondent and considering giving up investing ever again.

毋庸置疑，我需要帮助。所以，当到了该做这件事的时候……我把工作所得存入我的 401(k) 退休金账户，并选择了一些追踪股市的稳健指数基金。

Needless to say I needed help. So when it came time to put money in my 401(k) for work, I picked some safe index funds that track the stock market.

不久之后，管理我退休计划的先锋集团（Vanguard）给我发了一封简短的电子邮件，询问我是否愿意订阅他们的月度电子报《理财之道》 （MoneyWhys）。和大多数人一样，我尽量避免订阅新的邮件列表，但这份电子报看起来确实很有用。它提供一些税务方面的实用建议，解答一些常见的投资问题，以及对“金钱是否真的能买到幸福”这个古老问题的解答（或者至少是观点）。于是我订阅了。

Soon after, Vanguard, the firm that manages my retirement plan, sent me a short e-mail asking if I’d like to receive its monthly newsletter, MoneyWhys. Like most people, I try to avoid signing up for new mailing lists, but this one actually seemed useful. Last-minute tax tips, responses to common questions about investing, and an answer (or at least an opinion) on that age-old question of whether money can really buy happiness. I signed up.

现在，Vanguard每月都会给我发一封简短的电子邮件，内容是关于财务管理的实用信息。有一次，邮件里讲解了房屋保险的实际承保范围；另一次，则提供了如何利用电脑追踪个人财务状况的技巧。

Now, once a month, Vanguard sends me a short e-mail with useful information about financial management. One month it was tips on what homeowner’s insurance actually covers. Another month it provided tips on using your PC to track personal finances.

说实话，我并非每封 Vanguard 的邮件都会阅读（抱歉了，Vanguard），但我会把读过的邮件转发给我认为会从中受益的朋友。比如，我把关于房屋保险的文章转发给了刚买房的同事，又把关于个人财务管理的文章转发给了正在努力提升理财能力的朋友。Vanguard 将其专业知识精炼成简洁明了的实用信息，其价值之高促使我将其分享出去。同时，我也在向他人宣传 Vanguard 及其投资专长。

To be honest, I don’t read every e-mail Vanguard sends (sorry, Vanguard), but I end up forwarding many of the ones I do read to people I know who I think will find them useful. I sent the piece about homeowner’s insurance to a colleague who just bought a home. I forwarded the piece about tracking personal finances to a friend who is trying to become more fiscally responsible. Vanguard nicely packages its expertise into a short, tight bundle of useful information, and the practical value made me pass it along. And along the way I’m spreading the word about Vanguard and its investment expertise.

—————

—————

因此，有用的信息是另一种形式的实用价值。它能帮助人们更快、更好、更轻松地完成他们想做的事情，或者鼓励他们去做他们应该做的事情。

Useful information, then, is another form of practical value. Helping people do things they want to do, or encouraging them to do things they should do. Faster, better, and easier.

正如我们在“情绪”一章中所讨论的，我们对《纽约时报》电子邮件转发量最高的文章列表的分析发现，有关健康和教育的文章是转发量最高的文章类型之一。食谱和新兴餐厅的评论也广受欢迎。原因之一是这类文章都提供了实用信息。健康版块为听力障碍人士提供解决方案，并为中年人提升心理健康提供技巧。教育版块涵盖了适合青少年的实用项目，并深入剖析了大学申请流程。与他人分享这类内容，能够帮助他们更好地饮食、生活和学习。

As we discussed in the Emotions chapter, our analysis of The New York Times Most E-Mailed list found that articles about health and education were some of the most frequently shared. Recipes and reviews of up-and-coming restaurants were also highly shared. One reason is that these types of articles all provide useful information. The health section suggests solutions for people with hearing loss and techniques for boosting mental fitness in middle age. The education section covers useful programs for teens and provides insight into the college admissions process. Sharing this type of content with others enables them to eat, live, and learn better.

看看过去几个月你收到的邮件内容，你会发现类似的模式：比如《消费者报告》评选出的最佳防晒霜品牌、运动后快速恢复的技巧，或是万圣节南瓜雕刻的灵感等等。这些内容都很有用，实用建议也更容易分享。

Look at the content you’ve been e-mailed over the past few months and you’ll see similar patterns. Articles about sunscreen brands that Consumer Reports rated the best, tips to recover quickly from exercise, or hints for great pumpkin carving design around Halloween. All these things are useful. Practical advice is shareable advice.

思考为什么一些有用的内容更容易被分享时，有几点值得注意。首先是信息的包装方式。Vanguard 不会发送冗长的四页邮件，里面塞满关于十五个不同主题的二十五个建议链接。它发送的是一份简短的单页信息，包含一篇关键的标题文章，并在其下方附上三四个主要链接。这样一来，要点一目了然，如果想了解更多，只需点击链接即可。许多在《纽约时报》和其他网站上爆红的文章都采用了类似的结构，例如“五种减肥方法”、“新年十条约会建议”等等。下次你在超市排队结账时，不妨看看杂志，你会发现同样的模式也被运用其中。围绕一个关键主题，列出简短的列表。

In thinking about why some useful content gets shared more, a couple of points are worth noting. The first is how the information is packaged. Vanguard doesn’t send out a rambling four-page e-mail with twenty-five advice links about fifteen different topics. It sends out a short, one-page note, with a key header article and three or four main links below it. It’s easy to see what the main points are, and if you want to find out more, you can simply click on the links. Many of the most viral articles on The New York Times and other websites have a similar structure. Five ways to lose weight. Ten dating tips for the New Year. The next time you’re waiting in the checkout line at the grocery store, take a look at the magazines and you’ll see the same idea being applied. Short lists focused around a key topic.

一家化妆品制造商为商务旅行者开发了一款实用的 iPhone 应用。除了提供当地天气信息外，它还提供根据当地情况量身定制的专业护肤建议。湿度、降雨和空气质量都会影响皮肤状况。这款应用会根据你的头发和皮肤状况，告诉你正确的应对方法。这些实用信息不仅非常有效，也展现了公司在该领域的知识和专业水平。

A cosmetic manufacturer makes a helpful iPhone application for business travelers. In addition to providing local weather information, it also provides expert skin care advice that is tailored to those local conditions. Humidity, rain, and air quality affect your hair and skin, so the application tells you the right way to respond. This practically valuable information not only is useful, but also demonstrates the company’s knowledge and expertise in this domain.

第二个关键因素是受众。有些故事或信息的受众比其他故事或信息更广泛。至少在美国，关注职业橄榄球的人比关注水球的人多。同样，你身边喜欢美式餐厅的朋友可能比喜欢埃塞俄比亚餐厅的朋友多。

The second key is the audience. Some stories or information have a broader audience than others. In the United States, at least, more people follow professional football than follow water polo. Similarly, you probably have more friends that like American restaurants than like Ethiopian restaurants.

你可能会认为，受众更广的内容更容易被分享。一篇关于足球的文章应该比一篇关于水球的文章更容易被分享；一篇关于新开的美国餐厅的评论应该比一篇关于新开的埃塞俄比亚餐厅的评论更容易被转发。毕竟，人们可以分享文章的朋友更多，所以最终文章的受众范围应该更广才对吧？

You might think that content that has a broader audience is more likely to be shared. A piece about football should be shared more than one about water polo; a review about a new American restaurant should be passed on more than a review of a new Ethiopian place. After all, people have way more friends with whom they could share the article, so shouldn’t it end up reaching more people overall?

然而，这种假设的问题在于，人们能够分享的内容越多，他们就越有可能分享。事实上，受众面更窄的内容反而更容易被分享，因为它能让人想起某个特定的朋友或家人，从而产生分享的冲动。你可能有很多朋友喜欢美式食物或橄榄球。但由于很多人都对这类事物感兴趣，当你看到相关内容时，并不会立刻想到某个特定的人。相反，你可能只有一个朋友对埃塞俄比亚餐厅或水球感兴趣，但如果你读到一篇关于这些主题的文章，你会立刻想到你的朋友。而且因为这篇文章似乎特别适合她，你会觉得必须分享出去。

The problem with this assumption, though, is that just because people can share with more people doesn’t mean they will. In fact, narrower content may actually be more likely to be shared because it reminds people of a specific friend or family member and makes them feel compelled to pass it along. You might have a lot of friends who like American food or football. But because so many people are interested in that type of thing, no one person strongly comes to mind when you come across related content. In contrast, you may have only one friend who cares about Ethiopian restaurants or water polo, but if you read an article about those topics you think about your friend right away. And because it seems so uniquely perfect for her, you feel you have to share it.

因此，虽然受众面广的内容可能会被分享得更多，但显然与小众群体相关的内容实际上可能更容易传播开来。

So while broadly relevant content could be shared more, content that is obviously relevant to a narrow audience may actually be more viral.

关于真理的一点说明

A NOTE ON TRUTH

你可能听说过疫苗会导致自闭症。如果是这样，你并不孤单。1998年，一篇发表在医学期刊上的论文指出，接种麻疹、腮腺炎和风疹疫苗可能会导致儿童患上自闭症。健康相关的新闻传播速度很快，尤其是与儿童相关的新闻，很快，许多人就开始讨论疫苗的潜在弊端。结果，儿童疫苗接种率下降了。

You may have heard that vaccines cause autism. If so, you’re not alone. In 1998, a paper was published in a medical journal suggesting that an immunization against measles, mumps, and rubella could cause autism in children. Health-related news spreads fast, particularly when it relates to kids, and soon lots of people were talking about the potential downsides to vaccines. As a result, childhood vaccination rates decreased.

如果疫苗与自闭症之间存在关联，那一切都好。但事实并非如此。没有任何科学证据表明疫苗会导致自闭症。最初的那篇论文被证实是伪造的。撰写该论文的医生篡改了证据，显然是出于利益冲突，在被认定犯有严重的职业不端行为后，他被吊销了行医执照。但即便信息是错误的，仍然有很多人传播它。

All this would be good if the link between vaccines and autism were true. But it’s not. There is no scientific evidence that vaccines cause autism. The original paper turned out to be a fraud. The doctor who authored it had manipulated evidence, apparently owing to conflict of interest, and after being found guilty of serious professional misconduct, lost his medical license. But even though the information was false, lots of people shared it.

原因在于实用价值。人们并非有意传播虚假信息，他们只是听到了一些他们认为有用的消息，并且希望其他人的孩子安全。但许多人并未得知最初的报道已被证伪，因此他们继续传播错误的说法。我们分享有用信息的愿望如此强烈，以至于它甚至能让错误的观点传播开来。有时，这种助人的动机也会误入歧途。

The reason is practical value. People weren’t trying to share false things, they just heard something they thought was useful and they wanted others’ kids to be safe. But many people didn’t hear the news that the original report had been discredited, and so they continued to share an incorrect narrative. Our desire to share helpful things is so powerful that it can make even false ideas succeed. Sometimes the drive to help takes a wrong turn.

所以，下次如果有人告诉你某种神奇疗法，或者警告你某种食物或行为的健康风险，在转发之前，请务必独立核实这些信息。虚假信息传播的速度可能和真相一样快。

So the next time someone tells you about a miracle cure, or warns about the health risks of a particular food or behavior, try to verify that information independently before you pass it on. False information can spread just as quickly as the truth.

—————

—————

实际价值在于帮助。本章讨论了价值的运作机制和交易心理学，但这同样重要。要记住人们最初分享这类信息的原因。人们乐于助人。我们会主动提供建议或分享信息，让别人受益。当然，其中一些行为可能出于私心。我们认为自己是对的，忍不住想对别人的生活指手画脚。但并非所有行为都与我们自身有关。它也源于利他主义，源于人性中固有的善良。我们关心他人，希望改善他们的生活。

Practical value is about helping. This chapter discussed the mechanics of value and the psychology of deals, but it’s important to remember why people share that type of information in the first place. People like to help one another. We go out of our way to give advice or send others information that will make them better off. Sure, some of this may be selfish. We think we’re right and we can’t help but toss our two cents into other people’s lives. But not all of it is about us. It’s also about altruism, the inherent goodness of people. We care about others and we want to make their lives better.

在本书中，我们讨论了传染性的六个原则，其中实用价值可能是最容易应用的。

Of the six principles of contagiousness that we discuss in the book, Practical Value may be the easiest to apply.

有些产品和理念本身就具备很强的社交影响力，但要将其融入到搅拌机的宣传视频中，则需要投入大量的精力和创造力。构思如何制造“触发点”也需要花费一些心思，唤起人们的情感同样如此。但找到实际价值并不难。几乎所有你能想到的产品或理念都具有一定的实用价值。无论是帮人们省钱、提升幸福感、改善健康还是节省时间，这些都是可以利用的信息。因此，思考人们最初为什么会被我们的产品或理念所吸引，将有助于我们更好地理解其潜在的实际价值。

Some products and ideas already have lots of Social Currency, but to build it into a video for a blender takes some energy and creativity. Figuring out how to create Triggers also requires some effort, as does evoking emotion. But finding Practical Value isn’t hard. Almost every product or idea imaginable has something useful about it. Whether it saves people money, makes them happier, improves health, or saves them time, all of these things are news you can use. So thinking about why people gravitate to our product or idea in the first place will give us a good sense of the underlying practical value.

最难的是如何从海量信息中脱颖而出。市面上有很多不错的餐厅和实用的网站，所以我们需要让自己的产品或理念引人注目。我们需要强调其巨大的价值，并运用“百分法则”。就像先锋集团一样，我们需要精心包装我们的知识和专业技能，让人们在了解我们的同时，也乐于分享。我们需要清晰地阐明我们的产品或理念为何如此实用，以至于人们会不由自主地口口相传。实用资讯，值得信赖。

The harder part is cutting through the clutter. There are lots of good restaurants and helpful websites, so we need to make our product or idea stand out. We need to highlight incredible value and use the Rule of 100. Like Vanguard, we need to package our knowledge and expertise so that people learn about us while they pass it along. We need to make it clear why our product or idea is so useful that people just have to spread the word. News you can use.

6.故事

6. Stories

这场战争持续了整整十年，却始终看不到尽头。传说中，奥德修斯想出了一个巧妙的计策来结束这场徒劳的围攻。希腊人建造了一匹巨大的木马，并将他们最精锐的战士藏在里面。其余的军队则扬帆起航，假装返回故土，把这匹巨大的木马留在了海滩上。

The war had raged for ten long years, with no finish in sight. According to legend, Odysseus devised a cunning plan to end the fruitless siege. The Greeks built a giant wooden horse and hid their best warriors inside. The rest of their army then sailed away, pretending to return to their homeland and leaving the monumental horse behind on the beach.

特洛伊人找到了这匹木马，并把它拖进特洛伊城，作为胜利的象征。他们用绳索套住木马的脖子，数十名男子在木马下方放置巨大的滚木，慢慢地将它从沙滩上拉上来。其他人则忙着拆除城门，以便将这尊巨大的雕塑拖进城内。

The Trojans found the horse and dragged it into Troy as a symbol of their victory. They tied ropes around the beast’s neck and dozens of men set huge log rollers underneath the wooden body to pull it slowly up from the beach. Others worked to take down the gate so that the monstrous sculpture could be dragged inside the city walls.

雕像被安放在神庙内后，特洛伊人庆祝了这场长达十年的战争的结束。他们用绿植装饰神庙，挖出盛放祭祀用酒的罐子，载歌载舞，庆祝苦难的结束。

Once the statue was inside, the Trojans celebrated the end of the decade-long conflict. They decorated the temples with greenery, unearthed the jugs of sacrificial wine, and danced to rejoice at the conclusion of their ordeal.

但那夜，当全城沉浸在醉酒的沉睡中时，希腊人从藏身之处跃出。他们滑到地上，制服了守卫，打开了巨大的城门。这座城市。其余的希腊军队趁着夜色返回，很快与他们汇合，轻而易举地穿过了他们多年来徒劳进攻的城门。

But that night, while the city lay unconscious in drunken slumber, the Greeks sprang from their hiding place. They slid to the ground, silenced the sentries, and opened the huge gates to the city. The rest of the Greek army sailed back under the cover of darkness and soon joined them, easily walking through the very gates they had fruitlessly assaulted for so many years.

这座城市经受住了长达十年的战火，却无法抵挡来自内部的攻击。希腊人一旦攻入城内，便摧毁了这座城镇，彻底结束了特洛伊战争。

The city was able to stand a decade of battle, but it could not withstand an attack from within. Once inside, the Greeks destroyed the town, decisively ending the Trojan War.

—————

—————

特洛伊木马的故事已经流传了数千年。科学家和历史学家估计，这场战役大约发生在公元前1170年，但直到多年以后才被记录下来。几个世纪以来，这个故事以史诗的形式口口相传，或吟唱，并配以音乐。

The story of the Trojan Horse has been passed on for thousands of years. Scientists and historians estimate that the battle took place around 1170 BC, but the story was not written down until many years later. For centuries the tale was transmitted orally as an epic poem, spoken or sung to music.

这个故事读起来就像一档现代真人秀节目，充满了曲折离奇的情节，包括私人恩怨、婚外情和背叛。它巧妙地融合了戏剧性、浪漫和动作元素，牢牢抓住了听众的注意力。

The story reads like a modern-day reality show. It’s full of twists and turns that include personal vendettas, adultery, and double crosses. Through a potent mixture of drama, romance, and action, it holds listeners’ interest.

但特洛伊木马的故事也蕴含着一个深意：“提防希腊人送来的礼物。”更普遍的解读是：“永远不要相信你的敌人，即使他们看起来友善。”事实上，正当他们做出这种示好时，你才更应该提高警惕。所以，特洛伊木马的故事不仅仅是一个引人入胜的故事，它还蕴含着重要的教训。

But the story of the Trojan Horse also carries an underlying message: “Beware of Greeks bearing gifts.” A more general interpretation would be “never trust your enemies, even when they seem friendly.” In fact it is exactly when they are making such overtures that you should be especially suspicious. So the tale of the Trojan Horse is more than just an entertaining story. It also teaches an important lesson.

然而，如果荷马和维吉尔只是想教训人们，他们难道不能更有效地做到这一点吗？他们难道不能直奔主题，而不是写一首数百行诗句的史诗吗？

Still, if Homer and Virgil had simply wanted to teach people a lesson, couldn’t they have done it more efficiently? Couldn’t they have gotten right to the point rather than writing an epic poem with hundreds of lines of poetry?

当然。但这一课还能产生同样的效果吗？可能不会。

Of course. But would the lesson have had the same impact? Probably not.

通过将道理融入故事中，这些早期作家确保了……这样一来，信息就会被传递下去——而且或许比简单地、直白地讲述教训更容易被人们全心全意地相信。这是因为人们的思维方式并非基于信息本身，而是基于叙事。当人们专注于故事本身时，信息便随之而来。

By encasing the lesson in a story, these early writers ensured that it would be passed along—and perhaps even be believed more wholeheartedly than if the lesson’s words were spoken simply and plainly. That’s because people don’t think in terms of information. They think in terms of narratives. But while people focus on the story itself, information comes along for the ride.

故事如船

STORIES AS VESSELS

故事是娱乐的最初形式。想象一下，你是一位公元前1000年的希腊公民。那时没有互联网，没有体育中心，没有晚间六点新闻，没有广播，也没有报纸。所以，如果你想要娱乐，故事就是唯一的途径。《特洛伊木马》、《奥德赛》和其他著名故事都是当时的娱乐方式。人们会围坐在篝火旁，或坐在圆形剧场里，一遍又一遍地聆听这些史诗般的故事。

Stories are the original form of entertainment. Imagine you were a Greek citizen in 1000 BC. There was no Internet. No SportsCenter or six o’clock news. No radio or newspapers. So if you wanted entertainment, stories were the way to get it. The Trojan Horse, The Odyssey, and other famous tales were the entertainment of the day. People would gather round a fire, or sit in an amphitheater, to hear these epic narratives told again and again.

叙事本身就比事实更引人入胜。它们有开头、中间和结尾。如果人们一开始就被吸引，他们就会一直读到结尾。当你听到别人讲一个好故事时，你会全神贯注地听。你想知道他们是否错过了飞机，或者他们是如何应对一屋子尖叫的九岁孩子的。你踏上了一条路，你想知道它最终会如何发展。在故事结束之前，它已经牢牢抓住了你的注意力。

Narratives are inherently more engrossing than basic facts. They have a beginning, middle, and end. If people get sucked in early, they’ll stay for the conclusion. When you hear people tell a good story you hang on every word. You want to find out whether they missed the plane or what they did with a house full of screaming nine year olds. You started down a path and you want to know how it ends. Until it does, they’ve captured your attention.

如今娱乐方式五花八门，但我们讲故事的本能却丝毫未减。我们围坐在篝火旁——如今或许是饮水机旁，或许是闺蜜/兄弟聚会——讲述着彼此的故事。关于我们自己，关于我们最近经历的事情；关于我们的朋友，关于我们认识的其他人。

Today there are thousands of entertainment options, but our tendency to tell stories remains. We get together around our proverbial campfires—now water coolers or girls’/guys’ night out—and tell stories. About ourselves and the things that have happened to us lately. About our friends and other people we know.

人们讲述故事的原因与他们口口相传的原因相同。有些故事关乎社会价值。人们讲述有些人会讲述自己为了加入“请勿告知”（Please Don't Tell）节目而特意穿过电话亭的故事，因为这让他们看起来很酷，消息灵通。其他一些故事则是由（高度兴奋的）情绪驱动的。人们讲述“搅拌机能打碎吗？”（Will It Blend?）的故事，是因为他们惊叹搅拌机竟然能把弹珠或iPhone打碎。实用价值也发挥着作用。人们分享邻居家的狗吃了某种磨牙玩具后生病的故事，是因为他们希望自己的狗避免同样的命运。

People tell stories for the same reasons they share word of mouth. Some narratives are about Social Currency. People tell the story of going through the phone booth to get into Please Don’t Tell because it makes them look cool and in the know. Other stories are driven by (high arousal) Emotion. People tell the story of Will It Blend? because they are amazed that a blender could shred marbles or an iPhone. Practical Value also plays a role. People share the story of how their neighbor’s dogs got sick after eating a certain type of chew toy because they want your dog to avoid the same fate.

人们已经习惯于讲述故事，以至于即使没有必要，他们也会编造叙事。以网络评论为例。评论本应是关于产品特性的，比如新款数码相机的性能如何，变焦效果是否如厂商宣传的那样出色。但这些原本只是信息性的内容，最终往往都被包裹在某种背景叙事之中。

People are so used to telling stories that they create narratives even when they don’t actually need to. Take online reviews. They’re supposed to be about product features. How well a new digital camera worked and whether the zoom is as good as the company suggests. But this mostly informational content often ends up being embedded in a background narrative.

我儿子刚满八岁，所以我们计划去年七月带他去迪士尼乐园玩。我们需要一台数码相机来记录这次旅行，于是就买了这台，因为朋友推荐了它。它的变焦功能很棒，即使从远处也能轻松拍到清晰的灰姑娘城堡照片。

My son just turned eight so we were planning our first trip to Disney World last July. We needed a digital camera to capture the experience so bought this one because my friend recommended it. The zoom was great. We could easily get sharp pictures of Cinderella’s Castle even from far away.

我们太习惯于讲故事了，以至于即使简单的评分或意见就足够了，我们也会讲故事。

We’re so used to telling stories that we do it even when a simple rating or opinion would have sufficed.

—————

—————

就像特洛伊木马一样，故事远不止表面看起来那么简单。当然，故事的外在形式——我们可以称之为表面情节——会吸引你的注意力，激发你的兴趣。但剥开这层外壳，你通常会发现隐藏在里面的东西。在那些命运多舛的恋人和英勇无畏的英雄背后，往往隐藏着更深层次的含义。

Just like the Trojan Horse itself, stories are more than they seem. Sure, the outward shell of a story—we could call this the surface plot—grabs your attention and engages your interest. But peel back that exterior, and you’ll usually find something hidden inside. Underneath the star-crossed lovers and thundering heroes there is usually something else being conveyed.

故事承载着意义。它蕴含着教训或寓意，传递着信息或发人深省的道理。以著名的故事《三只小猪》为例。三兄弟离家闯荡世界，寻找属于自己的财富。第一只小猪迅速用稻草盖起房子，第二只小猪则用树枝搭建。他们都尽可能快地把房子搭好，好让他们可以尽情玩耍一整天。然而，第三只小猪却更有条理。即使他的兄弟们在他周围嬉戏玩耍，他也花时间和精力，用砖块精心建造自己的房子。

Stories carry things. A lesson or moral. Information or a take-home message. Take the famous story “The Three Little Pigs.” Three brothers leave home to head into the world to seek their fortune. The first little pig quickly builds his house out of straw. The second pig uses sticks. Both throw their houses together as quickly as possible so they can hang out and play the rest of the day. The third pig, however, is more disciplined. He takes the time and effort to carefully build his house out of bricks, even while his brothers have fun around him.

一天晚上，一只大灰狼来找吃的。他来到第一只小猪的家门口，念着孩子们最爱听的那句咒语：“小猪，小猪，让我进去！”可是小猪不肯，大灰狼就把小猪的房子吹倒了。他又把木棍房子吹倒了。可是，当大灰狼用同样的方法去吹第三只小猪的房子时，却失败了。他使劲吹气，却吹不倒第三只小猪的房子，因为房子是用砖头砌成的。

One night, a big bad wolf comes along looking for something to eat. He goes to the first pig’s house and says those words so beloved by small children: “Little pig, little pig, let me in.” But when the pig says no, the wolf blows the pig’s house down. He does the same to the house of sticks. But when the wolf tries the same thing at the third pig’s house, it doesn’t work. He huffs and he puffs but the wolf can’t destroy the third pig’s house because it’s made of bricks.

这就是这个故事的寓意：努力终有回报。花时间把事情做好。你可能不会立刻获得很多乐趣，但最终你会发现一切都是值得的。

And that’s the moral of the story. Effort pays off. Take the time to do something right. You might not have as much fun right away, but you’ll find that it’s worth it in the end.

成千上万的其他童话、寓言和都市传说中也蕴含着教训或道德寓意。《狼来了》警示人们说谎的危险；《灰姑娘》则告诉我们善待他人终有回报。莎士比亚的戏剧也蕴含着关于性格与人际关系、权力与疯狂、爱情与战争的宝贵教训。这些教训虽然复杂，却也极具启发意义。

Lessons or morals are also embedded in thousands of other fairy tales, fables, and urban legends. “The Boy Who Cried Wolf” warns about the dangers of lying. “Cinderella” shows that being good to others pays off. Shakespeare’s plays carry valuable lessons about character and relationships, power and madness, love and war. These are complex lessons, but they are instructive nevertheless.

—————

—————

我们每天互相讲述的普通故事也蕴含着信息。

The ordinary stories we tell one another every day also carry information.

就拿我表弟在Lands' End买的那件外套来说吧。几年前他从加州搬到东海岸，为了迎接即将到来的第一个冬天，他去了一家高档百货商店，买了一件漂亮的大衣。那件外套是那种男士常穿在西装外面的七分羊毛大衣。它很合身，颜色也完美，我表弟穿上后感觉自己像个风度翩翩的英国绅士。

Take the story of the coat my cousin bought from Lands’ End. He’d moved from California to the East Coast a couple of years ago, and in preparation for his first real winter he went to a fancy department store and bought a nice topcoat. The coat was one of those three-quarter-length wool varieties that men often wear over suits. It fitted well, the color was perfect, and my cousin felt like a dapper English gentleman.

只有一个问题。不够暖和。室外温度在华氏五十度甚至四十度的时候感觉很棒，但一旦温度降到华氏三十度，寒冷就直接穿透外套，刺进我表弟的骨头里。

There was only one problem. It wasn’t warm enough. It was great when the temperature outside was in the fifties and even the forties, but once the temperature got down to the thirties the cold seeped right through the coat into my cousin’s bones.

经历了一个冬天，虽然看起来精神抖擞，但每天上班路上都冻得瑟瑟发抖，他终于决定该买件真正的冬装了。他甚至决定一步到位，买一件那种裹得像睡袋一样的鹅绒大衣——这种大衣在东部和中西部随处可见，但在加州却难得一见。于是他上网，在Lands' End找到了一件价格合适的羽绒服，买下了一件保暖等级为零下30华氏度的通勤羽绒服。这件大衣足以抵御东海岸最寒冷的冬天。

After one winter of looking great but freezing every day on his way to work, he decided it was time to get a real winter coat. He even decided to go whole hog and get one of those goose-down numbers that make you look as if you’re wearing a sleeping bag—the kind of coat that is ubiquitous in the East and Midwest but never seen in California. So he went online, found a great deal at Lands’ End, and bought a down commuter coat rated to minus thirty degrees Fahrenheit. Warm enough to withstand even the coldest East Coast winter.

我表弟很喜欢这件外套，它确实非常保暖。但是到了季中，拉链就坏了，直接从内衬上扯了下来。他非常沮丧。这件外套他才买了几个月，就坏了。修理要多少钱？修好后要等多久才能拿回来？

My cousin really liked the coat, and indeed it was super warm. But halfway through the season he broke the zipper. Ripped it right off the lining. He was devastated. He had just bought the coat a few months before and it was broken already. How much would it cost to have it fixed? And how long would he have to wait to get it back from being repaired?

当时是1月中旬，不穿冬衣出门可不是个好主意。

It was mid-January, not a very ideal time to be walking around without a winter coat.

于是他打电话给兰兹角公司，问维修要多少钱，需要多长时间才能修好。

So he called Lands’ End. How much will it cost to repair, he asked, and how long will it take to be fixed?
