我的表弟做好了迎接他习以为常的冰冷回应的准备。客服人员总是这样。他们总是说：“很抱歉产品坏了或者服务出了问题，但很遗憾这不是我们的错。要么是产品过了保修期，要么是您进行了超出正常使用范围的操作。不过，我们很乐意以产品价格的两倍为您维修，或者派人上门检查。前提是您能请假在家三个小时，我们可能会来，也可能不会来。” 哦，对了，品牌顾问写的脚本提醒我们一定要告诉您，我们非常感谢您的惠顾。

My cousin braced for the icy reply he was used to getting from customer service people. It always seems to be the customer’s problem. So sorry to hear the product broke or the service isn’t working, customer service people usually say, but unfortunately it’s not our fault. It’s outside the warranty or you tried to do something beyond the normal use. But we’d be happy to repair it for twice the cost of the product or send someone out to check on it. Just as long as you can stay home from work for the three-hour window during which we may or may not show up. Oh, and by the way, the script the brand consultants wrote reminds us to tell you that we really appreciate your business.

但令他惊讶的是，Lands' End的客服人员却说了完全不同的话。“维修？”她问，“我们会直接给你寄一件新的。”“那要多少钱？”我表弟紧张地问。“免费，”她回答说，“我们会用两天就到的快递寄出，这样你就不用等了。今年冬天这么冷，可不能穿着破外套出门。”

But to his surprise, the Lands’ End customer service person said something entirely different. “Repair?” she asked. “We’ll just send you a new one in the mail.” “How much will that cost?” my cousin asked nervously. “It’s free,” she replied, “and we’ll send it out two-day mail so you don’t have to wait. It’s too cold this winter to go out with a broken coat.”

产品坏了就免费立即更换？哇！在这个“顾客永远是错的”时代，这简直闻所未闻。这才是真正的优质客户服务！我表弟对此印象深刻，迫不及待地跟我分享了这件事。

A free replacement sent right away if a product breaks? Wow! That’s almost unheard of in this day and age of “the customer is always wrong.” Remarkable customer service. Customer service the way it is supposed to be. My cousin was so impressed he just had to tell me what happened.

我表弟的经历听起来很有趣，但仔细想想，其中还隐藏着大量实用信息：（1）大衣看起来很棒，但在严寒的东海岸冬天其实不够保暖。（2）羽绒服会让你看起来像个木乃伊，但如果你想保暖，还是值得入手的。（3）Lands' End 的冬装外套非常保暖。（4）而且他们的客户服务也非常好。（5）如果出了什么问题，Lands' End 会负责维修。这些只是这个看似简单的故事中蕴含的众多实用信息中的一小部分。

My cousin’s experience makes for a nice story, but when you look closer there is also a huge amount of useful information hidden in the narrative: (1) Topcoats look great but aren’t really warm enough for a bitter East Coast winter. (2) Down coats make you look like a mummy, but they’re worth getting if you want to stay warm. (3) Lands’ End makes a really warm winter coat. (4) It also has outstanding customer service. (5) If something goes wrong, Lands’ End will fix it. These are just a handful of the nuggets of knowledge woven into a deceptively simple story.

大多数人们讲述的故事也是如此。我们如何这些故事讲述了如何避开交通堵塞，以及干洗店如何将沾满油渍的白衬衫焕然一新。它们还包含一些实用信息：例如，高速公路堵塞时的最佳路线；以及去除顽固污渍的优秀干洗店。

The same is true for most stories people tell us. How we avoided the traffic jam or how the dry cleaner was able to take our oil-splattered white shirt and make it look like new. These stories contain helpful information: a good route to take if the highway is blocked; a great dry cleaner if you need to get out tough stains.

因此，故事可以作为容器和载体，帮助将信息传递给他人。

Stories, then, can act as vessels, carriers that help transmit information to others.

通过故事学习

LEARNING THROUGH STORIES

故事是文化学习的重要来源，帮助我们理解世界。从宏观层面来看，这种学习可以涉及群体或社会的规则和标准。例如，一个好员工应该如何表现？做一个有道德的人意味着什么？或者更实际地说：什么样的技工才不会乱收费？

Stories are an important source of cultural learning that help us make sense of the world. At a high level, this learning can be about the rules and standards of a group or society. How should a good employee behave? What does it mean to be a moral person? Or on a more basic level: who’s a good mechanic who won’t overcharge?

除了故事之外，想想人们还能通过哪些其他途径获取这些信息。反复尝试或许可行，但这会极其耗时耗力。试想一下，如果找到一个诚实的修车师傅需要你把车开到城里二十几个不同的地方，每个地方都修一次，那该有多累（而且费用不菲）。

Beyond stories, think about other ways that people could acquire this information. Trial and error might work, but it would be extremely costly and time-consuming. Imagine if finding an honest mechanic required taking your car to two dozen different places around town and getting work done at each one. It would be exhausting (and expensive).

或者，人们也可以尝试直接观察，但这也很困难。你得跟所有修车店的技工套近乎，说服他们让你观看他们的工作过程，并告诉你他们的收费标准。你猜这种方法效果会怎么样？

Alternatively, people could try direct observation, but that’s also tough. You’d have to cozy up to the mechanics in all the different shops and convince them to let you watch what they did and tell you how much they charged. Guess how well that would work.

最后，人们还可以通过广告获取信息。但广告并非总是可信的，人们通常对推销手段持怀疑态度。大多数修车广告都会声称价格优惠、技术精湛，但如果不亲自核实，很难确定真假。

Finally, people could get their information from advertisements. But ads aren’t always trustworthy, and people are generally skeptical of persuasion attempts. Most ads for mechanics will say they have great prices and do good work, but without really checking, it’s hard to know for sure.

故事可以解决这个问题。它们提供了一种生动有趣的方式，让人们能够快速便捷地获取大量知识。一个关于修理工免费修好车的好故事，胜过几十次观察和多年的反复试验。故事省时省力，让人们能够以易于记忆的方式获取所需信息。

Stories solve this problem. They provide a quick and easy way for people to acquire lots of knowledge in a vivid and engaging fashion. One good story about a mechanic who fixed the problem without charging is worth dozens of observations and years of trial and error. Stories save time and hassle and give people the information they need in a way that’s easy to remember.

你可以把故事看作是类比证明。我无法确定如果我从Lands' End买东西，就能享受到我表弟享受到的同样优质的客户服务。但仅仅因为这种好事发生在像我这样的人身上，就让我觉得我也很有可能遇到同样的情况。

You can think of stories as providing proof by analogy. There is no way to be sure that if I buy something from Lands’ End, I’ll get the same wonderful customer service my cousin received. But the mere fact that it happened to someone who is like me makes me feel that there is a pretty good chance it will happen to me too.

人们更倾向于质疑故事本身，而不是广告宣传。Lands' End 的代表可能会告诉我们他们的客户服务很棒，但正如我们之前讨论的，他们试图推销产品的事实让人难以相信。而个人经历的故事则更难让人反驳。

People are also less likely to argue against stories than against advertising claims. Lands’ End representatives could tell us that they have great customer service, but as we discussed earlier, the fact that they are trying to sell something makes it difficult to believe them. It’s harder to argue with personal stories.

首先，很难反驳发生在特定人物身上的具体事情。谁会跟我的表弟说：“不，我觉得你在撒谎，Lands' End 不可能那么好”？几乎不可能。

First, it’s hard to disagree with a specific thing that happened to a specific person. What is someone going to tell my cousin, “No, I think you’re lying, there’s no way Lands’ End would be that nice”? Hardly.

其次，我们太过沉浸于某某人遭遇的事件中，以至于没有足够的认知资源去提出异议。我们太过热衷于追随叙事，以至于没有精力去质疑所说的话。所以最终，我们更容易被说服。

Second, we’re so caught up in the drama of what happened to so-and-so that we don’t have the cognitive resources to disagree. We’re so engaged in following the narrative that we don’t have the energy to question what is being said. So in the end, we’re much more likely to be persuaded.

—————

—————

人们不喜欢看起来像行走的广告牌。赛百味三明治连锁店提供七种脂肪含量低于6克的三明治。但没人会走到朋友面前，然后突然吐出一口这条信息。说出来不仅奇怪，而且不合时宜。当然，如果有人想减肥，这条信息确实很有价值，但除非减肥是谈话主题，或者某种情况促使人们思考减肥方法，否则他们不会主动提起。所以，赛百味有很多低脂选择这件事可能并不常被提及。

People don’t like to seem like walking advertisements. The Subway sandwich chain offers seven subs with less than six grams of fat. But no one is going to walk up to a friend and just spit out that information. Not only would it be weird, it would be out of context. Sure, this information is practically valuable if someone is trying to lose weight, but unless weight loss is the topic of conversation, or the situation triggers people to think about ways to lose weight, they’re not going to bring it up. So the fact that Subway has a bunch of low-fat options may not be brought up that often.

相比之下，贾里德的故事则截然不同。贾里德·福格尔靠吃赛百味三明治减掉了245磅（约111公斤）。不良的饮食习惯和缺乏运动导致他在大学期间体重飙升至425磅（约193公斤）。他体重如此之重，以至于选课的标准不再是课程内容，而是教室里的座位是否足够宽敞舒适。

Contrast that with the Jared story. Jared Fogle lost 245 pounds eating Subway sandwiches. Bad eating habits and lack of exercise led Jared to balloon to 425 pounds in college. He was so heavy that he picked his courses based on whether the classroom had large-enough seats for him to be comfortable rather than whether he liked the material.

但室友指出他的健康状况每况愈下后，贾里德决定采取行动。于是他开始了“赛百味减肥法”：几乎每天午餐都吃一个一英尺长的蔬菜三明治，晚餐吃一个六英寸长的火鸡三明治。三个月后，他坚持这种自我约束的饮食方式，减掉了近100磅。

But after his roommate pointed out that his health was getting worse, Jared decided to take action. So he started a “Subway diet”: almost every day he ate a foot-long veggie sub for lunch and a six-inch turkey sub for dinner. After three months of this self-imposed regimen he had lost almost 100 pounds.

但他并没有就此止步。贾里德坚持节食。很快，他的裤子尺寸就从惊人的60英寸减到了正常的34英寸腰围。他减掉了那么多体重，这都要感谢赛百味。

But he didn’t stop there. Jared kept up his diet. Soon his pants size had dropped from an enormous sixty inches to a normal thirty-four-inch waist. He lost all that weight and had Subway to thank.

贾里德的故事太精彩了，以至于人们即使不谈论减肥也会提起他。他减掉的体重令人印象深刻，但更令人震惊的是，他竟然靠吃赛百味三明治减掉了这么多体重。一个人靠吃快餐减掉了245磅？光是这个简介就足以吸引人们的眼球了。

The Jared story is so entertaining that people bring it up even when they’re not talking about weight loss. The amount of weight he lost is impressive, but even more astonishing is the fact that he lost it eating Subway sandwiches. A guy loses 245 pounds eating fast food? The summary alone is enough to draw people in.

这个故事之所以被广泛传播，原因有很多，我们在前面的章节中已经讨论过了。它引人入胜（社交价值），令人感到惊讶和惊奇（情感价值），并且提供了关于健康快餐的实用信息（实用价值）。

The story gets shared for many of the reasons we talked about in prior chapters. It’s remarkable (Social Currency), evokes surprise and amazement (Emotion), and provides useful information about healthy fast food (Practical Value).

人们谈论贾里德并非出于帮助赛百味的目的，但赛百味仍然从中受益，因为它成为了故事的一部分。听众在了解贾里德的同时，也了解了赛百味。他们了解到：（1）赛百味虽然看起来像快餐，但实际上提供许多健康的选择。（2）健康到有人可以通过食用赛百味来减肥。（3）而且是减掉很多体重。此外，（4）有人可以连续三个月只吃赛百味的三明治，并且仍然会继续光顾。所以，赛百味的食物一定非常美味。尽管人们讲述这个故事是因为贾里德，但听众仍然了解到了所有这些关于赛百味的信息。

People don’t talk about Jared because they want to help Subway, but Subway still benefits because it is part of the narrative. Listeners learn about Jared, but they also learn about Subway along the way. They learn that (1) while Subway might seem like fast food, it actually offers a number of healthy options. (2) So healthy that someone could lose weight by eating them. (3) A lot of weight. Further, (4) someone could eat mostly Subway sandwiches for three months and still come back for more. So the food must be pretty tasty. Listeners learn all this about Subway, even though people tell the story because of Jared.

这就是故事的魔力。信息往往伪装成闲聊而传播开来。

And that is the magic of stories. Information travels under the guise of what seems like idle chatter.

建造特洛伊木马

BUILD A TROJAN HORSE

因此，故事为人们提供了一种轻松谈论产品和理念的方式。赛百味可能有低脂三明治，兰兹角可能有优质的客户服务，但除了对话中的特定触发点之外，人们需要一个理由来提及这些信息。而好的故事恰好提供了这个理由。它们提供了一种心理上的掩护，使人们能够谈论产品或理念，而不会显得像是在做广告。

Stories thus give people an easy way to talk about products and ideas. Subway might have low-fat subs, and Lands’ End might have great customer service, but outside of triggers in a conversation, people need a reason to bring that information up. And good stories provide that reason. They provide a sort of psychological cover that allows people to talk about a product or idea without seeming like an advertisement.

那么，我们如何利用故事来引发人们的讨论呢？

So how can we use stories to get people talking?

我们需要打造我们自己的特洛伊木马——一个能够让人们在谈论我们的产品或理念时分享的载体故事。

We need to build our own Trojan Horse—a carrier narrative that people will share, while talking about our product or idea along the way.

—————

—————

蒂姆·派珀从来没有妹妹。而且他从小就读男校。所以他一直觉得这有点荒谬。他的很多女朋友都有外貌上的缺陷。她们总是担心自己的头发太直，眼睛太浅，或者肤色不够白皙。派珀不理解。在他看来，她们已经足够漂亮了。

Tim Piper never had a sister. And he grew up going to an all-boys school. So he had always thought it was a little ridiculous that so many of his girlfriends had beauty issues. They were always worried that their hair was too straight, their eyes were too light, or their complexion wasn’t clear enough. Piper didn’t get it. They seemed pretty enough to him.

但在采访了数十名女孩后，派珀开始意识到媒体才是罪魁祸首。广告，以及整个媒体，都在向年轻女性灌输一种观念：她们自身存在缺陷，需要被“改造”。多年来，她们不断受到这些信息的轰炸，最终开始相信这些话。

But after interviewing dozens of girls, Piper started to realize that the media were to blame. Advertising, and the media in general, taught young women that something was wrong with them. That they needed fixing. And after years of being bombarded with those messages, women started to believe them.

如何才能帮助女性意识到这些广告是虚假的？如何才能让她们意识到广告中的画面与现实不符？

What would help women realize that these ads were fake? That the images being shown didn’t reflect reality?

一天晚上，他当时的女友正在化妆准备出门，这时他突然意识到，女孩们需要了解她们化妆前的真实模样，也就是模特们在化妆、发型设计、修图和Photoshop等后期处理之前，原本的模样。

One night his girlfriend at the time was putting on makeup to go out when it hit him. He realized that girls needed to be exposed to the before before the after. What models look like before the makeup and hair styling and retouching and Photoshop swoop in to make them “perfect.”

于是他拍了一部短片。

So he created a short film.

斯蒂芬妮直视镜头，向工作人员点头示意她准备开始。她长得挺漂亮，但并非那种会让她鹤立鸡群的美。她有一头深金色的羽毛状短发，发尾比较直。她的皮肤很好，但偶尔会有些许瑕疵。她看起来就像任何人——你的邻居、你的朋友、你的女儿。

Stephanie stares into the camera and nods her head to the crew that she is ready to begin. She is pretty, but not in a way that would make her stand out in a crowd. Her hair is dark blond, feathered, and relatively straight. Her skin is nice but a few blemishes mar it here and there. She looks as though she could be anyone—your neighbor, your friend, your daughter.

一盏明亮的灯亮起，化妆过程开始了。我们看着化妆师们为斯蒂芬妮加深眼妆，并用唇彩提亮她的嘴唇。他们为她涂抹粉底，再用腮红为她增添气色。他们修饰她的眉毛，拉长她的睫毛。他们卷曲、蓬松、造型，为她打造完美发型。

A bright light turns on, and the process begins. As we watch, makeup artists darken Stephanie’s eyes and highlight her lips with gloss. They apply foundation to her skin and blush to color her cheeks. They groom her eyebrows and lengthen her lashes. They curl and tease and style her hair.

然后摄影师带着相机出现了。他拍了几十张照片。风扇打开，她的头发看起来自然蓬松。她头发蓬乱。斯蒂芬妮时而微笑，时而挑逗地盯着镜头。最终，摄影师拍到了一张满意的照片。

Then the photographer appears with his camera. He takes dozens of photos. Fans are turned on so her hair appears naturally tousled. Stephanie alternately smiles and stares provocatively at the camera. Finally, the photographer gets a shot he likes.

但拍到一张完美的照片仅仅是开始。接下来是修图。斯蒂芬妮的照片被输入电脑，在我们眼前开始变形。她的嘴唇变得丰满，脖子变细变长，眼睛也变大了。这只是数十处修改中的一小部分。

But getting the perfect snapshot is only the beginning. Next comes the Photoshopping. Stephanie’s image is fed into a computer, and begins to morph before our eyes. Her lips are inflated. Her neck is thinned and lengthened. Her eyes are enlarged. These are only a handful of the dozens of changes that are made.

你现在正凝视着一张超模的快照。随着镜头向后移动，你发现这张照片被放在了化妆品广告牌上。屏幕渐黑，白色小字浮现出来：“难怪我们对美的认知会扭曲。”

You are now gazing at a snapshot of a supermodel. As the camera pans backward, you can see that the image has been placed on a billboard for a makeup campaign. The screen fades to black, and small words appear in white writing. “No wonder our perception of beauty is distorted.”

哇。这段视频太震撼了。它让我们深刻地意识到美容行业幕后究竟隐藏着多少不为人知的秘密。

Wow. This is a powerful clip. A great reminder of all that really goes on behind the scenes in the beauty industry.

但它除了能引发热烈的讨论之外，也是推广多芬产品的巧妙特洛伊木马。

But in addition to being a great conversation piece, it’s also a clever Trojan Horse for Dove products.

—————

—————

媒体，尤其是美容行业，往往对女性形象进行扭曲的描绘。模特通常身材高挑纤瘦。杂志上的女性都拥有完美无瑕的肌肤和洁白整齐的牙齿。广告大肆宣传他们的产品能让你变得更好：更年轻的脸庞、更丰满的嘴唇、更柔嫩的肌肤。

The media in general, and the beauty industry in particular, tend to paint a skewed picture of women. Models are usually tall and skinny. Magazines show women with flawless complexions and perfect teeth. Ads scream that their products can transform you into a better you. Younger face, fuller lips, softer skin.

不出所料，这些信息对女性的自我认知产生了巨大的负面影响。只有2%的女性认为自己漂亮。超过三分之二的女性认为媒体设定的审美标准不切实际，她们永远无法达到，无论她们多么努力。这种无法达到期望的感觉甚至影响到年轻女孩。黑发女孩希望自己是金发女郎，红发女孩讨厌自己的雀斑。

Not surprisingly, these messages have a hugely negative impact on how women see themselves. Only 2 percent of women describe themselves as beautiful. More than two-thirds believe that the media has set an unrealistic standard of beauty that they’ll never be able to achieve. No matter how hard they try. This feeling of not living up to expectations even affects young girls. Dark-haired girls wish they were blond. Redheads hate their freckles.

Piper 的视频名为《进化》，揭示了我们每天目睹的那些惊艳影像背后的制作过程。它提醒人们，这些美艳动人的女性并非真实存在。她们是幻想的产物，是仅以真实人物为原型创作的虚构形象，运用了数字编辑的各种魔力精心打造而成。这段视频既真实又令人震惊，同时也引人深思。

Piper’s video, entitled “Evolution,” gives a behind-the-scenes look at what goes into making the images we are bombarded with every day. It reminds people that these stunning-looking women are not real. They are fantasies, fictions only loosely based on actual people. Concocted using all the magic that digital editing can provide. The clip is as raw and shocking as it is thought provoking.

但这部影片并非由热心市民或行业监督机构赞助。派珀与健康美容产品制造商多芬合作拍摄了这部影片，作为其“真美运动”的一部分。多芬希望借此赞美我们每个人与生俱来的自然体型差异，并鼓励女性自信地接纳自己。另一则香皂广告则以各种体型和身材的真实女性为主角，而非人们习以为常的骨瘦如柴的模特。

But the film wasn’t sponsored by concerned citizens or an industry watchdog group. Piper made the film in coordination with Dove, maker of health and beauty products, as part of its “Campaign for Real Beauty.” This was Dove’s effort to celebrate the natural physical variations we all have and then to inspire women to be confident and comfortable with themselves. Another ad for soap featured real women of all shapes and sizes, rather than the rail-thin models people are used to seeing.

不出所料，这场活动引发了广泛的讨论。美的定义是什么？媒体是如何塑造这些观念的？我们该如何改善现状？

Not surprisingly, the campaign sparked a great deal of discussion. What does it mean to be beautiful? How are the media shaping these perceptions? What can we do to make it better?

这场活动引发的不仅仅是争议。除了让这个问题更加公开化，让人们有机会谈论原本属于私密话题之外，这场活动还引发了人们对多芬的思考和讨论。

The campaign created more than just controversy. In addition to making the issue more Public, and giving people an excuse to talk about a topic that would have otherwise been private, the campaign also got them thinking, and talking, about Dove.

该公司因在广告宣传中使用真实人物并引发人们对这一复杂而重要议题的讨论而备受赞誉。而制作成本仅略高于10万美元的广告片《进化》却获得了超过1600万的点击量，为公司带来了数亿美元的曝光价值。该广告片荣获多项行业大奖，并使公司网站流量比多芬2006年超级碗广告带来的流量增长了三倍多。多芬的经历销售额实现两位数增长。

The company was commended for using real people in its campaigns and for getting people to talk about this complicated but important issue. And “Evolution,” which cost only a little over one hundred thousand dollars to make, got more than 16 million views. It netted the company hundreds of millions of dollars in exposure. The clip won numerous industry awards and more than tripled the website traffic the company received from Dove’s 2006 Super Bowl ad. Dove experienced double-digit sales growth.

“进化”之所以广为流传，是因为多芬抓住了人们早已想要讨论的话题：不切实际的审美标准。这是一个极具争议性的话题，人们原本可能不敢轻易提及。“进化”将这个问题公之于众，让人们得以倾诉不满，并思考解决方案。而多芬品牌也从中获益。它通过引发关于审美标准的讨论，成功地将品牌融入其中。通过打造一个充满情感的故事，多芬创造了一个能够承载品牌传播的载体。

“Evolution” was widely shared because Dove latched onto something people already wanted to talk about: unrealistic beauty norms. It’s a highly emotional issue, but something so controversial that people might have been afraid to bring up otherwise. “Evolution” brought it out in the open. It let people air their grievances and think about solutions. And along the way the brand benefited. Dove got people talking by starting a conversation about beauty norms—but the brand was smuggled in as part of the discussion. By creating an emotional story, Dove created a vessel that carried its brand along for the ride.

这就引出了罗恩·本西蒙的故事。

And that brings us to the story of Ron Bensimhon.

让病毒式传播更有价值

MAKING VIRALITY VALUABLE

2004年8月16日，加拿大选手罗恩·本西蒙小心翼翼地脱下热身裤，走到三米跳板边缘。他之前曾多次尝试从这个高度跳水，但从未在如此重要的赛事中出手。这是雅典奥运会，世界最大的体育舞台，也是竞技体育的巅峰。但罗恩似乎毫不怯场。他甩掉紧张，高举双手。随着观众的欢呼声，他从跳板一跃而下，完成了一个标准的腹部着水动作。

On August 16, 2004, Canadian Ron Bensimhon carefully shed his warm-up pants and stepped to the edge of the three-meter springboard. He had attempted dives from this height many times before, but never during an event of this magnitude. It was the Athens Olympics. The world’s biggest stage for sport and the pinnacle of athletic competition. But Ron did not seem fazed. He shook off the jitters and raised his hands high above his head. As the crowd roared, he leapt off the end of the board and completed a full belly flop.

跳水时腹部着地？在奥运会上？罗恩肯定崩溃了。但当他从水里出来时，却显得很平静，甚至还有些高兴。他游了一会儿，对着观众们卖力表演，然后慢慢游到泳池边，那里已经站着一群奥运官员和保安人员。

A belly flop? In the Olympics? Surely Ron must have been devastated. But as he emerged from the water he seemed calm, happy even. He swam around for a few moments, hamming it up for the audience and then slowly swam to the side of the pool, where he was met by a platoon of Olympic officials and security guards.

罗恩算是闯入了奥运会。但他其实并不在加拿大游泳队。事实上，他根本就不是奥运选手。完全不是。他自称是世界上最著名的裸奔者，而且他还闯入了奥运会。这是宣传噱头的一部分。

Ron had broken into the Olympics. He wasn’t actually on the Canadian swim team. In fact, he wasn’t an Olympic athlete at all. He was the self-proclaimed most famous streaker in the world, and he had crashed the Olympics as part of a publicity stunt.

—————

—————

罗恩从跳板上跳下来时，他虽然没全裸，但也没穿泳裤。他穿着蓝色芭蕾舞裙和白色波点紧身裤。胸前赫然印着一家网络赌场的名字：GoldenPalace.com。

When Ron jumped off the springboard, he wasn’t naked, but he wasn’t wearing swim trunks either. He wore a blue tutu and white polka dot tights. And emblazoned across his chest was the name of an Internet casino, GoldenPalace.com.

这并非金宫娱乐公司第一次搞宣传噱头（尽管该公司声称罗恩的噱头是他们不知情的）。2004年，该公司在eBay上以2.8万美元的价格拍下了一份烤奶酪三明治，一些人认为三明治上印有圣母玛利亚的画像。2005年，该公司支付给一位女士1.5万美元，让她把名字改成GoldenPalace.com。但这次让本西蒙（Bensimhon）——人们都称他为“泳池里的傻瓜”——跳进泳池的噱头，却是规模最大的一次。数百万观众观看了这场闹剧，世界各地的媒体都进行了报道。这件事也引发了广泛的口口相传。有人穿着芭蕾舞裙闯入奥运会，然后跳进泳池？真是个惊人的故事。相当了不起。

This wasn’t the first Golden Palace publicity stunt (though the company did say that Ron’s stunt was done without its knowledge). In 2004 it bid $28,000 on eBay for a grilled cheese sandwich that some people believed displayed an image of the Virgin Mary. In 2005 it gave a woman $15,000 to change her name to GoldenPalace.com. But the stunt with the “fool in the pool,” as Bensimhon has been called, was one of the biggest. Millions of people were watching, and the story got picked up by news outlets around the world. It also got a huge amount of word-of-mouth chatter. Someone crashing the Olympics and diving into a pool in a tutu? What a story. Pretty remarkable.

但随着时间推移，人们不再谈论赌场的事。当然，一些目睹本西蒙跳跃的人会去网站查看究竟发生了什么。但大多数分享这个故事的人谈论的是特技本身，而不是网站。他们讨论的是，这次干扰是否影响了中国跳水运动员——他们在特技表演后最后一跳失误，最终与金牌失之交臂。他们讨论的是奥运会的安保措施，以及为什么有人能在如此重要的赛事中如此轻易地蒙混过关。他们还讨论的是本西蒙的审判，以及他是否会被判入狱。

But as the days ticked by, people didn’t talk about the casino. Sure, some people who saw Bensimhon’s jump went to the website to try to figure out what was going on. But most people who shared the story talked about the stunt, not the website. They talked about whether the interruption threw off the Chinese divers, who flubbed their final dive right after the trick and lost the gold medal. They talked about security at the Olympics and how someone could slip through so easily at such a major event. And they talked about Bensimhon’s trial and whether he would serve jail time.

他们没有谈到GoldenPalace.com网站。为什么？

What they didn’t talk about was GoldenPalace.com. Why?

—————

—————

营销专家将“跳水傻瓜”事件视为史上最糟糕的游击营销失败案例之一。他们通常会抨击此举扰乱了比赛节奏，毁掉了那些毕生刻苦训练的运动员的荣耀时刻。他们还指出，这导致本西蒙被捕并被罚款。所有这些都足以说明，本西蒙的“跳水傻瓜”事件确实是一次彻底的失败。

Marketing experts talk about “the fool in the pool” as one of the worst guerrilla marketing failures of all time. Usually they deride it for having disrupted the competition and ruining the moment for athletes who had trained all their lives. They also point out that it led to Bensimhon being arrested and fined. These are all good reasons to consider Bensimhon’s belly flop, well, a flop.

但我还想补充一点。这次噱头和它试图推广的产品毫无关系。

But I’d like to add another one to the list. The stunt had nothing to do with the product it was trying to promote.

没错，人们都在谈论这个噱头，但他们并没有谈论赌场本身。波点紧身裤、芭蕾舞裙，以及闯入奥运会跳入泳池，这些都是绝佳的新闻素材。正因如此，人们才会谈论它们。所以，如果目的是为了让人们更加关注奥运会的安保措施，或是为一种新款紧身裤造势，那么这个噱头无疑成功了。

Yes, people talked about the stunt, but they didn’t talk about the casino. Polka dot tights, tutus, and breaking into the Olympics to dive into a pool are all great story material. That’s why people talked about them. So if the goal was to get people to think more about security at the Olympics or get attention for a new style of tights, the stunt succeeded.

但这和赌场一点关系都没有。

But it had nothing to do with casinos. Not even in the slightest.

所以人们谈论这个非凡的故事，却只字未提赌场，因为赌场无关紧要。他们或许会提到本西蒙有赞助商，但不会提及赌场，要么是因为赌场太过无关紧要以至于他们忘记了，要么是因为赌场并不能让故事更有说服力。这就像建造了一匹精美的特洛伊木马，却忘了在里面放任何东西。

So people talked about the remarkable story but left the casino out because it was irrelevant. They might have mentioned that Bensimhon was sponsored by someone but didn’t mention the casino either because it was so irrelevant that they forgot, or because it didn’t make the story any better. It’s like building a magnificent Trojan Horse but forgetting to put anything inside.

—————

—————

在尝试进行口碑营销时，许多人忽略了一个重要的细节。他们过于关注如何让人们谈论这件事，却忽略了真正重要的部分：人们在谈论什么。

When trying to generate word of mouth, many people forget one important detail. They focus so much on getting people to talk that they ignore the part that really matters: what people are talking about.

这就是创作与主题无关的内容的问题所在。它旨在推广的产品或理念。人们谈论内容和人们谈论创作该内容的公司、组织或个人之间有着很大的区别。

That’s the problem with creating content that is unrelated to the product or idea it is meant to promote. There’s a big difference between people talking about content and people talking about the company, organization, or person that created that content.

依云著名的“轮滑宝贝”视频也遇到了同样的问题。这段视频展示了一群穿着尿布的婴儿，他们穿着轮滑鞋表演各种特技。他们互相跳跃、翻越栅栏，并做出同步动作，所有动作都伴随着歌曲《Rapper's Delight》的节奏。虽然婴儿的身体明显是动画制作的，但他们的脸看起来很逼真，这使得这段视频非常引人注目。该视频的点击量超过5000万次，并被吉尼斯世界纪录认证为……史上观看次数最多的网络广告。

Evian’s famous “Roller Babies” video had the same problem. The clip shows what appear to be diaper-wearing babies doing tricks on roller skates. They jump over one another, hop over fences, and do synchronized moves, all to the beat of the song “Rapper’s Delight.” The babies’ bodies are clearly animated, but their faces look real, making the video remarkable to watch. The video got more than 50 million views, and Guinness World Records declared it the most viewed online advertisement in history.

但你或许会认为所有这些关注都会对品牌有利，事实并非如此。同年，依云的市场份额反而下降了。销售额下降了近25%。

But while you might think that all this attention would benefit the brand, it didn’t. That same year Evian lost market share and sales dropped almost 25 percent.

问题在于？滑旱冰的宝宝固然可爱，但这和依云矿泉水毫无关系。所以人们转发了这段视频，但这并没有给依云品牌带来任何好处。

The problem? Roller-skating babies are cute, but they have nothing to do with Evian. So people shared the clip, but that didn’t benefit the brand.

—————

—————

因此，关键不仅在于让内容迅速传播，更在于使其对赞助公司或机构具有价值。不仅仅是传播，而是有价值的传播。

The key, then, is to not only make something viral, but also make it valuable to the sponsoring company or organization. Not just virality but valuable virality.

以我们在本书开头提到的巴克莱高级餐厅（Barclay Prime）售价一百美元的芝士牛排为例。相比于跳舞的婴儿和瓶装水，一份昂贵的高档芝士牛排和一家昂贵的高档牛排餐厅显然更相关。而且，这并非噱头，而是巴克莱菜单上的真实选项。此外，它也直接体现了餐厅希望消费者对其菜肴的印象：品质上乘却不拘泥于形式，奢华而不失创意。

Take Barclay Prime’s hundred-dollar cheesesteak that we talked about at the beginning of the book. Compared with dancing babies and bottled water, an expensive, high-end cheesesteak and an expensive, high-end steak restaurant are clearly more related. And the item wasn’t just a stunt, it was an actual option on Barclay’s menu. Further, it directly spoke to the inferences the restaurant wanted consumers to make about its food: high quality but not stuffy, lavish but creative.

当品牌或产品优势在于……时，病毒式传播的价值最高。它对故事至关重要。它已经深深融入叙事之中，以至于人们在讲述故事时无法绕开它。

Virality is most valuable when the brand or product benefit is integral to the story. When it’s woven so deeply into the narrative that people can’t tell the story without mentioning it.

我最喜欢的病毒式传播案例之一来自埃及乳制品公司 Panda，该公司生产各种不同的奶酪产品。

One of my favorite examples of valuable virality comes from the Egyptian dairy company Panda, which makes a variety of different cheese products.

这些广告总是以看似无害的场景开始：工人们在讨论午饭吃什么，或者医院护士在查看病人的情况。一处场景中，一位父亲正带着儿子在超市购物。“爸爸，我们买点熊猫牌奶酪怎么样？”儿子边走边问。“够了！”父亲回答，“我们购物车里的东西已经够多了。”

The commercials always start innocuously: workers talking about what to have for lunch, or a hospital nurse checking in on a patient. In one spot a father is grocery shopping with his son. “Dad, why don’t we get some Panda cheese?” the son asks as they walk by the dairy aisle. “Enough!” the father replies. “We have enough stuff in the cart already.”

然后熊猫出现了。或者更确切地说，是一个穿着熊猫服的人。这一幕的荒诞程度简直难以用语言形容。没错，一只巨大的熊猫突然出现在一家杂货店的中央。或者在另一个广告里，出现在一间办公室里。又或者在另一个广告里，出现在一家诊所里。

Then the panda appears. Or rather, a man in a panda suit. There’s simply no way to describe adequately the ludicrousness of this moment. Yes, a giant panda is suddenly standing in the middle of a grocery store. Or in a different commercial, an office. Or in another, a medical clinic.

在超市的视频中，父子俩目瞪口呆地盯着熊猫，显然被逗乐了。伴随着巴迪·霍利的歌曲，父子俩一会儿看看货架上的熊猫奶酪，一会儿又看看熊猫。如此反复。父亲咽了口唾沫。

In the grocery-store video, the father and son stare at the panda, obviously dumbfounded. As a Buddy Holly tune plays, the boy and his father look at the Panda cheese on the shelf, then back to the panda. And back and forth again. The father gulps.

然后，一片混乱（请原谅我用了这个双关语）。

Then, pandemonium ensues (excuse the pun).

熊猫缓缓走向购物车，平静地将双手放在身体两侧，然后把它翻了过来。

The panda slowly walks toward the shopping cart, calmly places both hands on its sides, and flips it over.

食物散落一地，意大利面、罐头食品和饮料到处都是。父亲和熊猫分别站在购物车的两端，对视不休。一阵漫长的沉默后，熊猫又踢了一脚翻倒的食物。“永远不要拒绝熊猫！”一个声音响起，熊猫的手在屏幕上闪现出商品。

Food flies all over the aisle—pasta, canned goods, and liquids everywhere. The stare-down continues as the father and the panda stand on opposite ends of the cart. A long pause ensues. Then the panda kicks the overturned food for good measure. “Never say no to Panda,” a voice intones as a panda hand flashes the product on the screen.

这则广告以及其他类似的广告，播出时机把握得恰到好处，而且非常搞笑。我给包括大学生在内的所有人都看过。给金融服务行业的高管们听，大家都笑得肚子都疼了。

The commercial and others like it are impeccably timed and utterly hilarious. I’ve shown them to everyone from college kids to financial service executives and everyone laughs until their sides hurt.

但请注意，这些视频之所以如此精彩，不仅仅是因为它们搞笑。即使广告中的人穿着鸡服，或者广告语是“永远不要拒绝吉姆的二手车”，广告也一样好笑。无论广告中的动物是什么，也无论广告宣传的是什么产品，穿着动物服踢杂货的画面本身就很有趣。

But note that what makes these videos so great is not just that they’re funny. The commercial would have been just as funny if the guy was dressed in a chicken suit or if the tagline was, “Never say no to Jim’s used cars.” Someone dressed in an animal suit kicking groceries is funny regardless of which animal it is or what product it’s for.

它们之所以成功——也是病毒式传播的绝佳范例——是因为品牌是故事不可或缺的一部分。提到熊猫是对话中自然而然发生的事情。事实上，你得费尽心思才能不提及熊猫，否则故事就说得通（更别提让人们理解它的笑点了）。因此，故事中最精彩的部分和品牌名称完美地融合在一起。这不仅增加了人们在讲述故事时谈论熊猫品牌的可能性，也增加了他们在几天甚至几周后仍然记得这则广告宣传的是哪个产品的可能性。熊猫是故事中不可或缺的一部分，是叙事中不可或缺的元素。

They’re successful—and great examples of valuable virality—because the brand is an integral part of the stories. Mentioning the panda is a natural part of the conversation. In fact, you’d have to try pretty hard not to mention the panda and still have the story make sense (much less get people to understand why it’s funny). So the best part of the story and the brand name are perfectly intertwined. That increases the chance not only that people telling the story will talk about Panda the brand, but also that they will remember what product the commercial is for, days or even weeks later. Panda is part and parcel of the story. It’s an essential part of the narrative.

Blendtec 的“Will It Blend? ”活动也是如此。那些搅拌机撕裂 iPhone 的视频片段，简直让人难以置信。他们完全没提到搅拌机，也没意识到视频中展示的Blendtec搅拌机必须极其坚固耐用——强大到几乎可以搅拌任何东西。而这恰恰是Blendtec想要传达的信息。

The same can be said for Blendtec’s Will It Blend? campaign. It’s impossible to tell the story of the clips where the blender tears through an iPhone without talking about a blender. And without recognizing that the Blendtec blender in the videos must be extremely tough—so strong that it can blend almost anything. Which is exactly what Blendtec wants to communicate.

—————

—————

在打造具有病毒式传播潜力的内容时，真正有价值的病毒式传播至关重要。这意味着要将理念或预期收益融入叙事的核心。这就像一部优秀侦探小说的情节。有些细节对叙事至关重要，而有些则无关紧要。案发时嫌疑人是否各不相同？至关重要。侦探在仔细思考案情细节时晚餐吃了什么？这并不重要。

In trying to craft contagious content, valuable virality is critical. That means making the idea or desired benefit a key part of the narrative. It’s like the plot of a good detective story. Some details are critical to the narrative and some are extraneous. Where were the different suspects at the time of the murder? Critical. What was the detective eating for dinner while he mulled over the details of the case? Not so important.

同样的区别也适用于我们一直在讨论的内容。以罗恩·本西蒙的奥运会特技为例。跳进泳池？至关重要。GoldenPalace.com ？几乎无关紧要。

The same distinction can be applied to the content we’ve been discussing. Take Ron Bensimhon’s Olympic stunt. Jumping into a pool? Critical. GoldenPalace.com? Pretty much irrelevant.

当人们复述故事时，这些不同类型细节的重要性就更加显而易见了。想想特洛伊木马的故事。它流传了数千年。虽然有文字记载，但人们所了解的大部分细节都来自他人的讲述。那么，人们会记住并复述哪些细节呢？这并非随机的。关键细节得以保留，而无关紧要的细节则被遗忘。

The importance of these different types of details becomes even clearer when people retell the story. Think about the story of the Trojan Horse. It has survived for thousands of years. There is a written account of the story, but most of the details people know come from hearing someone else talk about it. But which details people remember and retell? It isn’t random. Critical details stick around, while irrelevant ones drop out.

心理学家戈登·奥尔波特和约瑟夫·波兹曼五十多年前就研究过类似的问题。他们非常关注谣言在人与人之间传播的过程中会发生什么变化。谣言在传播过程中是保持不变还是会发生变化？如果发生变化，谣言的演变是否存在可预测的模式？

Psychologists Gordon Allport and Joseph Postman examined a similar issue more than fifty years ago. They were keenly interested in what happened to rumors as they spread from person to person. Did the stories stay the same as they were transmitted or did they change? And if they changed, were there predictable patterns in how rumors evolved?

为了解答这个问题，他们让人们玩了一个我们大多数人都会称之为“传话游戏”的游戏。

To address this question, they had people play what most of us would describe as a game of Telephone.

首先，有人看到了一张描绘场景细节的图片——其中一张图片显示的是一群人在地铁车厢里。这辆车似乎是第八大道快线，正驶过戴克曼街。车厢里贴着各种广告，五个人坐在座位上，其中包括一位拉比和一位抱着婴儿的母亲。但图片的焦点是两个正在争吵的男人。他们站着，其中一人指着另一人，手里拿着一把刀。

First, someone was shown a picture of a detailed situation—in one case, a group of people on a subway car. The car appears to be an Eighth Avenue Express and it is going past Dyckman Street. There are various advertisements posted on the car, and five people are seated, including a rabbi and a mother carrying her baby. But the focus of the picture is two men having an argument. They are standing up, and one is pointing at the other and holding a knife.

然后，传话游戏就开始了。第一个人（发送者）要向另一个人（接收者）描述图片，接收者看不到图片。发送者将各种细节传达给接收者。他认为合适。然后，发送者离开房间，另一个人进入。这个新人成为接收者，而原来的接收者则成为发送者，将图像中的内容分享给同样没有看过图像的新接收者。之后，原来的接收者离开房间，另一个人进入，游戏重复进行，直到第四人、第五人，最终到第六人。奥尔波特和波斯特曼随后研究了哪些故事细节在传播链中得以保留。

Then the game of Telephone starts. The first person (transmitter) is asked to describe the picture to someone else (receiver), who cannot see it. The transmitter conveys the various details as he sees fit. The transmitter then leaves the room and a new person enters. That new person becomes the receiver, and the original receiver becomes the transmitter, sharing what happened in the image with the new receiver, who also hasn’t seen the image. Then the original receiver leaves the room, a new person enters, and the game is repeated to a fourth, fifth, and eventually sixth person. Allport and Postman then looked at which story details persisted along the transmission chain.

他们发现，每次谣言传播，信息量都会急剧下降。大约70%的故事细节在前五到六次传播中就丢失了。

They found that the amount of information shared dropped dramatically each time the rumor was shared. Around 70 percent of the story details were lost in the first five to six transmissions.

但故事不仅变短了，而且还围绕着要点或关键细节进行了精简。在数十条传播链中，都存在一些共同的模式。某些细节总是被省略，而某些细节则总是被保留。在关于地铁车厢的故事中，第一个讲述者提到了所有细节。他们说那节地铁车厢看起来像是第八大道快车，它正驶过戴克曼街，车厢里有好几个人，其中两个人正在争吵。

But the stories didn’t just become shorter: they were also sharpened around the main point or key details. Across dozens of transmission chains there were common patterns. Certain details were consistently left out and certain details were consistently retained. In the story about the subway car the first person telling the story mentioned all the details. They talked about how the subway car seemed to be an Eighth Avenue Express, how it was going past Dyckman Street, and how there were a number of people on it, two of them arguing.

但随着故事在电话线上流传，许多无关紧要的细节都被省略了。人们不再讨论是哪种地铁，也不再讨论它行驶的路线，而是把注意力集中在争吵本身，比如一个人指着另一个人，挥舞着刀子。就像侦探故事一样，人们只提及关键细节，而忽略了无关紧要的部分。

But as the story was passed on down the telephone line, many of the unimportant details got stripped out. People stopped talking about what type of subway it was or where it was traveling and instead focused on the argument. The fact that one person was pointing at the other and brandishing a knife. Just as in the detective story, people mentioned the critical details and left out the extraneous ones.

—————

—————

如果你想打造具有病毒式传播潜力的内容，不妨尝试构建自己的“特洛伊木马”。但务必确保你考虑的是真正有价值的病毒式传播。确保你想让人们记住并传播的信息对你的叙事至关重要。当然，你可以让你的叙事有趣、出人意料或引人入胜。但如果人们无法将内容与你联系起来，即使它爆红网络，对你也无济于事。

If you want to craft contagious content, try to build your own Trojan Horse. But make sure you think about valuable virality. Make sure the information you want people to remember and transmit is critical to the narrative. Sure, you can make your narrative funny, surprising, or entertaining. But if people don’t connect the content back to you, it’s not going to help you very much. Even if it goes viral.

所以，打造一个充满社交货币、触发式、情感化、公开且具有实际价值的特洛伊木马，但别忘了把你的信息隐藏其中。确保你想要传达的信息与剧情完美融合，以至于人们在讲述故事时无法忽略它。

So build a Social Currency–laden, Triggered, Emotional, Public, Practically Valuable Trojan Horse, but don’t forget to hide your message inside. Make sure your desired information is so embedded into the plot that people can’t tell the story without it.

结语

Epilogue

问问三个人上次在哪里做的美甲，很可能至少有一个人是找越南美甲师做的。但事情的来龙去脉或许会让你大吃一惊。一切都始于二十位女性和一套长长的珊瑚色美甲。

Ask three people where they got their last manicure, and chances are good that at least one of them had a Vietnamese nail technician. But the story of how it got that way might surprise you. It started with twenty women and a set of long coral nails.

她在家乡曾是一名高中教师，但是1975年，当Thuan Le抵达希望村时，她身无长物，只有身上穿的衣服。这座位于萨克拉门托郊外的帐篷城是西贡沦陷后逃往美国的越南难民的临时安置点。营地里挤满了新移民，既充满希望，也弥漫着绝望。人们怀揣着为自己和家人创造更美好生活的梦想来到美国，但由于英语水平有限，他们的选择十分有限。

She’d been a high school teacher in her home country, but when Thuan Le arrived at Hope Village in 1975, she had nothing but the clothes on her back. The tent city outside Sacramento was a holding ground for Vietnamese refugees who escaped to America after the fall of Saigon. Teeming with new immigrants, the camp simultaneously brimmed with hope and despair. People had come to America with dreams of a better life for themselves and their families, but with little English knowledge, so the possibilities were limited.

曾出演阿尔弗雷德·希区柯克电影《群鸟》的女演员蒂比·海德伦被难民的困境所触动，每隔几天就会去希望村探望她们。海德伦想要提供帮助，于是成为了部分妇女的导师。这些勤劳的女性曾在越南担任过企业主、教师和政府官员，她们渴望投入工作。海德伦被她们的故事深深打动。越南人。他们注意到她身上有一点特别之处：她漂亮的指甲。

Actress Tippi Hedren, who had starred in Alfred Hitchcock’s The Birds, was drawn to the refugees’ plight and would visit Hope Village every few days. Hedren wanted to help, so she became a mentor to some of the women. Former business owners, teachers, and government officials in Vietnam, these industrious women were eager to get to work. Hedren was enchanted by their stories of Vietnam. They, in turn, noticed something about her: her beautiful nails.

女人们都羡慕海德伦那光泽亮丽的浅粉色指甲，于是她每周都请美甲师来教她们。教她们如何修剪角质层、包扎指甲、去除老茧。这些女人学得很快，她们不仅在海德伦身上练习，也在自己身上练习，还跟任何她们能接触到的人练习。

The women admired Hedren’s glossy light pink fingernails, so she brought her manicurist in once a week to give them lessons. How to trim cuticles, wrap nails, and remove calluses. The women were quick studies and practiced on Hedren, themselves, and anyone they could get their hands on.

很快，一个计划就制定好了。赫德伦为这些妇女争取到了附近一所美容学校的免费课程。她们学习了如何修眉、涂漆和修剪头发。之后，赫德伦四处打听，帮助勒和其他妇女在圣莫尼卡及周边城市找到了工作。

Soon a plan was hatched. Hedren got the women free classes at a nearby beauty school. They learned how to file, paint, and trim. Then Hedren asked around and helped Le and the other women find jobs in Santa Monica and surrounding cities.

起初很艰难。美甲行业当时并不流行，竞争也很激烈。但Le和其他几位女性通过了执照考试，开始了自己的事业。她们努力工作，长时间辛勤劳作，接手那些别人不愿意做的活儿。这些女性勤奋努力，坚持不懈。她们赚到了钱，也一步步晋升。

It was tough at first. Manicures were not yet the rage and there was lots of competition. But Le and the other women passed their licensing exams and started doing business. They worked hard, labored long hours, and took the jobs no one else wanted. The women were diligent and kept at it. They made money and worked their way up.

看到Le的成功，她的几个朋友决定也进入这个行业。她们开设了最早一批由越南裔美国人拥有的美容院之一，并鼓励其他人效仿。

Seeing Le’s success, a few of her friends decided to get into the business. They opened one of the first beauty salons owned by Vietnamese Americans and encouraged others to do the same.

成功案例很快传播开来。成千上万来到美国寻求新机遇的越南人听说了其他人的做法，纷纷效仿。越南美甲店开始在萨克拉门托各地涌现，随后遍及加州其他地区，最终遍及全美。最初，这二十位女性引领了潮流，但很快，它便发展成为一种独特的风潮。

The success stories soon spread. The thousands of Vietnamese who came to the United States looking for new possibilities heard what others were doing, and they listened. Vietnamese nail salons started opening up all around Sacramento. Then through the rest of California. Then the entire country. These twenty women started the trend, but soon it had a life of its own.

如今，加州80%的美甲师是越南裔美国人。全美范围内，这一比例超过40%。

Today, 80 percent of manicurists in California are Vietnamese Americans. Nationwide the number is greater than 40 percent.

越南美甲店风靡一时。

Vietnamese nail salons became contagious.

—————

—————

Thuan、Tippi以及越南美甲店的兴起的故事非常精彩。但更令人惊讶的是，这并非个例。

The story of Thuan, Tippi, and the spread of Vietnamese nail salons is pretty amazing. But even more surprising is the fact that it’s not unique.

其他移民群体也占据了类似的市场地位。据估计，柬埔寨裔美国人拥有洛杉矶大约 80% 的甜甜圈店，而且纽约市65%的干洗店由韩国人拥有。19世纪50年代，波士顿60%的酒类商店由爱尔兰人经营。20世纪初，犹太人生产了85%的男装。这样的例子不胜枚举。

Other immigrant groups have cornered similar niches. Estimates suggest that Cambodian Americans own approximately 80 percent of the doughnut shops in Los Angeles, and that Koreans own 65 percent of the dry cleaners in New York City. In the 1850s, 60 percent of the liquor stores in Boston were run by Irishmen. In the early 1900s, Jews produced 85 percent of men’s clothes. The list goes on.

仔细想想，这些故事很有道理。人们移居到一个新的国家，开始寻找工作。虽然这些移民之前可能从事过各种技术性工作，但在新国家，他们的选择往往有限。语言障碍的存在，之前的证书或资格难以转移，而且他们的人脉也不如在家乡那么多。因此，移民们会寻求朋友和熟人的帮助。

When you think about it, these stories make a lot of sense. People move to a new country and start looking for work. But while the immigrants may have had various skilled jobs previously, their options in the new country are often limited. There is a language barrier, it’s tough to transfer previous certifications or qualifications, and they don’t have as many contacts as they had back home. So immigrants look to their friends and acquaintances for help.

正如我们在本书中讨论的其他产品和理念一样，社会影响和口碑传播发挥着重要作用。就业是新移民寻找工作时经常提及的话题（触发因素）。因此，他们会了解其他新移民从事了哪些工作（公众信息），并与他们交流最佳机会。这些已经站稳脚跟的移民希望提升自身形象（社会资本），并帮助他人（实用价值），所以他们会讲述一些关于他们认识的成功人士的精彩故事（情感诉求）（故事）。

And as with the rest of the products and ideas we’ve talked about throughout the book, social influence and word of mouth kick in. The topic of employment is frequent among new immigrants looking for work (Triggers). So they look to see what jobs other recent immigrants have taken (Public) and talk to them about the best opportunities. These more established immigrants want to look good (Social Currency) and help others (Practical Value) so they tell exciting (Emotion) narratives (Stories) about others they know who have been successful.

不久，这些新移民便效仿同龄人，从事同样的职业。

Soon these new immigrants follow their peers and pursue the same line of work.

—————

—————

越南美甲师的故事，以及移民对职业的普遍选择，凸显了我们在本书中讨论过的许多观点。

The story of Vietnamese manicurists, and immigrants’ choice of occupations more generally, highlights a number of points we’ve discussed throughout the book.

首先，任何产品、理念或行为都可能具有传染性。我们讨论过搅拌机（“Will It Blend?”）、能量棒（“Please Don't Tell”）和早餐麦片（“Cheerios”）。“自然而然”令人兴奋的产品包括折扣购物（“Rue La La”）和高档餐厅（“Barclay Prime”的百元芝士牛排），以及一些不太常见的热门商品，例如玉米（“Ken Craig 的‘Clean Ears Everytime’”）和在线搜索（“Google 的‘Parisian Love’”）。产品（例如 iPod 的白色耳机）和服务（例如 Hotmail），以及非营利组织（“Movember”和“Livestrong”乐队）、健康行为（“Man Drinks Fat”）和整个行业（“越南美甲沙龙”），甚至肥皂（“Dove 的‘Evolution’”），都可能被社会影响力所感染。

First, any product, idea, or behavior can be contagious. We’ve talked about blenders (Will It Blend?), bars (Please Don’t Tell), and breakfast cereals (Cheerios). “Naturally” exciting products, like discount shopping (Rue La La) and high-end restaurants (Barclay Prime’s hundred-dollar cheesesteak) and less traditionally buzz-worthy goods like corn (Ken Craig’s “Clean Ears Everytime”) and online search (Google’s “Parisian Love”). Products (iPod’s white headphones) and services (Hotmail) but also nonprofits (Movember and Livestrong bands), health behaviors (“Man Drinks Fat”), and whole industries (Vietnamese nail salons). Even soap (Dove’s “Evolution”). Social influence helps all sorts of products and ideas catch on.

其次，我们发现，社会流行病并非由少数有“影响力”的特殊人物引起，而是由产品和理念本身驱动的。

Second, we saw that rather than being caused by a handful of special “influential” people, social epidemics are driven by the products and ideas themselves.

当然，每个伟大的故事都有一个英雄。蒂比·海德伦帮助越南女性学习美甲，乔治·赖特则提出了创办“Will It Blend?”的创意。但尽管这些人提供了最初的火花，他们也只是故事的一小部分。社会学家解释了为什么少数几个酷炫或人脉广泛的人（所谓的“有影响力的人”）对社会流行病的影响远没有我们想象的那么大。邓肯·沃茨用森林火灾做了一个恰当的比喻。有些森林火灾规模更大，但没人会认为火灾的大小取决于最初火花的特殊性。大型森林火灾并非由巨大的火花引起。必须有许多树木着火并蔓延开来。

Sure, every great story has a hero. Tippi Hedren helped Vietnamese women learn about manicures, and George Wright had the creative idea that started Will It Blend? But while these individuals provided the initial spark, they’re only one small part of the story. Describing why a small handful of cool or connected people (so-called influentials) are not as important to social epidemics as we might think, sociologist Duncan Watts makes a nice comparison to forest fires. Some forest fires are bigger than others, but no one would claim that the size of the fire depends on the exceptional nature of the initial spark. Big forest fires aren’t caused by big sparks. Lots of individual trees have to catch fire and carry the flames.

具有传染性的产品和理念就像森林大火一样，它们无法蔓延。如果没有成百上千的普通人传播产品或信息，这一切就不会发生。

Contagious products and ideas are like forest fires. They can’t happen without hundreds, if not thousands, of regular Joes and Janes passing the product or message along.

那么，为什么成千上万的人要传播这些产品和理念呢？

So why did thousands of people transmit these products and ideas?

这就引出了第三点：某些特征会让产品和理念更容易被人们谈论和分享。你或许会认为某些事物流行起来纯属偶然，某些产品和理念只是运气好。但这并非仅仅是运气，也并非什么神秘之事。同样的原则驱动着各种社会现象的传播。无论是鼓励人们节约用纸、观看纪录片、尝试某项服务，还是投票给某个候选人，都有其成功的秘诀。同样的六项原则，或者说STEPPS原则，推动着事物流行起来。

And that’s where we get to the third point: certain characteristics make products and ideas more likely to be talked about and shared. You might have thought it was just random why some things catch on, that certain products and ideas just got lucky. But it’s not just luck. And it’s not a mystery. The same key principles drive all sorts of social epidemics. Whether it’s about getting people to save paper, see a documentary, try a service, or vote for a candidate, there is a recipe for success. The same six principles, or STEPPS, drive things to catch on.

社交货币

Social Currency

我们分享那些能让我们看起来很棒的事情

We share things that make us look good

触发器

Triggers

脱口而出，话到嘴边却说不出口。

Top of mind, tip of tongue

情感

Emotion

当我们关心他人时，我们会分享

When we care, we share

民众

Public

为展示而建，为成长而建

Built to show, built to grow

实用价值

Practical Value

实用新闻

News you can use

故事

Stories

信息往往伪装成闲聊而传播。

Information travels under the guise of idle chatter

因此，如果我们想让某个产品或想法具有感染力，请考虑如何构建这些关键的 STEPP 原则。

So if we’re trying to make a product or idea contagious, think about how to build in these key STEPPS.

有些事情可能发生在产品或理念本身的设计中。售价一百美元的芝士牛排三明治就是为了提升其社交影响力而设计的。丽贝卡·布莱克的歌曲经常因为歌名而引发争议。苏珊·博伊尔的表演唤起了人们的强烈情感。Movember活动通过将原本私密的行为——留胡子——公开化，为男性癌症筹集了数百万美元。Ken Craig 的“每次都能清洁耳朵”视频，两分钟的内容极具实用价值。

Some of this can happen in the design of the product or idea itself. The hundred-dollar cheesesteak was engineered to have Social Currency. Rebecca Black’s song was frequently triggered because of its title. Susan Boyle’s performance evoked lots of Emotion. Movember raised millions for men’s cancer by taking a once private behavior and using moustaches to make it Public. Ken Craig’s “Clean Ears Everytime” video is two minutes of pure Practical Value.

但这些STEPPs也可以融入到产品或理念的宣传信息中。Blendtec的搅拌机一直功能强大，但通过以一种引人注目的方式展现其强大功能，“它能搅拌吗？”系列视频创造了社交货币，引发了热议。Kit Kat并没有改变产品本身，但通过将其与一种流行的饮品（咖啡）联系起来，该公司增加了触发点，让人们思考（并讨论）这款巧克力棒。人们分享Vanguard的“理财之道”系列视频，是因为它们提供了实用价值，但分享这些视频也提升了公司自身的口碑。人们分享Dove的“进化”视频，是因为它唤起了人们的情感，但通过将自身融入叙事中，Dove也从中受益。

But these STEPPS can also be built into messaging around a product or idea. Blendtec’s blenders had always been powerful, but by showing that power in a remarkable way, the Will It Blend? videos generated Social Currency and got people buzzing. Kit Kat didn’t change its product, but by linking it to a popular beverage (coffee), the company increased the number of Triggers to make people think (and talk) about the candy bar. People share Vanguard’s MoneyWhys because they provide Practical Value, but passing them along boosts word of mouth for the company itself. People shared Dove’s “Evolution” video because it evokes lots of Emotion, but by embedding itself in the narrative, Dove benefits from the chatter as well.

如果你想应用这个框架，这里有一个清单，你可以用它来查看你的产品或想法在六个不同的 STEPP 框架中表现如何。

If you want to apply this framework, here’s a checklist you can use to see how well your product or idea is doing on the six different STEPPS.

遵循这六个关键步骤，或者哪怕只遵循其中几个，你就可以利用社会影响力和口碑传播，让任何产品或想法流行起来。

Follow these six key STEPPS, or even just a few of them, and you can harness social influence and word of mouth to get any product or idea to catch on.

最后补充一点。STEPPS框架最棒的地方在于任何人都能使用它。它不需要巨额广告预算、营销天才，也不需要什么创意天赋。没错，我们之前提到的那些病毒式传播的视频和热门内容确实出自某些人之手，但并非所有人都名声显赫，或者在推特上拥有上万粉丝。他们运用了STEPPS框架中的一个或多个关键要素，这使得他们的产品和创意更具传播力。

One last note. The best part of the STEPPS framework is that anyone can use it. It doesn’t require a huge advertising budget, marketing genius, or some sort of creativity gene. Yes, the viral videos and contagious content we’ve talked about were created by particular individuals, but not all of them were famous or could boast ten thousand followers on Twitter. They relied on one or more of the six key STEPPS and this made their products and ideas more contagious.

社交货币

Social Currency

谈论你的产品或理念能让人觉得你很棒吗？你能挖掘出它内在的独特之处吗？你能利用游戏机制吗？你能让人们感觉自己是内部人士吗？

Does talking about your product or idea make people look good? Can you find the inner remarkability? Leverage game mechanics? Make people feel like insiders?

触发器

Triggers

考虑一下背景。哪些线索会让人们想到你的产品或想法？如何营造这种氛围，让人们更频繁地想到它？

Consider the context. What cues make people think about your product or idea? How can you grow the habitat and make it come to mind more often?

情感

Emotion

关注感受。谈论你的产品或想法能否激发情感？如何才能点燃这种情感？

Focus on feelings. Does talking about your product or idea generate emotion? How can you kindle the fire?

民众

Public

你的产品或理念是否能够自我宣传？人们能否看到其他人正在使用它？如果不能，你如何将私密信息公开？你能否创造一种即使在人们使用后仍然持续存在的行为痕迹？

Does your product or idea advertise itself? Can people see when others are using it? If not, how can you make the private public? Can you create behavioral residue that sticks around even after people use it?

实用价值

Practical Value

谈论你的产品或理念是否能帮助人们帮助他人？如何突出其巨大价值，将你的知识和专业技能提炼成他人乐于传播的实用信息？

Does talking about your product or idea help people help others? How can you highlight incredible value, packaging your knowledge and expertise into useful information others will want to disseminate?

故事

Stories

你的特洛伊木马是什么？你的产品或理念是否融入了人们想要分享的更广泛的故事中？这个故事不仅能迅速传播，而且还具有价值吗？

What is your Trojan Horse? Is your product or idea embedded in a broader narrative that people want to share? Is the story not only viral, but also valuable?

霍华德·韦恩需要一种方法来帮助一家新餐厅在竞争激烈的市场中脱颖而出，既要提高知名度，又要忠于巴克莱顶级餐厅的品牌形象。百元芝士牛排恰好做到了这一点。它不仅提供了一个引人注目（社交货币）、出人意料（情感）的故事（故事），还展现了这家牛排馆所提供的优质产品（实用价值）。费城芝士牛排的普及也让人们更容易想起它并把它推荐给朋友（触发因素）。百元芝士牛排引发了人们的热议，并助力巴克莱顶级餐厅取得了巨大的成功。

Howard Wein needed a way to help a new restaurant break through the clutter, a way to raise awareness while staying true to the Barclay Prime brand. The hundred-dollar cheesesteak did just that. It not only provided a remarkable (Social Currency), surprising (Emotion) narrative (Story) but also illustrated the type of quality product that the steakhouse offered (Practical Value). And the prevalence of cheesesteaks in Philadelphia offered ready reminders for people to pass it on (Triggers). The hundred-dollar cheesesteak got people talking and helped make Barclay Prime a rousing success.

乔治·赖特几乎没有营销预算。他需要一种方法来为一款大多数人通常不会谈论的产品——搅拌机——制造话题。通过思考是什么让他的产品引人注目，并将这些想法融入一个更宏大的故事中，他成功地获得了数亿次的观看量，并提升了销量。“它能搅拌吗？”系列短片精彩绝伦（情感冲击）且引人注目（社交价值）。但真正让这些视频脱颖而出的是，它们巧妙地将产品的优势（实用价值）融入到更宏大的故事中（故事性），从而有效地让人们开始讨论这款日常家用电器，并最终让Blendtec品牌走红。

George Wright had almost no marketing budget. He needed a way to generate buzz about a product most people wouldn’t ordinarily talk about: a blender. By thinking about what made his product compelling and wrapping that idea in a broader narrative, he was able to generate hundreds of millions of views and boost sales. The Will It Blend? clips are amazing (Emotion) and remarkable (Social Currency). But by making the product’s benefits (Practical Value) integral to a broader narrative (Stories), the videos provided a perfect Trojan horse to get people talking about an everyday household appliance and make Blendtec catch on.

他们都是普通人，拥有普通的产品和想法。但他们巧妙地运用了口碑营销的心理，最终使自己的产品和想法获得了成功。

Regular people with regular products and ideas. But by harnessing the psychology of word of mouth, they were able to make their products and ideas succeed.

本书通篇探讨了口碑传播和社会影响力运作机制的前沿科学。如果您遵循以下六个关键步骤（STEPPS），就能让任何产品或创意迅速传播开来。

Throughout the book we’ve discussed cutting-edge science about how word of mouth and social influence work. If you follow these six key STEPPS, you can make any product or idea contagious.

致谢

Acknowledgments

每当我提到我在写书时，人们常常会问是否有人帮我。虽然我没有合著者，但这个问题很难回答，因为如果没有无数人的帮助，这本书根本不可能完成。

Whenever I said I was writing a book, people often asked whether anyone was helping me. While I did not have a co-author, that question was tough to answer because this book would never have reached fruition without countless people’s help.

首先，我要感谢多年来与我合作的各位。感谢像Ezgi Akpinar、Eric Bradlow、Dave Balter和BzzAgent团队、Gráinne Fitzsimons、Raghu Iyengar、Ed Keller和Keller Fay Group的各位、Blake McShane、Katy Milkman、Eric Sc​​hwartz以及Morgan Ward这样的人，没有他们的帮助，我在书中讨论的论文是不可能完成的。像Rebecca Greenblatt、Diana Jiang、Lauren McDevitt、Geneva Long、Keri Taub和Jennifer Wu这样才华横溢的学生也为这些项目提供了支持。Malcolm Gladwell的著作精彩绝伦，引领我走上了这条道路。Anna Mastri激励我成为一名更优秀的作家，而Seth Godin、Stanley Lieberson、Everett Rogers、Emanuel Rosen、Thomas Schelling和Jonathan Weiner的著作则启发我继续从事这项研究。此外，我还要感谢像Glenn Moglen这样的人，是他带我走进了学术界。感谢我的研究；感谢艾米丽·普罗宁（Emily Pronin），她引领我走进了社会心理学的世界；感谢诺亚·马克（Noah Mark），他引领我走进了社会学的世界；感谢李·罗斯（Lee Ross）和伊塔玛·西蒙森（Itamar Simonson），他们告诉我永远要追求远大的理想。还要感谢我在沃顿商学院和斯坦福大学的所有同事，以及蒙哥马利·布莱尔高中和塔科马帕克中学的所有老师和员工，是他们教会了我以及成千上万其他幸运的孩子，让他们领略了数学和科学的奇妙之处。

First, I want to thank my various collaborators over the years. People like Ezgi Akpinar, Eric Bradlow, Dave Balter and the team at BzzAgent, Gráinne Fitzsimons, Raghu Iyengar, Ed Keller and the folks at Keller Fay Group, Blake McShane, Katy Milkman, Eric Schwartz, and Morgan Ward, without whom the papers I discussed in the book would not have been possible. Bright students like Rebecca Greenblatt, Diana Jiang, Lauren McDevitt, Geneva Long, Keri Taub, and Jennifer Wu helped support these projects. Malcolm Gladwell wrote the amazing book that sent me down this road. Anna Mastri pushed me to be a better writer, and books by Seth Godin, Stanley Lieberson, Everett Rogers, Emanuel Rosen, Thomas Schelling, and Jonathan Weiner inspired me to pursue this line of research. A debt of gratitude also goes out to people like Glenn Moglen, who introduced me to academic research; Emily Pronin, who introduced me to social psychology; Noah Mark, who introduced me to sociology; and Lee Ross and Itamar Simonson, who said to always shoot for big ideas. Thanks also to all my colleagues at Wharton and Stanford and all the teachers and staff at Montgomery Blair High School and Takoma Park Middle School who taught me, and thousands of other lucky kids, about the wonders of math and science.

其次，我要感谢所有促成这本书出版的人。丹·艾瑞里、丹·吉尔伯特和莎拉·莱勒帮助我真正理解了写作的意义。爱丽丝·拉普兰特润色了我的文笔。吉姆·莱文和他在莱文·格林伯格文学经纪公司的所有同事在整个过程中给予了我指导。乔纳森·卡普、鲍勃·本德、特雷西·盖斯特、理查德·罗勒、迈克尔·阿科迪诺以及西蒙与舒斯特出版社的其他团队成员帮助我将这些想法变成了一本真正的书。安东尼·卡法罗、科琳·乔拉克、肯·克雷格、本·菲什曼、丹尼斯·格雷迪、科琳·约翰内森、斯科特·麦克伊彻恩、吉姆·米汉、蒂姆·派珀、肯·西格尔、布莱恩·谢拜罗、霍华德·韦恩和乔治·赖特抽出时间与我分享了他们的故事。沃顿商学院的几位EMBA学员也慷慨地为我的初稿提供了反馈。宾夕法尼亚大学的午餐足球队让我从写作中得到了放松。玛丽亚·安娜则以她敏锐的眼光对修改稿进行了精雕细琢。我的哥哥弗雷德、丹尼以及整个布鲁诺家族不仅对草稿提出了反馈意见，还提醒我当初为什么要做这一切。

Second, I want to thank the people who made the book itself possible. Dan Ariely, Dan Gilbert, and Sarah Lehrer helped me understand what writing a book really meant. Alice LaPlante sharpened the writing. Jim Levine and all of his colleagues at Levine Greenberg Literary Agency were guiding lights throughout the process. Jonathan Karp, Bob Bender, Tracey Guest, Richard Rhorer, Michael Accordino, and the rest of the team at Simon & Schuster helped form these ideas into a real book. Anthony Cafaro, Colleen Chorak, Ken Craig, Ben Fischman, Denise Grady, Koreen Johannessen, Scott MacEachern, Jim Meehan, Tim Piper, Ken Segall, Brian Shebairo, Howard Wein, and George Wright took the time to share their stories with me. Various Wharton Executive EMBA students were nice enough to provide feedback on the draft. The UPenn lunchtime soccer crew provided a welcome break from writing. Maria Ana brought an eagle eye to revising. My brother, Fred, Danny, and the whole Bruno family not only gave feedback on the drafts but reminded me why I was doing all of this in the first place.

还有几个人值得特别感谢。首先是Chip，他不仅是我的顾问、导师和朋友，还教会了我写作和研究方面的大部分知识：我对你的感激之情无以言表。其次是Jordan，他一路陪伴我，既是一位细致周到的编辑，也是一位不知疲倦的支持者，根据需要灵活调整。第三，是……感谢我的父母，黛安·阿金和杰弗里·伯杰，不仅感谢他们阅读和支持这个项目，更感谢他们为这一切的实现奠定了基础。最后，感谢我的祖母，感谢她开启了这段旅程，并一路支持着我。

A few more people deserve special note. First, to Chip, who not only has been an advisor, mentor, and friend, but has taught me most of what I know about writing and research: I cannot thank you enough. Second, to Jordan for sticking through the process with me and being both a thoughtful editor and a tireless champion, depending on what was needed. Third, to my parents, Diane Arkin and Jeffrey Berger, not only for reading and supporting this project, but for laying the groundwork to make it all possible. And finally, to my grandmother. For kicking off this journey and supporting me along the way.

传染性：为什么事物会流行起来

Contagious: Why Things Catch On

作者：乔纳·伯格

By Jonah Berger

西蒙与舒斯特读者团体指南

Simon & Schuster Readers Group Guide

是什么让产品和理念流行起来？为什么有些故事比其他故事更容易传播？为什么有些谣言会迅速蔓延？是什么让事物“病毒式传播”？

What makes products and ideas catch on and become popular? Why do some stories get shared more than others? Why are some rumors infectious? What makes things “go viral”?

在《传染》一书中，乔纳·伯杰揭示了社交传播背后的科学奥秘。他解释了为什么我们会谈论和分享某些事物而非其他事物，以及为什么我们会传播某些信息。本书充满了引人入胜的故事和全面的研究，是任何希望推广产品或理念的人的必备工具。

In Contagious, Jonah Berger shares the secret science behind social transmission. Why we talk about and share some things rather than others. Why we pass things on. Filled with engaging stories and comprehensive research Contagious is an essential tool for anyone that wants to make their product or idea spread.

讨论题

QUESTIONS FOR DISCUSSION

1）思考并讨论你最近收到的转发邮件。它可能是一篇新闻文章、一段视频或一个故事。它符合STEPPS框架的哪些方面？对你最近观看的热门视频、尝试过的热门餐厅、观看的热门电影等进行同样的分析。框架中的哪些概念适用？

1) Consider and discuss the most recent email forward you received. It might have been a news article, video, or story. What aspects of the STEPPS framework did it adhere to? Do the same analysis for the last viral video you watched, hot restaurant you tried, hit movie you saw, etc. Which concepts in the framework apply?

2）在阅读本书之前，伯杰提到的哪些例子（例如，Blendtec、Dove的Evolution 、白色iPhone耳机）是你已经知道的？追溯一下你是如何接触到这些文化标志的。

2) Which examples that Berger mentioned (e.g., Blendtec, Dove’s Evolution, white iPhone earbuds) had you been aware of before the book? Trace how these cultural touchstones came to you.

3）运用游戏机制的视角，讨论并分析你所做的事情。是什么让这款游戏、俱乐部、网站、社区或活动如此吸引人？思考一下STEPPS模型中的每一个要素。是什么让你如此着迷？此外，为什么我们如此重视成就？你注意到自己在哪些方面受到了成就的激励？

3) Discuss and analyze something you do using the lens of game mechanics. What makes that game, club, website, community, or activity so engaging? Consider each of the STEPPS. What keeps you hooked? Also, why do we value achievement so much? Where is an area you’ve noticed yourself being motivated by it?

4）像搅拌机这样的普通事物真的能像非凡事物那样迅速地在公众意识中传播开来吗？即使考虑到STEPPS因素，耸人听闻或新颖性（内在的非凡性）是否仍然具有优势？

4) Can mundane things (like a blender) really diffuse through public consciousness as quickly as remarkable things can? Does sensationalism or novelty (inherent remarkability) carry an advantage, even when STEPPS are considered?

5）举例说明你如何发现自己会受到稀缺性的驱动？新型高科技产品（例如新款手机、电子产品等）在上市时往往供不应求。拥有这些产品是否具有社会价值？它们的频繁上市（市场供过于求）是否会降低它们的稀缺性？

5) What is an example where you’ve noticed yourself being motivated by scarcity? New high tech devices (new phones, gadgets, etc.) are frequently scarce when they are released. Does possession of these products carry social currency? Does their frequent release (a veritable flooding of the market) make them less scarce?

6）你愿意冒着电话排队的危险去参加“请勿告知”活动吗？它的隐蔽性吸引你的是什么？又有没有什么因素让你对抢座位感到反感？

6) Would you brave the phone lines to enter Please Don't Tell? What about its seclusion appeals to you? Is there also something that turns you off to the fight for a seat?

7）比较一下持续口碑传播和即时口碑传播。哪一种更有效？

7) Compare an instance of ongoing and immediate word of mouth. Is one more powerful than the other?

8）不去深思熟虑，你现在最先想到的是什么？你能找出生活中哪些事情会触发你的联想吗？例如，每当我看到或听到__________，我就会想到____________。

8) Without thinking too hard, what is currently top of mind for you? Can you identify triggers is your own life that bring things to mind? For example, whenever I see or hear __________ I think about ____________.

9）像Mars巧克力棒和Rebecca Black的“星期五”活动一样，讨论那些具有“天然”触发因素的产品或营销活动。预先存在的“栖息地”是否会增加分享的可能性？购买的可能性？

9) Like the Mars Bar and Rebecca Black's Friday, discuss products or campaigns that have "natural" triggers. Does having a pre-existing "habitat" increase likelihood of sharing? Of purchase?

10）讨论高唤醒情绪和低唤醒情绪。你在生活中观察到过哪些例子？“满足感”（很多人在日常生活中追求的）几乎不会引起太多的情绪唤醒，这是否很奇怪？你有没有分享过悲伤的事情？

10) Discuss high-arousal versus low-arousal emotions. What are some examples of each that you’ve noticed in your own lives? Is it strange that "contentment" (which so many people strive for in every day life) elicits little arousal? Have you ever shared something sad?

11）在当今信息驱动的数字时代，隐私至关重要。这与“将私人信息公开”的理念有何关联？（想想Movember、Hotmail和Facebook一代。）我们何时希望自己的选择和观点公开，何时又希望它们保持私密？开放信息和封闭信息之间的界限在哪里？

11) Privacy is a paramount concern in today's information-driven digital era. How does this fit with the idea of making the private public? (Consider Movember, Hotmail, and the Facebook generation.). When do we want our choices and opinions to be public versus private? Where are the lines drawn between open and closed information?

12）观看蒂姆·派珀/多芬的《进化》视频。讨论特洛伊木马的概念，以及情感是如何被编码到思想叙事中的。

12) Watch Tim Piper/Dove’s Evolution video. Discuss the idea of Trojan Horses and how emotion is coded into the narrative of ideas.

13）你认为STEPPS模型中哪个方面最有效？比较Berger提出的社交货币、触发因素、情感、公共性、实用价值和故事等不同例子。这些特征中哪个对促进分享最为重要？是否存在某种特定的组合，使得某种事物比其他事物更具吸引力？

13) Which aspect of STEPPS do you think is most affective? Compare Berger’s various examples of Social Currency, Triggers, Emotions, Public, Practical Value, and Stories. Is one of these characteristics most important for driving sharing? Is there a specific combination that makes one thing more appealing than another?

扩大你的读书俱乐部

EXPAND YOUR BOOK CLUB

1. 进行一项实验，持续一周监测纽约时报网站“邮件转发量最高”版块的内容。你是否开始注意到分享内容的规律？考虑文章引发的情绪、实用价值以及文章的主题。

1. Perform an experiment and monitor the “most emailed” section of NYTimes.com for a week. Do you begin to notice patterns in what is shared? Consider arousal emotions, practical value, and the very subject matter of the articles.

2. 记录传播日志，并记下产品、理念、活动和公司如何利用人类行为分享的原则来使其产品“流行”和“传播”。你的生活中有多少内容遵循了STEPPS的基本原则？

2. Keep a transmission journal and note the ways in which products, ideas, campaigns, and companies use the tenets of human behavioral sharing to make their wares “stick” and “spread.” How much of your life is subject to the basic STEPPS?

3. 运用 STEPPS 原则，创作你自己的病毒式传播视频。制作一个能够兼顾所有这些分享因素的视频会面临哪些挑战？追踪你的视频的成功情况以及人们的反应。你是否已经掌握了病毒式传播内容的秘诀？

3. Create your own viral video with the STEPPS in mind. What are the challenges of making something that addresses all of these sharing factors? Track the success of your video and how people react. Have you cracked the code to viral content?

与乔纳·伯格的对话

A CONVERSATION WITH JONAH BERGER

1. 哈佛大学的神经科学研究表明，分享带来的愉悦感与食物和其他高愉悦感奖励类似，这非常有趣。考虑到这一点，你如何解释所谓的“自我一代”——在推特和脸书上，人们分享日常生活中最细微的细节？所有世代都以“自我”为中心吗？现代社会是否有什么不同之处？

1. The Harvard neuro study revealing that sharing is rewarding in the same way as food and other high-pleasure reward was incredibly interesting. Taking this into consideration, how do you explain what is referred to as the “me” generation of Twitter and Facebook, in which individuals share the most minute aspects of their everyday lives. Are all generations focused on “me?” Is there something different going on in modernity?

人们一直以来都关注自身，而社交媒体让这一点更加显而易见，因为它会记录我们的行为：我们说过的话、分享过的内容以及我们“点赞”的内容。但研究表明，这些沟通方式也可能使我们更加以自我为中心。计算机媒介的沟通，以及与一大群人（而非单个人）交流，可能会让我们更加关注自身，而忽略他人的需求和愿望。

People have always thought about and cared about themselves, but social media makes this easier to see because it creates a written record of our actions. What we said, what we shared, and what we “like.” But research suggests that these methods of communication may also contribute to making us “me” focused. Computer mediated communication, and talking to large groups (rather than a single other), may focus us more on ourselves and less on the wants and needs of others.

2. 你最近分享过什么？最近别人跟你分享过什么？

2. What’s the most recent thing you shared? And what was most recently shared with you?

哇，问得好。我最近分享了一篇《纽约时报》的文章，里面引用了一句与我们正在进行的研究项目相关的话。我还收到了一份餐厅推荐，说那里的亚洲炸鸡很好吃。之前我在《金融时报》一篇关于品牌延伸的文章里也谈到过类似的话题，结果有人读了那篇文章后给我发了条信息，想证明我是错的！

Wow. Good question. One thing I recently shared was a New York Times article that has a quote related to a research project we’re working on. One thing I just received was a restaurant recommendation for good Asian fried chicken. I had talked about something related in a Financial Times article about brand extensions, and someone who read the article sent me a note to prove me wrong!

3. 你认为你已经掌握了高效产品广告的秘诀吗？你会尝试一下吗？

3. Do you think you’ve cracked the code of efficient product advertising? Would you ever try your hand at it?

我们能让广告更有效、更火爆吗？当然可以。我们是否已经“破解了密码”？还有很多东西需要学习。我非常乐于尝试。我经常帮助企业运用STEPPS框架来改进他们的产品和理念，这总是让我乐在其中。

Can we make ads more effective and viral? Yes. Have we “cracked the code”? There is always more to learn. Definitely like trying my hand at it. I often help companies use the STEPPS framework to improve their products and ideas and it is always lots of fun.

4. 同样地，你的学生中是否有人后来成为成功的广告商/病毒视频制作者/思想传播者？

4. Along those same lines, have any of your students gone on to become successful advertisers/viral video makers/idea-spreaders?

当然。我在课堂上会布置一项练习，让学生们运用STEPPS框架来尝试制作爆款视频。这很难，但有些人做得非常出色！

Definitely. I teach an exercise in my class where students use the STEPPS framework to try and create a viral video. It’s tough but some people do amazingly well!

5. 讨论一下你认为如今的分享方式与30年前的分享方式有何不同。我们的数字文化有哪些变化？分享是否在某些方面有所减少？

5. Discuss how you think sharing today compares to sharing 30 years ago. What about our digital culture has made things different? Has sharing decreased in any way?

如今的分享方式在某些方面确实有所不同。我们与亲朋好友面对面交流的机会减少了，因此更多地通过电话或网络沟通。研究表明，这会降低社交互动带来的一些益处。温暖的人际交往可以减轻压力，但像发短信这样的方式却没有同样的效果。我们分享得更少了吗？恐怕未必，但我们分享的方式确实发生了变化。

Sharing today is certainly different in some ways. There is less face-to-face interaction with our friends and family, so we talk more over the phone or through the web. Research shows that this decreases some of the benefits of social interaction. Warm interpersonal contact reduces stress, but things like texting don’t have the same effect. Do we share less? Doubtful, but we do share differently.

6. 目前的广告似乎越来越“脱离品牌”，因为公司制作一些不合逻辑、荒诞不经的广告来博取笑声或利用“讽刺”效应。他们是否没有遵循STEPPS原则？与产品无关的滑稽行为是否真的具有某种特殊意义？（例如食品店里的熊猫广告）。

6. The nature of current commercials seems more and more “off-brand,” as companies create non-sequitur and nonsensical ads to elicit a laugh or capitalize on “irony.” Are they failing to follow the STEPPS? Is there remarkability to silliness un-related to product? (Unlike the Panda in the food store).

有趣的广告很棒，作为消费者，我也很喜欢看。但如果广告的目标不仅仅是逗人发笑，而是要促成购买，那么有效的病毒式传播就至关重要了。人们会分享有趣或讽刺的广告，但最终，如果消费者根本不知道广告的目的是什么，这对公司来说毫无益处。

Funny ads are great. And as a consumer, I love to watch them. But if the goal is not just to make people laugh, but to get them to buy something, then valuable virality becomes vital. People will share funny or ironic ads, but at the end of the day it doesn’t help the company if the consumer has no idea what the ad was for.

7. 你认为大多数 STEPPS 行为是在无意识层面发生的，还是你认为人们在创造事物时会考虑到这些基本的人类行为？

7. Do you think most STEPPS happen at the unconscious level, or do you believe people create things with these fundamental human behaviors in mind?

人们对STEPPs中的某些要素比其他要素更了解。实用价值？我们每天都能感受到。社交价值？我们经常在别人身上看到它（即使很难在自己身上看到）。但我们不太清楚触发因素或公共因素如何影响我们的行为。

People are more aware of some of the STEPPS than others. Practical Value? We see that every day. Social Currency? We see it in others all the time (even if it’s hard to see in ourselves). But we are less aware of how Triggers or Public affects our behavior.

8. 过度饱和是否也有其益处？在我们这个瞬息万变的文化中，一种好的病毒式传播方式会不会最终失效？

8. Is there something to be said for over-saturation? Can a good method of viral sharing exhaust itself in our fast-as-lightning culture?

心理学和营销策略之间存在着关键区别。我们可能会对某种营销策略（例如弹出式广告或某种特定风格的广告）感到厌倦，但当初驱使我们喜欢它的潜在心理因素依然存在。如果每家公司都让自己的产品“稀缺”，消费者会逐渐意识到这一点，但这是否意味着我们会彻底放弃对稀缺性的重视呢？恐怕不会。

There is a key difference between psychology and marketing tactics. We may get over-saturated with a particular tactic (e.g., pop-up ads or a certain style of ad) but the underlying psychology that drove us to like it still remains. If every company makes their product “scarce” consumers will start to catch on, but does that mean we’ll stop valuing scarcity altogether? Probably not.

9. STEPPS 的哪个要素比其他要素更重要？

9. Is one element of STEPPS more vital than the others?

STEPPS营销策略中没有哪一项是最重要的，但某些策略在特定情况下更容易应用。如果你销售的是实体产品，那么运用“公众”策略就更容易；如果你销售的是与儿童或动物相关的产品，那么运用“情感”策略就更容易。但关键不在于仅仅使用容易的策略，尝试运用那些更复杂的策略才能真正提升其效果。

No one of the STEPPS is most important, but certain ones are definitely easier to apply in certain situations. It’s easier to leverage Public if you have a physical product. It’s easier to use Emotion if you sell something related to children or animals. But the key is not just using the easy STEPPS. Trying to incorporate the more difficult ones will really boost their impact.

10. 你吃过巴克莱银行的100美元的芝士牛排吗？

10. Have you ever eaten the $100 cheesesteak at Barclay’s?

是的，我强烈推荐。

Yes. I highly recommend it.

© 黛博拉·费恩戈尔德

© DEBORAH FEINGOLD

乔纳·伯杰是宾夕法尼亚大学沃顿商学院的詹姆斯·G·坎贝尔市场营销学助理教授。他已在顶级学术期刊上发表了数十篇文章，其研究成果也曾被《纽约时报》、《华尔街日报》、《华盛顿邮报》、《科学》、《哈佛商业评论》、《连线》、《商业周刊》和《快公司》等媒体广泛报道。他的研究成果还曾被《纽约时报杂志》年度“年度思想”特刊收录。伯杰教授因其学术成就和教学贡献而荣获多项奖项，其中包括被沃顿商学院授予“铁杆教授”称号。他现居宾夕法尼亚州费城。

JONAH BERGER is the James G. Campbell Assistant Professor of Marketing at The Wharton School at the University of Pennsylvania. He has published dozens of articles in top-tier academic journals, and popular accounts of his work have appeared in places like The New York Times, The Wall Street Journal, The Washington Post, Science, Harvard Business Review, Wired, BusinessWeek, and Fast Company. His research has also been featured in The New York Times Magazine’s annual “Year in Ideas” issue. Berger has been recognized with awards for both scholarship and teaching, including being named Wharton’s “Iron Prof.” He lives in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
